feedbackModel.decisionSignal: In the worst case, the validator reaches all n values before it can return.
feedbackModel.details: The validator performs O(1) work for each inspected value. If the first invalid value is last, or every value is valid, it performs n inspections, so n × O(1) = O(n). Returning early changes the best case to O(1), but it does not change this worst-case bound.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Worst-case analysis follows the execution that reaches every value, not the early return that happens only on favorable input.
feedbackModel.nextAction: For an early-exit routine, identify the input that delays the exit before naming its worst-case cost.

distractorExplanations.wrong_constant: This treats an available early return as guaranteed. The invalid value can be last or absent, so one execution still inspects all n values.
distractorExplanations.wrong_quadratic: This assumes pairwise work that the routine never performs. Each value is checked once against the validator rule, rather than against every other value.

items.prompt: A validator receives an array of n values. It checks one value at a time and returns immediately when it finds an invalid value. What is its worst-case time complexity?
items.title: Derive worst-case single scan
items.instruction: Count the most inspections this validator can perform before it returns.
items.answerFeedback: The worst case performs one constant-time validation for every one of the n values.

options.text: O(n), because an invalid value can be last or every value can be valid, requiring n checks.
O(1), because the routine can return as soon as it finds an invalid value.
O(n^2), because validation compares many values before returning.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The routine compares each unordered pair once: 1 + 2 + ... + (n - 1) comparisons.
feedbackModel.details: For the first value there are n - 1 later values to compare; for the next there are n - 2, and so on. That total is n(n - 1) / 2, which grows as O(n^2), with O(1) work per comparison. A shrinking inner range is not repeated halving: its total is still a triangular number here.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Count all pair comparisons, not just outer-loop iterations or the fact that the inner range shrinks.
feedbackModel.nextAction: When a bound shrinks, write the total number of inner iterations before assigning a growth class.

distractorExplanations.wrong_outer_only: This counts the n outer positions but omits the comparisons made for each position. The omitted inner work sums to about n^2 / 2 comparisons.
distractorExplanations.wrong_logarithmic: This mistakes a linearly shrinking range for repeated halving. The inner counts are n - 1, n - 2, and so on, not n/2, n/4, and so on.

items.prompt: For each index i in an array of n values, a routine compares value i with every later value j. What is the routine's worst-case time complexity?
items.title: Derive pair-enumeration cost
items.instruction: Add the comparisons made across all outer positions.
items.answerFeedback: The decreasing inner ranges add to about n^2 / 2 comparisons, so the total is quadratic.

options.text: O(n^2), because the pair count is (n - 1) + (n - 2) + ... + 1.
O(n), because the outer position advances through the array only once.
O(n log n), because the inner range becomes shorter after each outer position.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Every unsuccessful comparison leaves at most half of the candidate range.
feedbackModel.details: After one comparison at most n / 2 candidates remain, then n / 4, then n / 8. After k comparisons the remaining range is about n / 2^k; reaching one candidate requires k = O(log n). Sortedness makes the discard rule valid, but the repeated constant-factor reduction produces the logarithm.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The logarithm comes from halving the candidate range at every step, not from merely searching an array.
feedbackModel.nextAction: For a range-based routine, write how much of the candidate set remains after each unsuccessful step.

distractorExplanations.wrong_linear: This treats every array search as a left-to-right scan. Here one comparison eliminates roughly half the candidates instead of examining the next single candidate.
distractorExplanations.wrong_constant: This counts only the first middle comparison. A missing target can require several halvings, and that number grows with n.

items.prompt: A binary search runs on a sorted array of n values. Each comparison checks the middle value and then retains at most half of the remaining candidate range. What is its worst-case time complexity?
items.title: Derive repeated-halving cost
items.instruction: Track the size of the candidate range after each unsuccessful comparison.
items.answerFeedback: Each comparison keeps at most half the candidates, so only logarithmically many comparisons are needed.

options.text: O(log n), because the candidate range is halved after each unsuccessful comparison.
O(n), because an array can contain n values that might need to be considered.
O(1), because the routine checks the middle value first.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Binary search is logarithmic only when each comparison justifies discarding a constant fraction of candidates.
feedbackModel.details: On sorted input, comparing the middle value with the target proves that one half cannot contain the target. The next range is therefore at most half as large, giving n, n/2, n/4, and eventually O(log n) comparisons. Names such as left and right do not change the work, and sortedness alone is only a precondition until the algorithm uses it to eliminate a half.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Explain binary search from the discard rule: sorted order permits a middle comparison to remove half the remaining candidates.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Separate an algorithm's enabling precondition from the operation that actually reduces its remaining work.

distractorExplanations.wrong_names: This uses implementation labels as evidence. Renaming the bounds leaves the same number of comparisons, so the names cannot determine complexity.
distractorExplanations.wrong_sorted_only: This confuses a precondition with the mechanism. Sortedness is useful because it validates the half-discard inference; without that repeated discard, sorted data alone does not force logarithmic work.

items.prompt: A binary-search implementation uses bounds named left and right on a sorted array of n values. Which explanation correctly justifies its O(log n) worst-case time?
items.title: Justify the binary-search bound
items.instruction: Choose the explanation based on the work eliminated by each comparison.
items.answerFeedback: The cost is logarithmic because each valid comparison eliminates about half the candidates.

options.text: Each middle comparison uses sorted order to discard about half the remaining candidate range.
The bound is logarithmic because the variables are named left and right.
The bound is logarithmic simply because the input is sorted, even if no range is discarded.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Merge sort does O(n) merging work at each of O(log n) levels of splitting.
feedbackModel.details: Merge sort divides n values until singleton lists, creating O(log n) levels. At each level, merging processes all n values once, so the total is O(log n) levels × O(n) work per level = O(n log n) in the worst case. This conclusion is specific to merge sort; a sort's name or the fact that it compares values does not by itself establish one universal bound.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Derive merge sort from its levels and per-level merge work rather than assuming that sorting is either a scan or all-pairs comparison.
feedbackModel.nextAction: For divide-and-conquer work, count the levels and the work completed across one level.

distractorExplanations.wrong_linear: This counts only one merge pass over n values. Merge sort repeats that total-size merge work across logarithmically many split levels.
distractorExplanations.wrong_quadratic: This assumes every pair must be compared. Merge sort merges ordered sublists and processes each value once per level rather than enumerating all pairs.

items.prompt: Merge sort orders n arbitrary values. Under its standard worst-case comparison model, what is its time complexity?
items.title: Derive merge-sort cost
items.instruction: Relate merge work across one level to the number of split levels.
items.answerFeedback: Merge sort processes n values across each of logarithmically many levels, giving O(n log n).

options.text: O(n log n), because each of O(log n) levels merges a total of n values.
O(n), because a merge pass touches every value once.
O(n^2), because sorting must compare every pair of values.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A single scan visits n values; enumerating every unordered pair performs about n^2 / 2 comparisons.
feedbackModel.details: Plan A does one O(1) operation for each of n values, so it is O(n). Plan B compares each value with every later value, for n(n - 1) / 2 pair comparisons, so it is O(n^2). The number of loop keywords or local variables is not the growth mechanism; total executed operations are.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Compare plans by the total work their operation patterns execute, not by their surface syntax.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Translate a plan into the number of visits or pairs it can execute before comparing its Big-O cost.

distractorExplanations.wrong_loop_count: This treats the presence of loops as the whole analysis. One loop can run n times, while a nested independent comparison can create roughly n^2 / 2 total operations.
distractorExplanations.wrong_memory: This substitutes a memory heuristic for a time derivation. Variable count does not tell us how many scan visits or pair comparisons execute.

items.prompt: Plan A performs one constant-time check for each of n values. Plan B compares every unordered pair of those n values once. Which time comparison is correct?
items.title: Contrast scan and pair work
items.instruction: Compare the total number of constant-time operations each plan can perform.
items.answerFeedback: One visit per value is linear; one comparison per unordered pair is quadratic.

options.text: Plan A is O(n), while Plan B is O(n^2) because its number of pair comparisons grows quadratically.
The plans have the same growth rate because both can be written with loops.
The plan with fewer local variables is always the faster plan.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: n can be 100000, and a candidate plan compares every pair of values. What should you decide first?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: At this input size, pair enumeration is too expensive before implementation details matter.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice using the input limit before comparing code simplicity.

distractorExplanations.wrong_short_code: Short code does not make O(n^2) viable when n can be 100000.
distractorExplanations.wrong_values: The value range may matter for some strategies, but the first signal here is the number of pair checks.

items.prompt: Choose the first strategy decision.
items.title: Reject pair enumeration at 100000
items.constraintSignal: n can be 100000, and a candidate plan compares every pair of values.
items.reasonSignal: Reject the pair-enumeration plan because O(n^2) is too large for n near 100000.
items.instruction: n can be 100000, and a candidate plan compares every pair of values. What should you decide first?
items.answerFeedback: O(n^2) pair enumeration is not viable for n near 100000.

options.text: Reject the pair-enumeration plan because O(n^2) is too large for n near 100000.
Prefer the pair-enumeration plan because it is usually the shortest code.
Ignore n first and focus on whether the input values are positive or negative.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A teammate says, "Let's write the double loop first, then check if it passes." n can be 100000. What is the reasoning mistake?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The constraint should rule out the growth class before implementation starts.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice using constraints as the first filter for candidate strategies.

distractorExplanations.wrong_style: The main issue is not style; it is choosing an infeasible growth class.
distractorExplanations.wrong_memory: The prompt gives a time-scale problem, not a memory-limit problem.

items.prompt: Choose the reasoning mistake.
items.title: Catch implementation-first constraint use
items.instruction: A teammate says, "Let's write the double loop first, then check if it passes." n can be 100000. What is the reasoning mistake?
items.answerFeedback: The mistake is implementation-first reasoning; the input limit already rejects the double loop.

options.text: They are treating the constraint as a later validation step instead of using it to reject O(n^2) first.
They are mainly choosing a style that will be harder to read.
They are mainly ignoring that the double loop always uses O(n) extra memory.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Plan A compares every pair. Plan B scans once and keeps lookup state. n can be 100000. Which comparison should drive the choice?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The input limit makes growth rate decisive: O(n) with state is viable where O(n^2) pair checks are not.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice comparing candidate strategies by growth under the given constraint.

distractorExplanations.wrong_memory: Lower memory is not enough if the time growth is infeasible.
distractorExplanations.wrong_lines: Code length is not the deciding signal when the constraint rules out one growth class.

items.prompt: Choose the decisive comparison.
items.title: Choose viable growth under large n
items.instruction: Plan A compares every pair. Plan B scans once and keeps lookup state. n can be 100000. Which comparison should drive the choice?
items.answerFeedback: For n = 100000, linear time with lookup state is the viable scaling direction; quadratic pair checks are not.

options.text: Plan B trades memory for O(n) time, while Plan A's O(n^2) pair checks do not fit the input limit.
Plan A is better because it avoids lookup memory, regardless of the input limit.
Choose whichever plan has fewer lines of code before estimating growth.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: n is at most 40, and a simple plan checks every pair once. What does the constraint suggest?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Small input limits can make brute force acceptable; constraints do not always reject nested loops.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice using the specific limit instead of applying a blanket rule against brute force.

distractorExplanations.wrong_always_reject: O(n^2) is not automatically unacceptable; n = 40 is a small bound.
distractorExplanations.wrong_ignore_limit: The limit is the key signal for deciding whether this brute force is acceptable.

items.prompt: Choose the constraint-based interpretation.
items.title: Allow brute force for tiny n

items.instruction: n is at most 40, and a simple plan checks every pair once. What does the constraint suggest?
items.answerFeedback: With n capped at 40, O(n^2) pair enumeration may be acceptable.

options.text: The small bound means a pair-enumeration plan may be acceptable.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The same pair-checking approach is considered for two tasks: one has n <= 30, the other has n <= 200000. What should change?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The strategy decision changes with the input limit; the same O(n^2) plan can be acceptable for tiny n and infeasible for large n.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice making strategy decisions conditional on the actual constraint.

distractorExplanations.wrong_same: The same algorithmic idea does not have the same viability under very different bounds.
distractorExplanations.wrong_syntax: The syntax of the loop is not the deciding factor; the bound is.

items.prompt: Choose the constraint-aware comparison.
items.title: Compare brute force across bounds
items.instruction: The same pair-checking approach is considered for two tasks: one has n <= 30, the other has n <= 200000. What should change?
items.answerFeedback: The pair-checking plan may be acceptable at n <= 30 but should be rejected at n <= 200000.

options.text: The viability changes: O(n^2) may fit n <= 30 but not n <= 200000.
The viability is the same because both tasks compare pairs.
The decision should depend mainly on whether the loops are written compactly.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: n can be 200000. Plan A sorts once and scans. Plan B checks every pair without sorting. Which strategy signal matters first?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: O(n log n) is usually a viable candidate at this scale; O(n^2) pair enumeration is not.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice comparing O(n log n) and O(n^2) under large input constraints.

distractorExplanations.wrong_no_sort: Avoiding sorting is not automatically better if the alternative is quadratic.
distractorExplanations.wrong_same: Both plans inspect input values, but their growth rates are not the same.

items.prompt: Choose the strategy signal.
items.title: Prefer sort-scan over pair checks at scale
items.instruction: n can be 200000. Plan A sorts once and scans. Plan B checks every pair without sorting. Which strategy signal matters first?
items.answerFeedback: Sorting once and scanning is O(n log n), while pair enumeration is O(n^2).

options.text: Plan A has O(n log n) growth, while Plan B has O(n^2) growth that does not fit the large n.
Plan B is better because it avoids the cost of sorting.
Both plans scale the same because both compare values.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A task has n <= 100000 and asks for a repeated membership decision. Which candidate should survive the first constraint check?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A one-pass lookup plan is a better scaling candidate than scanning previous values for each item.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice matching repeated membership to a lookup plan under a large n constraint.

distractorExplanations.wrong_previous_scan: Scanning previous values for each item becomes quadratic.
distractorExplanations.wrong_pair: Pair enumeration solves too broad a problem and violates the input-size constraint.

items.prompt: Choose the viable candidate.
items.title: Keep lookup plan under large n
items.constraintSignal: n <= 100000 and the repeated operation is membership.
items.reasonSignal: A one-pass scan with lookup state avoids repeated scans under the large input limit.
items.instruction: A task has n <= 100000 and asks for a repeated membership decision. Which candidate should survive the first constraint check?
items.answerFeedback: For repeated membership at large n, one pass with lookup state is the viable candidate.

options.text: A one-pass scan with lookup state, because it avoids repeated scans.
For each item, scan all previous items to avoid using memory.
Compare all pairs because membership is about relationships between values.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A brute force plan would do about n^2 checks. n can be 100000. Which rough scale explains the rejection?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The constraint implies an enormous operation count; the exact constant is less important than the quadratic growth.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice using rough operation scale to reject infeasible growth.

distractorExplanations.wrong_exact: You do not need an exact runtime; the rough n^2 scale is enough.
distractorExplanations.wrong_constant: Constant factors cannot usually rescue 100000^2 pair checks.

items.prompt: Choose the rough-scale reasoning.
items.title: Estimate rough quadratic scale
items.instruction: A brute force plan would do about n^2 checks. n can be 100000. Which rough scale explains the rejection?
items.answerFeedback: 100000^2 is about 10^10 checks, which is the rough scale that rejects the plan.

options.text: Around 10^10 checks, which is too large for a normal candidate plan.
The plan cannot be judged until exact machine speed is known.
The plan is probably fine because nested loops can have small constants.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A solution has a best case that often returns after a few checks, but its worst case compares every pair. n can be 100000. Which signal should drive acceptance?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The input constraint must be checked against worst-case growth unless the problem guarantees early exit.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice distinguishing typical early exits from guaranteed bounds.

distractorExplanations.wrong_best: A common best case does not make the worst-case constraint disappear.
distractorExplanations.wrong_examples: Passing sample inputs does not prove the worst-case growth is viable.

items.prompt: Choose the acceptance signal.
items.title: Use worst-case for constraint fit
items.instruction: A solution has a best case that often returns after a few checks, but its worst case compares every pair. n can be 100000. Which signal should drive acceptance?
items.answerFeedback: The worst-case O(n^2) behavior must fit the constraint, unless early exit is guaranteed by the problem.

options.text: The worst-case pair enumeration, because early exit is not guaranteed.
The common best case, because many inputs return quickly.
The sample cases, because passing them means the complexity is acceptable.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A plan scans once for each possible threshold value from 1 to 100. n can be 100000. What constraint signal makes this different from pair enumeration?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A fixed small multiplier is still linear in n, unlike n nested against n.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice distinguishing fixed repeated work from input-sized nested work.

distractorExplanations.wrong_nested: Not every repeated scan is O(n^2); the outer count is fixed at 100.
distractorExplanations.wrong_ignore: You still need to estimate the repeated work, but the fixed bound changes the result.

items.prompt: Choose the correct constraint signal.
items.title: Accept fixed repeated scans
items.instruction: A plan scans once for each possible threshold value from 1 to 100. n can be 100000. What constraint signal makes this different from pair enumeration?
items.answerFeedback: 100 scans is O(100n), which simplifies to O(n); this is not pair enumeration.

options.text: The repeated scan count is fixed at 100, so the plan is still linear in n.
Any repeated scan should be rejected as O(n^2).
The input limit means repeated work does not need to be estimated.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: n can be 100000. A candidate has an outer loop over n items and an inner loop that always runs at most 5 checks. What should the constraint check conclude?

distractorExplanations.wrong_quadratic: Nested syntax alone does not imply O(n^2); the inner bound is fixed.
distractorExplanations.wrong_unknown: The prompt gives enough information: the inner loop is capped at 5.

items.title: Do not reject fixed inner loop

items.answerFeedback: The plan is O(5n), which is O(n), so this specific nested loop is not rejected as quadratic.

options.text: It is still O(n), because the inner work is capped by a constant.
It must be O(n^2), because there is a loop inside a loop.
The complexity cannot be estimated because there are two loops.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: n can be 100000 and q can be 100000. A candidate scans all n items for each query. What should the constraint check notice?

distractorExplanations.wrong_single_query: The plan is not just one scan; it repeats for q queries.
distractorExplanations.wrong_space: The issue is repeated time cost, not necessarily extra space.

items.instruction: n can be 100000 and q can be 100000. A candidate scans all n items for each query. What should the constraint check notice?
items.answerFeedback: Scanning n items for q queries is O(nq), which is not viable when both can be 100000.

options.text: The plan does O(nq) work, so it fails when both n and q are large.
The plan is O(n), because each individual query scans the list once.
The plan is acceptable because it can answer queries without extra memory.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A plan uses O(n log n) sorting for n = 100000. Another uses O(n^2) pair checks. Which one survives the first constraint screen?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The constraint screen does not require proving final correctness; it filters out the infeasible growth class.

distractorExplanations.wrong_quadratic: O(n^2) is the plan the constraint screen should reject.
distractorExplanations.wrong_correctness: Correctness still matters later, but this step asks which growth class survives.

options.text: The O(n log n) sorting-based candidate survives the first growth check.
The O(n^2) pair-check candidate survives because it avoids sorting.
