items.prompt: Choose the correct complexity reasoning.
items.title: Cost string comparator sort
items.instruction: A list of n strings is sorted, and comparing two strings can inspect up to m characters. What hidden cost should be included?
items.answerFeedback: Include comparator cost when sorting values whose comparison is not constant-time.

options.text: The sort does O(n log n) comparisons, and each comparison can cost O(m), so the character cost can make it O(n log n * m).
Only O(n log n), because string comparison is always constant.
Only O(m), because comparing strings is the only operation.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A fixed array receives many range-sum queries. What preprocessing signal should you recognize?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: When many queries reuse the same array, prefix sums can move work from each query into one preparation pass.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice separating preprocessing cost from per-query cost.

distractorExplanations.wrong_recompute: Recomputing each range ignores that many queries reuse the same fixed array.
distractorExplanations.wrong_sort: Sorting changes order and does not directly answer original-index range sums.

items.prompt: Choose the preprocessing signal.
items.title: Recognize prefix preprocessing
items.instruction: A fixed array receives many range-sum queries. What preprocessing signal should you recognize?
items.answerFeedback: A prefix-sum array preprocesses the fixed input once and makes each range query constant time.

options.text: Build prefix sums once, then answer each range sum from two stored totals.
Scan the requested range from scratch for every query because each query is separate.
Sort the array first because sorted data is always easier to query.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: After prefix sums are built, a range-sum query asks for sum(left..right). What per-query cost should you expect?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A prefix-sum query uses two stored totals and constant arithmetic.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice distinguishing build cost from query cost.

distractorExplanations.wrong_range_length: Range length matters for recomputation, not for a prefix-sum lookup.
distractorExplanations.wrong_build_again: The prefix array is already built; do not rebuild it per query.

items.prompt: Choose the per-query cost.
items.title: Cost one prefix query
items.instruction: After prefix sums are built, a range-sum query asks for sum(left..right). What per-query cost should you expect?
items.answerFeedback: Each query is O(1) after preprocessing because it reads two prefix totals.

options.text: O(1), because the query reads two prefix totals and subtracts them.
O(right - left), because every queried value must still be scanned.
O(n), because the prefix array must be rebuilt for every query.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: You build prefix sums for n values, then answer q range-sum queries. What total time signal should you name?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The full cost combines one O(n) build with q constant-time queries.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice writing total cost across preprocessing and query phases.

distractorExplanations.wrong_n_only: O(n) describes only the build, not the q query phase.
distractorExplanations.wrong_nq: O(nq) describes scanning the array for each query, not prefix queries.

items.prompt: Choose the total time signal.
items.title: Combine prefix build and query cost
items.instruction: You build prefix sums for n values, then answer q range-sum queries. What total time signal should you name?
items.answerFeedback: Total time is O(n + q): O(n) to build and O(1) for each of q queries.

options.text: O(n + q): build prefix sums once, then answer q constant-time queries.
O(n), because the preprocessing scan is the only important cost.
O(nq), because every query must scan all n values.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Plan A scans each requested range. Plan B builds prefix sums once and answers each query by subtraction. q is large. Which comparison is decisive?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: When many queries reuse the same input, preprocessing can replace repeated range scans.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice comparing per-query recomputation with reusable preprocessing.

distractorExplanations.wrong_no_space: Avoiding O(n) space can be worse if it causes many repeated scans.
distractorExplanations.wrong_each_query_isolated: The queries are not isolated; they reuse the same fixed array.

items.prompt: Choose the decisive comparison.
items.title: Compare recomputation with prefix sums
items.instruction: Plan A scans each requested range. Plan B builds prefix sums once and answers each query by subtraction. q is large. Which comparison is decisive?
items.answerFeedback: Prefix sums spend O(n) space and build time to avoid repeated range scans across many queries.

options.text: Plan B pays O(n) once so q queries become O(1) each, avoiding repeated range scans.
Plan A is better because it uses less memory, regardless of q.
Treat every query as unrelated, so preprocessing cannot help.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: n and q can both be 100000. A candidate scans all n values for every query. What total cost should reject it?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Repeated full scans create O(nq), which is too large when both dimensions are large.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice treating query count as a separate input dimension.

distractorExplanations.wrong_single_query: O(n) only describes one query, not q repeated queries.
distractorExplanations.wrong_space: Low extra space does not rescue repeated O(nq) time.

items.prompt: Choose the rejecting total cost.
items.title: Reject scan per query
items.constraintSignal: n and q can both be 100000.
items.reasonSignal: O(nq) is not viable when both n and q are large.
items.instruction: n and q can both be 100000. A candidate scans all n values for every query. What total cost should reject it?
items.answerFeedback: Scanning n values for each of q queries is O(nq), which fails when both are large.

options.text: O(nq), because q queries each scan n values.
O(n), because one query scans the array once.
O(1), because the plan does not allocate query storage.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A fixed array has one range-sum query. Which preprocessing claim should you be cautious about?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Preprocessing pays off mainly when the prepared state is reused across many queries.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice checking query count before choosing a preprocessing-heavy plan.

distractorExplanations.wrong_always_prefix: Prefix sums are useful, but one query may not justify building a full prefix array.
distractorExplanations.wrong_sort: Sorting is unrelated to preserving original index ranges.

items.prompt: Choose the cautious preprocessing claim.
items.title: Question preprocessing for one query
items.instruction: A fixed array has one range-sum query. Which preprocessing claim should you be cautious about?
items.answerFeedback: With one query, scanning that range may be simpler than building O(n) prefix state.

options.text: Do not assume prefix preprocessing pays off when there is only one query.
Always build prefix sums because every range query requires preprocessing.
Sort first because one query is too small for prefix sums.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Plan A builds prefix sums in O(n), then answers one query in O(1). Plan B scans only the requested range of length k once. Which comparison is more precise?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: For one query, compare O(n) preprocessing with the actual one-time scan length.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice comparing preprocessing cost to actual query reuse.

distractorExplanations.wrong_query_only: Counting only O(1) query time ignores the O(n) preprocessing.
distractorExplanations.wrong_prefix_always: Prefix sums are not automatically best when there is no reuse.

items.prompt: Choose the precise comparison.
items.title: Compare one query with preprocessing
items.instruction: Plan A builds prefix sums in O(n), then answers one query in O(1). Plan B scans only the requested range of length k once. Which comparison is more precise?
items.answerFeedback: For one query, Plan A costs O(n) total, while Plan B costs O(k) for the scanned range.

options.text: Plan A is O(n) total for one query; Plan B is O(k) for the one scanned range.
Plan A is always O(1) because the final query is constant time.
Plan A is always better because preprocessing is a standard optimization.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: q range queries each scan a range of length k on average. No preprocessing is used. What total cost should you expect?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Repeated query cost depends on query count multiplied by average range length.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice using q and range length instead of only n.

distractorExplanations.wrong_q_only: q counts queries, but each query still does k work.
distractorExplanations.wrong_nq_always: O(nq) is the worst case when each query scans about n values; the prompt gives average length k.

items.prompt: Choose the total cost without preprocessing.
items.title: Cost repeated average-length ranges
items.instruction: q range queries each scan a range of length k on average. No preprocessing is used. What total cost should you expect?
items.answerFeedback: q queries times k scanned values per query gives O(q * k).

options.text: O(q * k), because each of q queries scans about k values.
O(q), because there are q queries.
Always O(nq), even when ranges are much shorter than n.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A prefix array is built for immutable data. Later the original array changes between queries. What must you reconsider?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Preprocessed state is valid only while it matches the underlying data.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice checking whether preprocessing can be reused safely.

distractorExplanations.wrong_reuse_blindly: A stale prefix array can answer the wrong values after updates.
distractorExplanations.wrong_no_preprocessing: Updates do not make preprocessing impossible, but they change the maintenance cost.

items.prompt: Choose what must be reconsidered.
items.title: Check preprocessing reuse after updates
items.instruction: A prefix array is built for immutable data. Later the original array changes between queries. What must you reconsider?
items.answerFeedback: You must account for update or rebuild cost, because the preprocessed state can become stale.

options.text: Whether the prefix state must be updated or rebuilt after data changes.
Nothing; prefix sums stay correct after any array update.
All preprocessing must be rejected whenever data can change.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Order the reasoning steps for many range-total queries on a fixed array.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Start with reuse, then decide whether preprocessing reduces repeated query work.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice ordering preprocessing reasoning before writing mechanics.

items.prompt: Tap the steps in order.
items.title: Order preprocessing reasoning
items.instruction: Order the reasoning steps for many range-total queries on a fixed array.
items.answerFeedback: The key sequence is reuse: fixed input, many queries, repeated recomputation cost, then preprocessing.

subgoals.text: Notice that all queries reuse the same fixed array.
Notice that many range queries will arrive.
Estimate the repeated cost of scanning ranges independently.
Choose preprocessing if it reduces total repeated work.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A teammate says the prefix-sum plan is O(1) because each query is O(1). What correction should you make?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Per-query cost is not the full algorithm cost; preprocessing and space must be counted too.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice naming build time, query time, total time, and space separately.

distractorExplanations.wrong_query_only: This ignores the O(n) preprocessing phase.
distractorExplanations.wrong_reject: The plan is not wrong; the explanation is incomplete.

items.prompt: Choose the best correction.
items.title: Correct query-only complexity claim
items.instruction: A teammate says the prefix-sum plan is O(1) because each query is O(1). What correction should you make?
items.answerFeedback: Prefix sums have O(n) preprocessing and O(n) space, even though each query is O(1).

options.text: Say each query is O(1), but the full plan also has O(n) preprocessing and O(n) space.
Agree that the whole plan is O(1) because query time is the only cost.
Reject prefix sums because any preprocessing makes the plan worse.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A fixed array receives many queries asking for the minimum value in a range. Which warning should you notice before applying prefix sums?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Prefix sums work for additive totals; not every range query has the same preprocessing structure.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice checking whether the operation matches the preprocessing structure.

distractorExplanations.wrong_prefix_all: Prefix sums do not directly answer arbitrary range minimum queries.
distractorExplanations.wrong_scan_only: Many queries may still justify preprocessing, but not necessarily prefix sums.

items.prompt: Choose the warning.
items.title: Do not overgeneralize prefix sums
items.instruction: A fixed array receives many queries asking for the minimum value in a range. Which warning should you notice before applying prefix sums?
items.answerFeedback: Prefix sums support additive range totals, not arbitrary range minimum queries.

options.text: Prefix sums fit additive totals, not arbitrary range minimum queries.
Prefix sums answer every type of range query after O(n) preprocessing.
No preprocessing can ever help range minimum queries.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A fixed array has n values and q range-total queries. Which answer correctly separates the phases?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A good complexity answer names preprocessing, per-query work, total time, and extra space separately.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice giving complete multi-phase complexity contracts.

distractorExplanations.wrong_scan_each: This describes recomputation, not the prefix-sum plan.

items.prompt: Choose the complete phase-separated answer.
items.title: Separate preprocessing query total and space
items.instruction: A fixed array has n values and q range-total queries. Which answer correctly separates the phases?
items.answerFeedback: The complete prefix-sum contract is O(n) build, O(1) per query, O(n + q) total time, and O(n) extra space.

options.text: Preprocess O(n), query O(1), total O(n + q), extra space O(n).
The algorithm is simply O(1), because each query is O(1).
Preprocess O(1), query O(n), total O(nq), extra space O(1).

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A scan keeps current value, previous value, and best answer. Which space signal should you name?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Current, previous, and best are a constant-size running summary.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice recognizing fixed running state in scans.

distractorExplanations.wrong_linear: Seeing n input values does not mean storing n auxiliary values.
distractorExplanations.wrong_map: No later membership lookup is needed, so a map is not implied.

items.prompt: Choose the correct space signal.
items.title: Identify fixed running summary
items.instruction: A scan keeps current value, previous value, and best answer. Which space signal should you name?
items.answerFeedback: The extra space is O(1) because the scan stores a fixed number of values.

options.text: O(1), because the scan stores only a fixed running summary.
O(n), because the scan has processed n values.
O(n), because every scan should store a map of previous values.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A teammate says a set is O(1) space because it is one variable. What correction should you make?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Auxiliary space counts the values stored inside the collection.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice counting collection capacity as part of auxiliary space.

distractorExplanations.wrong_one_variable: One variable can reference a collection with many stored values.
distractorExplanations.wrong_ignore_contents: The collection contents are exactly what auxiliary space must count.

items.prompt: Choose the correction.
items.title: Correct one-variable set reasoning
items.instruction: A teammate says a set is O(1) space because it is one variable. What correction should you make?
items.answerFeedback: A set can be O(n) auxiliary space if it stores up to n input-derived values.

options.text: Count the number of values stored inside the set, not just the variable holding it.
The set is O(1) because the code declares only one set variable.
Collection contents do not count as auxiliary space.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A frequency map stores one entry per distinct value. n values are scanned, and there can be d distinct values. Which space signal is most precise?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The map grows with the number of distinct keys, bounded by n.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice using distinct-key count when it is more precise than raw n.

distractorExplanations.wrong_n_only: O(n) is a valid upper bound, but d distinct keys is the more precise signal here.
distractorExplanations.wrong_constant: The map is not constant if the number of distinct keys grows.

items.prompt: Choose the most precise space signal.
items.title: Use distinct-key map space
items.instruction: A frequency map stores one entry per distinct value. n values are scanned, and there can be d distinct values. Which space signal is most precise?
items.answerFeedback: The map stores d distinct keys, so O(d) is precise and O(n) is the upper bound.

options.text: O(d), bounded by O(n), because the map stores one entry per distinct value.
Exactly O(n) in all cases, even if there are only a few distinct values.
O(1), because the map is one variable.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A function returns all matching pairs, and there may be many of them. What output-space warning should you name?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Auxiliary state may be small, but the returned output can grow with the number of produced pairs.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice comparing in-place and new-output contracts separately from time.

distractorExplanations.wrong_same: Both scan once, but they do not necessarily use the same extra space.
distractorExplanations.wrong_time_only: The question asks for space, not only time.

items.prompt: Choose the decisive space comparison.
items.title: Compare in-place and new-output space
items.instruction: A function returns all matching pairs, and there may be many of them. What output-space warning should you name?
items.answerFeedback: Plan A can be O(1) extra space, while Plan B can use O(n) space for the returned array.

options.text: Plan A can use O(1) extra space; Plan B can use O(n) for the new result array.
They have the same space because both scan the same input once.
Only time matters because both plans are O(n).

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A routine builds and returns a list of all accepted records. What should the space answer say about output storage?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: State whether you are counting auxiliary working state, returned output storage, or both.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice checking whether output size is part of the contract.

distractorExplanations.wrong_counters: The routine is not storing only counters; it returns all matching pairs.
distractorExplanations.wrong_constant: Returning a large result list is not constant space.

items.prompt: Choose the space warning.
items.title: Notice large output space
items.instruction: A routine builds and returns a list of all accepted records. What should the space answer say about output storage?
items.answerFeedback: The result list itself can require O(n^2) output space.

options.text: The output list can grow to O(n^2) because it stores all matching pairs.
The space is O(1) because pair generation can use two loop indexes.
The output list does not count as space because it is returned.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A pair routine only returns the count of matching pairs, not the pairs themselves. What space signal should you name?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Counting many conceptual pairs can still use constant extra space if only a scalar count is stored.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice separating conceptual combinations from stored output.

distractorExplanations.wrong_output_pairs: The routine does not store the pairs; it stores only the count.
distractorExplanations.wrong_time_space_mix: Time may be quadratic, but space depends on what is stored.

items.prompt: Choose the correct space signal.
items.title: Separate counted pairs from stored pairs
items.instruction: A pair routine only returns the count of matching pairs, not the pairs themselves. What space signal should you name?
items.answerFeedback: The extra space can be O(1) because only a scalar count is stored.

options.text: O(1), because only a scalar count is stored.
O(n^2), because there may be O(n^2) conceptual pairs.
O(n), because every pair routine must store the input again.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A map has only three fixed keys, but each key points to an array of input records. What space mistake should you catch?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Fixed key count does not imply constant space when the values stored under those keys grow.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice counting nested collection contents, not only top-level keys.

distractorExplanations.wrong_fixed_keys: The keys are fixed, but the arrays under them can grow.
distractorExplanations.wrong_map_variable: The map variable can reference storage proportional to input size.

items.prompt: Choose the space mistake.
items.title: Catch fixed-key bucket mistake
items.instruction: A map has only three fixed keys, but each key points to an array of input records. What space mistake should you catch?
items.answerFeedback: The mistake is counting only fixed keys and ignoring that the bucket arrays can store n records.

options.text: Counting only the fixed keys and ignoring the growing arrays stored as values.
Assuming the map must be O(n) because every map always grows by key count.
Ignoring time complexity because the map is stored in one variable.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A routine returns only true or false. A teammate says the boolean result alone proves O(1) extra space. What is missing from that reasoning?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The result type is not enough; you must check whether the algorithm stores seen values or other input-growing state.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice naming the operation that requires memory before choosing set/map.

distractorExplanations.wrong_constant: The returned boolean is small, but the algorithm may still store seen values while computing it.
distractorExplanations.wrong_always_set: Some boolean scans need only counters or flags; memory depends on the operation, not the answer type.

items.prompt: Choose the correct memory reasoning.
items.title: Avoid automatic set for boolean result
items.instruction: A routine returns only true or false. A teammate says the boolean result alone proves O(1) extra space. What is missing from that reasoning?
items.answerFeedback: The result type is not enough; check whether the algorithm stores seen values or other input-growing state.

options.text: The result type is not enough; you must check whether the algorithm stores seen values or other input-growing state.
A boolean result always means O(1) auxiliary space.
A boolean result always requires a set, because every yes/no question needs memory.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Order the review steps for auxiliary-space accounting.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: First identify what is stored, then check whether its size grows with input, then separate auxiliary state from returned output when the contract requires it.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice reviewing storage contents before naming space complexity.

items.prompt: Tap the space-review steps in order.
items.title: Order auxiliary-space review
items.instruction: Order the review steps for auxiliary-space accounting.
items.answerFeedback: Space accounting starts from what is stored and whether it grows with input, then handles returned output explicitly.

subgoals.text: Identify every variable or collection that stores extra data.
Check whether each stored structure grows with input size or distinct values.
Separate auxiliary state from output storage required by the contract.
Name the final extra-space complexity.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A function receives an input array of n values and scans it using two counters. It does not allocate another collection. What auxiliary space should you name?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Auxiliary space counts extra working storage, not the input already supplied to the function.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice explaining the repeated work rather than naming only a label.

distractorExplanations.wrong_input: Auxiliary space excludes the already-provided input.
distractorExplanations.wrong_future: Space should describe the stated algorithm, not a possible different one.

items.prompt: Choose the correct complexity reasoning.
items.title: Exclude input from auxiliary space
items.instruction: A function receives an input array of n values and scans it using two counters. It does not allocate another collection. What auxiliary space should you name?
items.answerFeedback: Auxiliary space counts extra working storage, not the input already supplied to the function.

options.text: O(1), because the input array itself is not auxiliary space.
O(n), because the input array has n values.
O(n^2), because the function could compare values later.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A routine emits every matching item and there may be r matches. What should a precise space answer mention?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Name the auxiliary state separately from returned output storage.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice explaining the repeated work rather than naming only a label.

distractorExplanations.wrong_never: Some analyses report output storage separately, and the answer should state the convention.
distractorExplanations.wrong_square: Output size depends on the number of matches, here r.

items.prompt: Choose the correct complexity reasoning.
items.title: Report output size separately
items.instruction: A routine emits every matching item and there may be r matches. What should a precise space answer mention?
items.answerFeedback: Name the auxiliary state separately from returned output storage.

options.text: Auxiliary state may be small, but the returned output can be O(r).
Returned output never matters in complexity discussion.
The output must be O(n^2) whenever matches are returned.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Order the reasoning steps for choosing a strategy from input constraints.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Start with the input limit, count repeated work, estimate growth, then choose a plan that fits.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice using constraints before implementation details.

items.prompt: Tap the steps in the correct order.
items.title: Order constraint-based strategy reasoning
items.instruction: Order the reasoning steps for choosing a strategy from input constraints.
items.answerFeedback: Read the largest input size first, estimate repeated work, translate it into growth, then keep only viable plans.

subgoals.text: Read the largest input size.
Count how often the repeated operation can run.
Translate the repeated work into growth.
Choose a plan whose growth fits the limit.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A teammate chooses a double loop first and says they will check constraints after it works. What mistake should you diagnose?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Constraints are a first-pass filter for strategy viability, not a final check after implementation.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice rejecting implementation-first reasoning when the input limit already rules out a plan.

distractorExplanations.wrong_style: The core issue is not code style; it is using constraints too late.
distractorExplanations.wrong_testing: Tests do not replace an upfront growth check against the input limit.

items.prompt: Choose the reasoning mistake.
items.title: Diagnose implementation-first reasoning
items.instruction: A teammate chooses a double loop first and says they will check constraints after it works. What mistake should you diagnose?
items.answerFeedback: This is implementation-first reasoning: the constraint should screen the strategy before coding.

options.text: They are using constraints too late instead of screening the strategy before implementation.
They are mainly choosing a code style that may be harder to read.
They only need more test cases before complexity matters.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A review must choose between two explanations of the same solution: one names the Big-O label only, the other explains repeated work. Which is better?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A useful complexity explanation names the operation pattern that causes the growth.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice justifying Big-O labels with the repeated work that creates them.

distractorExplanations.wrong_label_only: A label without the operation pattern is weak diagnostic feedback.
distractorExplanations.wrong_variable_names: Variable names do not explain growth.

items.prompt: Choose the better explanation style.
items.title: Prefer operation-pattern explanation
items.instruction: A review must choose between two explanations of the same solution: one names the Big-O label only, the other explains repeated work. Which is better?
items.answerFeedback: The better explanation connects the Big-O label to the repeated work pattern.

options.text: The explanation that says which repeated operation creates the growth.
The explanation that only says O(n^2), because labels are enough.
The explanation that focuses on whether the loop variables are named clearly.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A learner sees two loops and immediately says O(n^2). The inner loop always runs at most 4 times. What mistake should you diagnose?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Nested syntax alone is not enough; the inner bound must grow with input size to create quadratic work.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice checking whether repeated work is input-sized or constant-bounded.

distractorExplanations.wrong_nested: A loop inside a loop is not automatically quadratic.
distractorExplanations.wrong_unknown: The prompt gives the missing bound: the inner loop is capped at 4.

items.prompt: Choose the mistake diagnosis.
items.title: Diagnose nested-loop overgeneralization
items.instruction: A learner sees two loops and immediately says O(n^2). The inner loop always runs at most 4 times. What mistake should you diagnose?
items.answerFeedback: They are using nested-loop syntax as the signal instead of checking whether the inner work grows with n.

options.text: They assumed nested syntax means O(n^2) without checking the constant inner bound.
They are correct because every nested loop is O(n^2).
The complexity cannot be reasoned about because there are two loops.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A learner sees one loop and immediately says O(n). The loop body calls a helper that scans the whole input. What mistake should you diagnose?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: One visible loop does not guarantee linear time when the body hides another scan.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice inspecting loop body cost before naming complexity.

distractorExplanations.wrong_one_loop: A helper can hide repeated work.
distractorExplanations.wrong_helper_constant: A helper is not automatically constant time.

items.prompt: Choose the mistake diagnosis.
items.title: Diagnose hidden-helper oversight
items.instruction: A learner sees one loop and immediately says O(n). The loop body calls a helper that scans the whole input. What mistake should you diagnose?
items.answerFeedback: They counted only outer iterations and ignored the hidden scan inside the helper.

options.text: They counted the visible loop but ignored the helper's full-input scan.
They are correct because one visible loop is always O(n).
They are correct because helper calls are always O(1).

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A learner gives only the final complexity pair but cannot say why. What feedback should they receive?
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Correct labels are weaker without the repeated-work signal that explains them.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice naming the exact operation pattern that causes the complexity.

distractorExplanations.wrong_label_enough: A correct label without reasoning is fragile and hard to transfer.
distractorExplanations.wrong_code_first: Writing code does not replace explaining the cost model.

items.prompt: Choose the best feedback.
items.title: Require explanation beyond Big-O label
items.instruction: A learner gives only the final complexity pair but cannot say why. What feedback should they receive?
items.answerFeedback: They should connect the complexity label to the repeated operation pattern.

options.text: Name the repeated operation pattern that produces the time and space growth.
No feedback is needed if the final Big-O label is correct.
Skip reasoning and write the implementation first.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Order the review steps when a proposed solution might be too slow.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A useful review goes from constraints to repeated work to growth to the specific mistake.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice structuring complexity feedback instead of jumping to a label.

items.prompt: Tap the review steps in order.
items.title: Order complexity review steps
items.instruction: Order the review steps when a proposed solution might be too slow.
items.answerFeedback: First read constraints, then find repeated work, estimate growth, and name the reasoning mistake.

subgoals.text: Read the input limits and query counts.
Find the operation that repeats with input size.
Estimate the growth class.
Name the specific reasoning mistake if the plan does not fit.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The work is a left-to-right neighbor scan with a boundary around the first index.

distractorExplanations.nested_pair_enumeration: All-pairs comparison solves a broader problem than the prompt asks for. Only the previous neighbor matters at each step.
distractorExplanations.global_sorting: Sorting changes the original order, so it destroys the neighbor relationships you are trying to inspect.
distractorExplanations.frequency_counting: Counts tell you how often a character appears, but not whether equal characters sit next to each other.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Name the local movement first: scan forward and guard the first index before reading a previous neighbor.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice separating local adjacency from global reorderings such as sorting or all-pairs checks.

items.prompt: Select every mechanic that matches the task.
items.title: Recognize a neighbor scan
items.instruction: A task asks you to scan a string and compare each character with the character immediately before it. Which mechanics best describe the work?
items.answerFeedback: Neighbor comparison is local, so one forward scan plus a boundary guard is enough.

options.text: A linear scan over the string.
A boundary guard for the first character or a loop starting at index 1.
A nested comparison of every character with every other character.
Sorting the characters first so equal values group together.
Counting how many times each character appears.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A left-to-right neighbor scan processes the next safe current index after each comparison.

distractorExplanations.index_4: Jumping to the last index skips unprocessed work. Nothing in the prompt lets you discard index 3.
distractorExplanations.index_1: Index 1 was used as context for the comparison, but it is not the next active position.
distractorExplanations.stop: Using the previous character does not finish the scan. Later positions can still change the answer.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Trace local scans one current index at a time unless the algorithm has a stated reason to skip a range.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Practice tracing what the next live index is after each comparison step.

items.prompt: Choose the next trace step.

stepByStepTrace.description: Process current index 3 next because index 2 has already been handled and 3 is still within the safe range.
state: Compared s[2] with s[1] in a 5-character string.

items.title: Trace the next scan index
items.instruction: You are scanning a 5-character string with indexes 0 through 4. The loop has just compared `s[2]` with `s[1]`. What is the next safe index to process?
items.answerFeedback: After processing current index 2, the next unprocessed safe current index in a left-to-right scan is 3.

options.text: Move to index 3.
Jump to index 4 because it is the last valid index.
Move back to index 1 because it was used in the comparison.
Stop because a previous character has already been checked.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Previous-index access is unsafe at the first position unless the loop guards it.

distractorExplanations.middle_duplicate: Repeated letters may affect the answer, but they do not cause a logical out-of-range read on their own.
distractorExplanations.uppercase_letters: Case matters in normalization tasks, not in whether reading s[i - 1] is safe.
distractorExplanations.long_input: Input size affects performance expectations, not whether index -1 is outside the valid logical index range.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Boundary bugs come from where the scan starts, not from the character values inside the string.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Check whether neighbor access can happen at index 0 before thinking about larger optimizations.

items.prompt: Choose the boundary case that creates the real risk.
items.title: Catch the neighbor-access boundary bug
items.instruction: A routine reads `s[i - 1]` while scanning a string. Which input case is most likely to break a careless implementation?
items.answerFeedback: If the loop starts at index 0, reading the previous character uses a non-existent previous element.

options.text: The loop starts at `i = 0` and tries to read `s[-1]`.
The string contains the same character twice in the middle.
The string contains uppercase letters.
The string is very long.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Original adjacency is positional evidence, so sorting is invalid even before you compare runtimes.

distractorExplanations.sorting_too_slow_only: Runtime is not the main problem. The deeper issue is that sorting changes which characters are neighbors.
distractorExplanations.needs_hash_map: A hash map tracks counts or lookup state. It does not preserve original adjacency by itself.
distractorExplanations.needs_nested_loop: All-pairs comparison is unnecessary because only adjacent positions affect the answer.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: When a question depends on original positions, preserve the order and inspect those positions directly.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Ask whether the task depends on the original sequence before considering any reordering step.

items.prompt: Choose the real flaw in the learner's approach.
items.title: Explain why sorting breaks adjacency questions
items.instruction: A learner solves "count how many positions have the same character as the previous position" by sorting the string first. What is the main mistake?
items.answerFeedback: The task is about original neighboring positions, so sorting changes the very evidence you are supposed to inspect.

options.text: Sorting destroys the original neighbor relationships.
Sorting is wrong only because it is slower than scanning.
The task always requires a hash map.
The task requires every pair of characters to be compared.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The loop reads both before the string at index 0 and past the string at index `s.length`.

distractorExplanations.sorting_missing: Sorting would change the original neighbor relationships and does not fix the boundary reads.
distractorExplanations.frequency_map_missing: A frequency map solves a different multiplicity problem and does not repair out-of-range neighbor reads.
distractorExplanations.nested_loop_missing: The task is local adjacency, so a nested loop would add irrelevant work.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: When code reads `s[i - 1]`, inspect the loop start. When code reads `s[i]`, inspect the loop end.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Trace the first and last loop iterations before judging the algorithm idea.

items.prompt: Choose the bug that actually follows from the loop bounds.
items.title: Diagnose both ends of a neighbor loop
items.instruction: A learner writes: `for (let i = 0; i <= s.length; i++) { if (s[i] === s[i - 1]) return false; }`. What is the main bug?
items.answerFeedback: At `i = 0`, the code reads `s[-1]`, outside the valid logical index range. At `i = s.length`, it reads past the last character. The loop boundaries are wrong.

options.text: The loop reads `s[-1]` on the first iteration and reads past the end on the last iteration.
The code should sort the string before checking neighbors.
The code needs a frequency map to compare duplicate counts.
The code needs a nested loop to compare every pair.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Reading `s[i - 1]` means the first safe current index is 1.

distractorExplanations.start_zero: Starting at 0 makes the first iteration read a non-existent previous element.
distractorExplanations.start_two: Starting at 2 skips the valid adjacent pair at indexes 0 and 1.
distractorExplanations.start_last: Starting at the last index skips earlier adjacent pairs.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Choose the loop start from the farthest previous-index expression used inside the loop.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Before writing the loop, name the first index where every indexed read is valid.

items.prompt: Choose the safe loop start.
items.title: Choose the first safe previous-neighbor index
items.instruction: A loop compares `s[i]` with `s[i - 1]`. Which loop start is boundary-safe without adding a separate guard inside the loop?
items.answerFeedback: Starting at `i = 1` makes both `s[i]` and `s[i - 1]` inside the valid logical index range for the first comparison.

options.text: Start at `i = 1`.
Start at `i = 0`.
Start at `i = 2`.
Start at the last index.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: For a next-neighbor read, the current index must stop before the final array position.

distractorExplanations.inclusive_last: At `i = arr.length - 1`, `arr[i + 1]` reads past the array.
distractorExplanations.start_at_one: Starting at 1 skips the valid adjacent pair at indexes 0 and 1 for a next-neighbor scan.
distractorExplanations.all_pairs: Nested all-pairs comparison is broader than checking adjacent next-neighbor pairs.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The last safe current index is `arr.length - 2`, so `i < arr.length - 1` checks the final pair without reading past the end.
feedbackModel.nextAction: When the body reads `arr[i + 1]`, choose a loop condition that keeps `i + 1` inside the valid index range.

items.prompt: Choose the safe and complete loop condition.
items.title: Choose a safe next-neighbor bound
items.instruction: A loop compares `arr[i]` with `arr[i + 1]` while scanning adjacent pairs. Which loop condition keeps the next-neighbor read safe and still checks the final valid pair?
items.answerFeedback: Use `i < arr.length - 1`. The last safe current index is `arr.length - 2`, so the final valid pair is checked without reading past the end.

options.text: `i < arr.length - 1`
`i <= arr.length - 1`
`i = 1; i < arr.length`
Use nested loops to compare every pair.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The loop starts safely for `i - 1`, but the `<=` condition still creates a past-the-end read.

distractorExplanations.start_boundary_only: Starting at 1 protects `s[i - 1]`, but it does not protect `s[i]` when `i === s.length`.
distractorExplanations.needs_sorting: Sorting changes the original neighbor relationships and does not repair the loop bound.
distractorExplanations.needs_nested_loop: The condition is local adjacency, so nested comparison is not required.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A loop can have one boundary correct and the other boundary wrong. Check both first and last iterations.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Trace the first valid iteration and the final attempted iteration separately.

items.prompt: Choose the remaining boundary bug.
items.title: Catch the unsafe inclusive end bound
items.instruction: A learner writes `for (let i = 1; i <= s.length; i++)` before comparing `s[i]` with `s[i - 1]`. What boundary bug remains?
items.answerFeedback: The loop start is safe, but `i <= s.length` eventually makes the code read `s[s.length]`, outside the valid logical string index range.

options.text: The loop still reads `s[s.length]` on the final iteration.
There is no bug because starting at 1 fixes every boundary.
The remaining bug is that the string was not sorted first.
The loop still needs to compare every pair of characters.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: For length 0 or 1, the previous-neighbor loop has no safe current index to process.

distractorExplanations.throws_empty: The loop does not need to throw unless the problem contract forbids empty input.
distractorExplanations.needs_manual_special_case: A special branch can work, but the loop boundary already handles these short inputs naturally.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Trace whether the loop runs before reasoning about a neighbor read. A safe loop can skip short inputs without a special case.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Check whether the loop runs at all for the smallest valid inputs.

items.prompt: Choose the boundary behavior for short strings.
items.title: Trace short inputs through a safe loop

state: Loop starts with i = 1 and condition i < s.length.
stepByStepTrace.description: For length 0 or 1, the condition is false immediately, so there is no current index to process.

items.instruction: A function uses `for (let i = 1; i < s.length; i++)` to compare `s[i]` with `s[i - 1]`. What happens for strings of length 0 or 1?
items.answerFeedback: For length 0 or 1, `i = 1` is not less than `s.length`, so the loop does not run and no invalid previous-neighbor read occurs.

options.text: The loop skips, so no neighbor access occurs.
The loop must throw on empty input.
The first iteration reads `s[-1]`.
The loop is unsafe unless both cases are manually special-cased.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The condition `i > 0` is the guard that makes previous-neighbor access safe at index 0.

distractorExplanations.i_less_than_length: This protects the current index from the end, but it does not protect `i - 1` at the start.
distractorExplanations.s_i_exists: Checking the current character does not prove the previous character exists.
distractorExplanations.s_previous_equals_current: This is the comparison, not the guard that makes the comparison safe.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A guard should protect the specific index expression that could go out of bounds.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Pair each guarded expression with the boundary that can break it.

items.prompt: Choose the guard that protects `s[i - 1]`.
items.title: Choose the guard for previous-neighbor access
items.instruction: A scan starts at `i = 0`, but only reads `s[i - 1]` inside an `if` guard. Which guard protects the previous-neighbor read?
items.answerFeedback: `i > 0` means the previous index exists before `s[i - 1]` is read.

options.text: `i < s.length`
`s[i]` is not empty.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The condition `i < arr.length - 1` is the guard that makes next-neighbor access safe.

distractorExplanations.i_greater_than_zero: This protects previous-neighbor access, not `i + 1` at the end.
distractorExplanations.i_less_equal_length: This permits positions outside the array.
distractorExplanations.arr_i_exists: The current element can exist while the next element does not.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The boundary guard depends on the direction of the neighbor access.
feedbackModel.nextAction: For `i + 1`, trace what happens at the last valid index.

items.prompt: Choose the guard that protects `arr[i + 1]`.
items.title: Choose the guard for next-neighbor access
items.instruction: A scan starts at `i = 0`, but only reads `arr[i + 1]` inside an `if` guard. Which guard protects the next-neighbor read?
items.answerFeedback: `i < arr.length - 1` ensures there is a next element before `arr[i + 1]` is read.

options.text: `i < arr.length - 1`
`i <= arr.length`
`arr[i]` exists.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Comparing with `i - 2` requires starting at index 2, not index 1.

distractorExplanations.start_one: At index 1, `i - 2` is -1, so the read is unsafe.
distractorExplanations.start_zero: At index 0, `i - 2` is -2, so the read is even farther before the array.
distractorExplanations.start_three: Starting at 3 is safe but skips the valid comparison at index 2.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The loop start must account for the largest backward offset used in the loop body.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Find the most negative index expression and choose the first `i` that makes it zero or greater.

items.prompt: Choose the first safe index.
items.title: Choose the boundary for a two-step previous read
items.instruction: A loop compares each value with the value two positions before it using `arr[i - 2]`. What is the first safe index for `i`?
items.answerFeedback: At `i = 2`, `arr[i - 2]` becomes `arr[0]`, the first valid read for that expression.

options.text: Start at `i = 2`.
Start at `i = 1`.
Start at `i = 0`.
Start at `i = 3`.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Reading `arr[i + 2]` requires stopping before the final two indexes as current positions.

distractorExplanations.length_minus_one: This still lets `i` reach the second-to-last index, where `i + 2` is past the end.
distractorExplanations.length: This allows current positions at or beyond the end.
distractorExplanations.length_minus_three: This is safe but skips the last valid current index.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The loop end must account for the largest forward offset used in the loop body.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Find the largest forward index expression and choose the final `i` that keeps it within bounds.

items.prompt: Choose the correct loop condition.
items.title: Choose the boundary for a two-step next read
items.instruction: A loop compares each value with the value two positions after it using `arr[i + 2]`. Which condition keeps the read boundary-safe while checking every valid pair?
items.answerFeedback: The last safe current index is `arr.length - 3`, so the condition should be `i < arr.length - 2`.

options.text: `i < arr.length - 2`
`i < arr.length - 1`
`i < arr.length`
`i < arr.length - 3`

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The code uses the previous index, so an empty array and a first-index iteration are the important risks.

distractorExplanations.large_array: A large array affects performance but does not create a boundary error by itself.
distractorExplanations.negative_values: Negative values are data values, not invalid indexes.
distractorExplanations.duplicates_late: Later duplicates affect the answer, but not whether the first previous-index read is safe.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Boundary bugs come from index expressions and loop bounds, not from ordinary values stored in the array.
feedbackModel.nextAction: When checking a boundary bug, choose a test that forces the dangerous index expression to execute.

items.prompt: Choose the test case that targets the first-index boundary.
items.title: Pick a test for first-index previous access
items.instruction: A learner scans an array from index 0 and compares `arr[i]` with `arr[i - 1]`. Which test case most directly exposes the boundary bug?
items.answerFeedback: A one-element array still enters a careless `i = 0` loop and immediately reads `arr[-1]`, outside the valid logical array index range.

options.text: An array with one million values.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A next-neighbor loop that reaches the last index will read past the end.

distractorExplanations.empty_array: An empty array may skip the loop depending on the condition, so it may not expose the last-index next read.
distractorExplanations.all_same: Equal values affect the predicate result, not whether the end boundary is safe.
distractorExplanations.sorted_values: Sorted order is unrelated to protecting `i + 1`.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: To expose a next-neighbor end bug, pick the smallest input where the loop reaches a last element and tries to read one more.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Trace the final iteration, not only the first one.

items.prompt: Choose the smallest direct boundary test.
items.title: Pick a test for last-index next access
items.instruction: A learner scans with `i < arr.length` and compares `arr[i]` with `arr[i + 1]`. Which test case most directly exposes the end-boundary bug?
items.answerFeedback: With `[7]`, the loop reaches `i = 0` and immediately reads `arr[1]`, which is past the end.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: When scanning backward and reading `i - 1`, the loop must stop before `i` reaches 0.

distractorExplanations.i_less_than_length: This is an upper-bound check, not the lower-bound needed in a backward scan.
distractorExplanations.i_greater_than_one: This is safe but skips the valid comparison at `i = 1`.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Backward scans still need the lower boundary protected when reading previous indexes.
feedbackModel.nextAction: For reverse loops, trace the final iteration near index 0.

items.prompt: Choose the safe reverse-loop condition.
items.title: Protect index zero in a reverse previous-neighbor scan
items.instruction: A reverse loop compares `arr[i]` with `arr[i - 1]`. Which condition lets it stop safely while still checking the pair at indexes 0 and 1?
items.answerFeedback: The loop can run down to `i = 1`, where `arr[i - 1]` is `arr[0]`. It must not run at `i = 0`.

options.text: Continue while `i > 0`.
Continue while `i >= 0`.
Continue while `i < arr.length`.
Continue while `i > 1`.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A while loop with manual index updates has the same boundary risk as a for loop.

distractorExplanations.increment_missing: The snippet increments `i`; the issue is the out-of-range first previous read.
distractorExplanations.nested_loop_missing: A nested loop would add irrelevant comparisons.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Loop syntax does not change boundary reasoning. Inspect the first and last index values actually used.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Apply the same boundary checklist to `for`, `while`, and manual pointer loops.

items.prompt: Choose the real bug in the while loop.
items.title: Diagnose previous access in a while loop
items.instruction: A learner writes `let i = 0; while (i < s.length) { if (s[i] === s[i - 1]) return false; i++; }`. What is the main bug?
items.answerFeedback: The first loop iteration uses `i = 0`, so `s[i - 1]` reads `s[-1]`, outside the valid logical string index range.

options.text: The first iteration reads `s[-1]`.
The loop never increments `i`.
The string must be sorted before the loop.
The code needs a nested loop to compare all pairs.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A safe refactor can either start at 1 or keep index 0 and guard the previous-neighbor comparison.

distractorExplanations.sort_first: Sorting changes the original adjacency evidence instead of fixing the boundary.
distractorExplanations.use_frequency_map: A frequency map solves multiplicity questions, not local previous-neighbor safety.
distractorExplanations.skip_last: Skipping the last index protects the wrong end for `i - 1` access.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Fix the boundary that the indexed expression actually threatens. For `i - 1`, protect the start.
feedbackModel.nextAction: When refactoring boundary bugs, prefer the smallest loop-bound or guard change that preserves the original task.

items.prompt: Choose the boundary-preserving refactor.
items.title: Refactor a previous-neighbor boundary bug
items.instruction: A loop starts at `i = 0` and reads `s[i - 1]`. Which refactor fixes the boundary bug while preserving the same adjacent-scan task?
items.answerFeedback: Starting the comparison loop at `i = 1` preserves every adjacent pair and avoids reading a non-existent previous character.

options.text: Start the comparison loop at `i = 1`.
Sort the string first.
Build a frequency map before scanning.
Stop the loop before the last character.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Skipping the last index is the correct refactor for a next-neighbor read.

distractorExplanations.start_at_one: Starting at 1 protects previous-neighbor reads and skips the first valid next-neighbor pair.
distractorExplanations.sort_first: Sorting changes the original adjacency relationships.
distractorExplanations.use_frequency_map: Counting values does not repair the end boundary for `i + 1`.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: For `i + 1`, the unsafe boundary is the end of the array, not the beginning.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Tie each refactor to the exact boundary threatened by the index expression.

items.prompt: Choose the boundary-preserving refactor.
items.title: Refactor a next-neighbor boundary bug
items.instruction: A loop scans with `i < arr.length` and reads `arr[i + 1]`. Which refactor fixes the boundary bug while preserving every valid adjacent comparison?
items.answerFeedback: Using `i < arr.length - 1` makes the last safe current index `arr.length - 2`, so the final adjacent pair is checked without reading past the end.

options.text: Change the condition to `i < arr.length - 1`.
Start at `i = 1`.
Sort the array before scanning.
Build a frequency map before the loop.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The phrase immediately after means the algorithm needs next-neighbor access and an end boundary.

distractorExplanations.previous_boundary: Previous-neighbor access is used for `immediately before`, not `immediately after`.
distractorExplanations.frequency_counting: Counts do not preserve which character sits immediately after another character.
distractorExplanations.global_sorting: Sorting changes the original next-neighbor relationships.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Map directional words in the prompt to the index expression and boundary they imply.
feedbackModel.nextAction: When a prompt says before or after, translate it into `i - 1` or `i + 1` before choosing loop bounds.

items.prompt: Choose the boundary signal in the prompt.
items.title: Map immediately-after wording to the end boundary
items.instruction: A task asks whether any character is the same as the character immediately after it. Which boundary matters most?
items.answerFeedback: Immediately after maps to `s[i + 1]`, so the loop must protect the end of the string.

options.text: A next-neighbor read with an end boundary.
A previous-neighbor read with a start boundary.
A count table for each character.
Sorting first so equal characters group together.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The loop body reads both `i - 1` and `i + 1`, so both ends must be protected.

distractorExplanations.start_one_only: Starting at 1 protects `i - 1`, but the loop must also stop before the last index to protect `i + 1`.
distractorExplanations.stop_before_last_only: Stopping before the last index protects `i + 1`, but starting at 0 still breaks `i - 1`.
distractorExplanations.full_range: Scanning every index from 0 through the last index makes both boundary reads unsafe.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: When a loop reads neighbors on both sides, the safe current indexes are the interior positions only.
feedbackModel.nextAction: List every indexed expression in the loop body and intersect their safe ranges.

items.prompt: Choose the safe current-index range.
items.title: Intersect both-side neighbor boundaries
items.instruction: A loop compares `arr[i]` with both `arr[i - 1]` and `arr[i + 1]`. Which range is safe for `i`?
items.answerFeedback: The current index must be at least 1 for `i - 1` and at most `arr.length - 2` for `i + 1`, so only interior indexes are safe.

options.text: `i` from 1 through `arr.length - 2`.
`i` from 1 through `arr.length - 1`.
`i` from 0 through `arr.length - 2`.
Every index from 0 through `arr.length - 1`.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: For a 5-element array, indexes 1, 2, and 3 are the only positions with both a previous and next neighbor.

distractorExplanations.index_four: Index 4 has a previous neighbor but no next neighbor.
distractorExplanations.index_zero: Index 0 has a next neighbor but no previous neighbor.
distractorExplanations.stop: The scan is not finished after index 2 because index 3 is still an interior index.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Interior scans advance through positions that have all required neighbor reads available.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Mark the safe current-index range first, then trace movement inside that range.

items.prompt: Choose the next safe trace step.

stepByStepTrace.description: The safe interior range for a 5-element array is indexes 1 through 3. After processing index 2, the next safe current index is 3.
state: Processed index 2 while reading both arr[i - 1] and arr[i + 1].

items.title: Trace an interior-neighbor scan
items.instruction: You scan a 5-element array and at each current index read both `arr[i - 1]` and `arr[i + 1]`. You just processed index 2. What is the next safe current index?
items.answerFeedback: For a 5-element array, the safe current indexes with both neighbors are 1, 2, and 3. After index 2, index 3 is next.

options.text: Move to index 3.
Move to index 4.
Move back to index 0.
Stop because index 2 has both neighbors.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Neighboring pair means local adjacency: scan left to right, compare adjacent values, and protect one boundary.

distractorExplanations.all_pairs: Comparing every pair solves a broader problem than neighboring pairs and includes non-adjacent values.
distractorExplanations.sort_first: Sorting changes the original neighbor relationships the task asks you to inspect.
distractorExplanations.frequency_table: A frequency table tracks counts, not whether two matching values are next to each other.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Translate neighboring pair into a local scan before choosing data structures or broader comparisons.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Name the movement first: compare current value with the adjacent value and guard the boundary at one end.

items.prompt: Select every mechanic that matches the task.
items.title: Recognize neighboring-pair traversal
items.instruction: A task asks you to inspect every neighboring pair in an array and count how many pairs match a condition. Which mechanics best describe the work?
items.answerFeedback: A neighboring-pair task uses one left-to-right scan, compares adjacent values, and protects the boundary where the adjacent value would be outside the array.

options.text: Use a single left-to-right scan.
Compare the current value with an adjacent value.
Protect the boundary at one end of the array.
Compare every pair of values.
Sort the array first.
Build a frequency table.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A frequency map detects repeated values anywhere, but adjacency depends on original neighboring positions.

distractorExplanations.always_slower: The main issue is correctness, not a blanket performance claim.
distractorExplanations.cannot_store_numbers: Frequency maps can store numeric keys; the problem is that counts lose position adjacency.
distractorExplanations.sort_required: Sorting changes the original order and can create neighbors that were not adjacent in the input.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Do not replace a local order constraint with a global count unless the prompt stops caring about original positions.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Ask whether the prompt means duplicate anywhere or immediately next to it in the original order.

items.prompt: Choose the correctness reason.
items.title: Separate adjacent duplicates from duplicates anywhere
items.instruction: A task asks whether any value is equal to the value immediately next to it. Why is a frequency map not the direct solution?
items.answerFeedback: A frequency map can detect repeated values anywhere, but it does not preserve whether the repeats are adjacent in the original order.

options.text: It can detect repeated values anywhere, but not whether repeats are adjacent in the original order.
It is always slower than scanning.
It cannot store numbers.
Adjacency requires sorting first.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Adjacent pairs are formed by each valid current index that still has a next neighbor.

distractorExplanations.n_pairs: The last element has no next neighbor, so it cannot start a next-neighbor pair.
distractorExplanations.n_plus_one_pairs: Boundary-safe scanning never creates more pairs than elements.
