items.title: Avoid unnecessary frequency state in sorted collapse
items.instruction: A sorted array should be collapsed so each value appears once. Which extra state is unnecessary for the intended solution?
items.answerFeedback: Because equal values are already adjacent, a boundary comparison can collapse duplicates without storing full counts.

options.text: A frequency map of every value.
A write boundary for the next unique slot.
The previous kept or previous read value.
A scan index moving left to right.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The wrong implementation forgets to seed the first run before later duplicate checks begin.

distractorExplanations.empty_array: An empty array checks empty-input handling, not whether the first non-empty run is initialized.
distractorExplanations.unsorted_input: The duplicate-collapse contract assumes sorted input, so an unsorted case mixes in a different issue.
distractorExplanations.single_run_only: A single run can reveal the missing first value, but it does not also check that later unique runs are still processed.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: For sorted duplicate collapse, the first run must enter the result before comparisons against previous values can safely control later writes.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Use counterexamples that isolate both initialization of the first run and continued processing of later unique values.

items.prompt: Choose the test case that best targets the missing-first-run bug.
items.title: Pick a counterexample for the missing first run
items.instruction: A sorted-duplicate collapse writes later unique values when they differ from the previous value, but it forgets to seed the first run before the loop. Which test case best checks both the missing first run and a later unique run?
items.answerFeedback: `[1, 1, 2]` checks that the first run contributes `1` and that the later unique value `2` is still kept.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Sorted duplicates become adjacent, so one scan can collapse repeated runs.

distractorExplanations.frequency_map: A frequency map stores more information than needed when the input is already sorted and only one copy should remain.
distractorExplanations.nested_pairs: Sorted adjacency removes the need to compare every pair.
distractorExplanations.sort_again: The prompt already says the array is sorted, so sorting again is unnecessary.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Use the sorted order as structure: equal values form runs that can be collapsed by comparing with the previous kept or previous read value.
feedbackModel.nextAction: When the input is sorted, ask whether duplicate handling can be reduced to adjacent-run reasoning.

items.prompt: Choose the property that enables the collapse.
items.title: Use sorted adjacency for duplicate collapse
items.instruction: A sorted array should be collapsed so each value appears once. Which property makes a one-pass duplicate collapse possible?
items.answerFeedback: In a sorted array, equal values appear next to each other, so a scan can keep the first value of each run.

options.text: Equal values are adjacent in the sorted input.
A frequency map is always required.
Every pair of values must be compared.
The array must be sorted again inside the algorithm.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The first element has no previous element, so it must be handled before previous-value comparisons begin.

distractorExplanations.start_at_zero_compare_previous: At index 0, there is no previous value to compare safely.
distractorExplanations.skip_first_run: Skipping the first run drops a valid unique value.
distractorExplanations.count_all_values: Counting every frequency is stronger than needed for keeping one copy of each sorted value.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Seed the result with the first value when the array is non-empty, then scan later values for new runs.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Before writing duplicate-collapse logic, decide how the first run enters the output.

items.prompt: Choose why first-value initialization matters.
items.title: Explain first-run initialization
items.instruction: When collapsing duplicates in a non-empty sorted array, why is the first value usually accepted before the main duplicate check?
items.answerFeedback: The first value starts the first run and has no previous value to compare against.

options.text: It starts the first run and has no previous value.
It should be compared with index -1.
The first run should be skipped.
Every value's full frequency must be stored first.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A new run starts exactly when the current sorted value differs from the previous kept value.

distractorExplanations.same_as_previous: If the value equals the previous kept value, it is still part of the same duplicate run.
distractorExplanations.always_write: Writing every value keeps duplicates instead of collapsing them.
distractorExplanations.count_required: A full count is unnecessary when sorted adjacency reveals runs directly.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Duplicate collapse keeps one representative per run, not every occurrence.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Trace each value as either same run or new run.

items.prompt: Choose when a value starts a new run.
items.title: Write only the first value of each sorted run
items.instruction: In sorted duplicate collapse, when should the current value be written to the next unique slot?
items.answerFeedback: A different value starts a new sorted run, so it should be kept.

options.text: When it differs from the previous kept value.
When it equals the previous kept value.
Every time, regardless of duplicates.
Only after a full frequency table is built.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The duplicate run of `1`s collapses to one kept value, and `2` starts the next run.

distractorExplanations.write_second_one: The second `1` is still part of the existing run and should not be written as a new unique value.
distractorExplanations.skip_two: `2` differs from the previous kept value, so it starts a new run.
distractorExplanations.need_count_first: The sorted order already exposes the runs without counting every frequency.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Trace sorted collapse by runs: keep the first value of a run and skip the rest of that run.
feedbackModel.nextAction: For each read value, ask whether it starts a new run.

items.prompt: Choose the next duplicate-collapse step.

stepByStepTrace.description: `2` starts a new run after the run of `1`s, so it is written to the next unique slot.

items.title: Trace a new run after duplicates
items.instruction: You collapse sorted array `[1, 1, 2]`. After keeping the first `1`, what should happen when the scan reaches `2`?
items.answerFeedback: `2` differs from the previous kept value `1`, so it starts a new run and should be written.

options.text: Write `2` as the next unique value.
Write the second `1` as another unique value first.
Skip `2` because a duplicate was seen earlier.
Build a full frequency table before deciding.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: An empty sorted array has no first value to seed, so duplicate collapse should return an empty result.

distractorExplanations.read_index_zero: Reading index 0 is unsafe because no element exists.
distractorExplanations.write_one: No value was accepted, so the logical length cannot become 1.
distractorExplanations.need_dummy: A dummy value would pollute the result unless the contract explicitly asks for one.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: First-value initialization must be guarded by a non-empty check.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Handle empty input before seeding the first unique value.

items.prompt: Choose the empty-input behavior.
items.title: Guard first-value initialization on empty input
items.instruction: A sorted duplicate-collapse routine normally accepts the first value before scanning from index 1. What should happen for an empty array?
items.answerFeedback: There is no first value to seed, so the result should remain empty or the logical length should be 0.

options.text: Return an empty result or logical length 0.
Read index 0 and accept it.
Return logical length 1.
Insert a dummy value as the first unique value.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A single-element sorted array contains exactly one unique run.

distractorExplanations.zero_length: A single existing value should be kept.
distractorExplanations.compare_previous: There is no previous value to compare against at index 0.
distractorExplanations.need_second_value: A second value is not needed to decide that the first run exists.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Short inputs are handled by the same first-run initialization guard.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Test empty and single-element arrays separately.

items.prompt: Choose the single-element behavior.
items.title: Handle single-element duplicate collapse
items.instruction: What should sorted duplicate collapse return for a single-element array like `[7]`?
items.answerFeedback: `[7]` has one unique run, so the kept result is `[7]` or logical length 1.

options.text: Keep `[7]` with logical length 1.
Return logical length 0.
Compare `7` with a previous element first.
Reject it because there is no second value.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The sorted-input precondition is what makes adjacent duplicate collapse valid.

distractorExplanations.works_unsorted: In unsorted input, equal values can be separated, so comparing only adjacent values can miss duplicates.
distractorExplanations.frequency_unneeded_always: A count table may be needed if the input is not sorted and the task still depends on duplicates anywhere.
distractorExplanations.sort_destroys_contract: Sorting can be invalid for tasks requiring original order, but sorted duplicate collapse explicitly depends on sorted order.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Do not apply sorted-run logic unless the input is sorted or you are allowed to sort first.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Check whether sortedness is a stated precondition before using previous-value duplicate collapse.

items.prompt: Choose why sortedness matters.
items.title: Respect the sorted-input precondition
items.instruction: Why does sorted duplicate-collapse logic not automatically work on an unsorted array?
items.answerFeedback: Without sorted order, equal values can be separated, so adjacent-run logic can miss duplicates.

options.text: Equal values may be separated rather than adjacent.
It works the same on every unsorted array.
Frequency or seen-state is never useful for unsorted input.
Sorted duplicate collapse never depends on sorted order.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A good test for duplicate collapse includes repeated runs and later distinct runs.

distractorExplanations.all_unique: All unique values do not test whether duplicates are skipped.
distractorExplanations.empty_array: Empty input tests initialization but not duplicate-run handling.
distractorExplanations.unsorted_case: Unsorted input tests a different precondition issue.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Choose tests that exercise the actual run-collapse behavior under the sorted-input contract.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Use sorted inputs with multiple runs to test both skipping duplicates and keeping new values.

items.prompt: Choose the strongest sorted-run test case.
items.title: Pick a strong sorted-run collapse test
items.instruction: Which sorted input best tests that duplicate collapse skips repeats but keeps later new values?
items.answerFeedback: `[1, 1, 2, 2, 3]` tests duplicate skipping across multiple runs and keeping later distinct values.

items.prompt: While scanning an unsorted array, the algorithm must repeatedly answer: "Has this value appeared earlier?" Which strategy signal is strongest?

options.text: Maintain keyed seen-before state, such as a Set of values from the processed prefix.
Maintain one scalar containing the number of processed elements.
Maintain the sum of all processed values.
Compare only the first and last processed values.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The query is repeated and is addressed by the current value as a key.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Keyed state is justified when future decisions repeatedly ask for information associated with a particular value.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Complete the question the algorithm asks in the form: "What state is stored for this key?"

items.prompt: A function must emit only the first occurrence of each value while preserving encounter order. Which state best supports the decision for each observation?

options.text: A Set of values already emitted.
One boolean indicating whether any duplicate has appeared.
One scalar containing the smallest value seen.
Two endpoint indexes compared as an independent pair.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Each observation needs a membership answer for its own value.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Stable deduplication requires per-value seen state; one global scalar cannot distinguish which values were emitted.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Identify the exact value whose prior presence determines whether the current item is retained.

items.prompt: A stream of updates repeatedly refers to users by userId and must retrieve the current state for that specific user. Which representation is the natural starting point?

options.text: A Map keyed by userId with the user's current state as the associated value.
One variable containing the state of the most recently updated user.
One scalar counting all updates across every user.
A list sorted by update time without any state indexed by userId.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The algorithm repeatedly retrieves and modifies state addressed by a stable identity.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: When updates name a key directly, keyed state avoids treating unrelated entities as one aggregate.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Identify the field used to address the state that must be retrieved repeatedly.

items.prompt: While scanning values, a pair condition can be rewritten as: "For the current value, has the required partner value appeared earlier?" Which strategy signal is present?

options.text: Maintain prior candidates keyed by value and look up the required complement.
Maintain every element between two boundaries as one contiguous window.
Maintain only the sum of all values processed so far.
Use one pointer to read and another to overwrite rejected values.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The current item determines one specific partner key that can be queried directly.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Complement lookup is keyed candidate retrieval, not a property of the complete interval between two positions.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Rewrite the relationship to isolate the prior value required by the current observation.

items.prompt: Items must be divided into groups so that all items with the same category identifier appear in the same bucket. Which strategy fits?

options.text: Use a Map from category identifier to the bucket of items assigned to that category.
Use only a Set of category identifiers, even though the group members must be returned.
Append every item to one global array without retaining category associations.
Compare only the first and last items to determine all groups.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Every item belongs to a bucket addressed by an equivalence key.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Grouping requires keyed association between each class identifier and the state accumulated for that class.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Identify the value that names an equivalence class and the information accumulated under it.

items.prompt: One collection contains allowed codes. A second, much longer collection must be filtered by repeatedly asking whether each code is allowed. Which high-level design is appropriate?

options.text: Store the allowed codes in a Set and query membership for each item in the second collection.
Store one boolean indicating whether the allowed collection is non-empty.
Add all allowed codes into one scalar and compare each item with that sum.
Treat the two collections as boundaries of one sliding window.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Many observations query membership against the same reusable reference collection.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Repeated lookup by value is the hash-state signal; the two collections do not form one contiguous candidate range.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Precompute the state that answers the repeated per-item query.

items.prompt: A traversal must avoid processing the same object instance twice. Different objects with identical properties must still be treated as separate instances. Which state matches the contract in JavaScript?

options.text: A Set of the object references already processed.
A Set of serialized property values that intentionally merges equal-looking objects.
One scalar containing how many objects have been processed.
Sort the objects and assume adjacent objects share reference identity.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Membership is defined by JavaScript object identity rather than structural equality.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Set can directly track object references when the contract concerns whether the exact same instance was seen.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Determine whether equality means the same reference or merely equal contents.

items.prompt: A function only needs the total sum of all numeric values. A candidate proposes storing every value in a Map before calculating the sum. What is the best review?

options.text: Keyed state is not justified; one running total represents all information required by the output.
A Map is required because numeric values cannot be accumulated directly.
A Set is required so duplicate values do not contribute more than once.
Every sum problem should first group equal values into buckets.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The required state is one aggregate over all observations, not information retrieved by individual key.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Hash state is unnecessary when one scalar can fully summarize the processed input for the required result.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Ask whether future decisions retrieve state for a specific key or only update one global aggregate.

items.prompt: An algorithm tracks which lowercase English letters have appeared. The domain is permanently limited to the 26 letters a–z. Which representation is a reasonable alternative to hash state?

options.text: A fixed-size boolean array indexed by letter position.
A new nested scan over the complete processed prefix for every character.
One boolean shared by all 26 letters.
One scalar formed by adding character codes.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Keys come from a small, fixed, directly indexable domain.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Keyed lookup does not always require a hash table. Bounded domains can often use direct indexed storage.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Check whether every possible key can be mapped to a compact fixed index.

items.prompt: An array may be reordered, and the only required result is whether any duplicate value exists. Which statement is correct?

options.text: A seen Set is one valid strategy, while sorting and checking adjacent values is another valid high-level strategy.
Hash state is mandatory because sorting cannot expose duplicate values.
A single duplicate count is sufficient without remembering or ordering values.
A variable-size sliding window is required because duplicates define a contiguous range.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Reordering is legal, and sorting can place equal values next to one another.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Recognizing a hash-state signal does not mean hashing is the only legal solution.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Check whether input reordering is permitted and whether ordering exposes the needed relationship.

items.prompt: A task must emit every pair of records satisfying an arbitrary comparison function. No single lookup key can be derived from one record to identify all matching partners. Which observation is most precise?

options.text: A keyed hash strategy is not automatically justified; direct pair enumeration or another structure matching the predicate may be required.
Putting the records in a Set automatically identifies every matching pair.
Choosing any record field as a Map key guarantees that all valid partners can be recovered.
One scalar containing the number of processed records is sufficient to emit the pairs.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The predicate does not expose a stable key that maps one observation to its possible matches.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Hash state helps only when the required relationship can be queried meaningfully by key.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Explain what exact key would retrieve all and only the relevant prior candidates.

items.prompt: A stream contains transactions for many accounts. The algorithm must maintain a separate running total for each accountId. Which signal is present?

options.text: Per-key aggregation: use accountId to retrieve and update that account's total.
One global total is sufficient because all transactions are numeric.
A Set of account IDs is sufficient because membership contains each account's balance.
Compare transactions from both ends to determine each account's total.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The same operation must maintain an independent aggregate for every identity.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A global accumulator loses which portion of the total belongs to each key.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Identify whether the aggregate is global or separately owned by each key.

items.prompt: A task requires values to be returned in ascending order. A candidate chooses a Map solely because it offers keyed lookup. What is missing from the reasoning?

options.text: Keyed lookup does not itself satisfy ascending output order; the strategy must separately account for ordering.
Maps always return keys in descending order, so they can never be used.
Nothing is missing because hashing automatically orders keys.
A Set membership check determines the correct ascending position of every value.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The stated requirement concerns result order rather than lookup by identity.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Hash state and sorting solve different needs and may need to be combined or compared separately.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Separate the state-access requirement from the final output-order requirement.

items.prompt:
A review says:

"Use a Map because Maps are fast."

Which replacement explanation would properly justify keyed hash state?

options.text: Future observations repeatedly need to retrieve state associated with a specific value or identity, so that state should be indexed by the queried key.
Map is a modern JavaScript collection and should therefore replace all arrays and scalar variables.
The algorithm has at least two variables, which is enough to require a Map.
The input may be large, so keyed state is correct even when no lookup by key is performed.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The representation should be derived from the operations the algorithm must support.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Hash state is justified by repeated key-addressed queries, not by a generic preference for a data structure.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Name the exact lookup question and the key used to answer it.

items.prompt: An algorithm only needs to answer: "Have I already seen this value?" It does not need a count, index, or metadata. Which state representation best matches the contract?

options.text: A Set containing each value that has been seen.
A Map from each value to true.
A Map from each value to the number of times it has appeared.
A Map from each value to every index where it appeared.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Membership is the only query the algorithm needs to support.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: When the state for a key is only present or absent, the key itself carries all required information.
feedbackModel.nextAction: List the exact question asked about each key before selecting the collection.

options.text: The Map works, but its boolean values add no information; a Set expresses the presence-only contract more directly.
The Map is required because a Set cannot test whether a key exists.
Every possible value should first be inserted with false and later changed to true.
A duplicate detector must always store the exact occurrence count for every value.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Every stored value in the Map is the same constant boolean.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A Map is useful when its associated value carries information beyond key membership.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Remove stored values that do not distinguish one key's state from another.

items.prompt: An algorithm must know how many times each value currently occurs. Which representation matches that requirement?

options.text: A Map from each value to its current occurrence count.
A Set containing each value that occurs at least once.
A Map from each value to true.
One number storing the total number of processed elements.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Different keys may have different counts, and the algorithm must preserve those values.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A Set can distinguish zero occurrences from at least one occurrence, but not one occurrence from several.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Use a key-value structure whenever each key needs its own numeric state.

items.prompt:
A changing active range may contain duplicate values. When one occurrence leaves, the algorithm must still know whether another occurrence of that value remains.

Which state is sufficient?

options.text: A Map from value to active occurrence count, deleting the key only when its count reaches zero.
A Set that deletes the value whenever any occurrence leaves.
A Map from value to true, changing nothing when duplicate occurrences enter.
Only one number storing the current number of distinct values.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Removing one occurrence does not necessarily change the key from present to absent.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Presence-only state is insufficient when several active occurrences can share one key.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Ask whether one removal always implies that the key is no longer represented.

items.prompt: A pair-search function must return the original index of a previously seen complement. Which state should it maintain?

options.text: A Map from each seen value to an original index where that value occurred.
A Set of seen values, because membership alone identifies the required index.
A Map from each value to true.
A Set of processed indexes without storing their associated values.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The output requires positional information about the matching key.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A Set can prove that a complement exists but cannot recover the index associated with it.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Store every piece of information that must later be returned or used to construct the result.

items.prompt: A function must return every original index at which each value appears. Which representation fits the output contract?

options.text: A Map from each value to an array of all its original indexes.
A Set containing each distinct value.
A Map from each value to only its most recent index.
A Set containing all indexes without grouping them by value.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Each key needs a collection of associated positions rather than a presence flag or one scalar.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The Map value should model the complete information required for that key.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Determine whether each key maps to one result item or a group of result items.

items.prompt: Words must be grouped by a computed signature, with every word having the same signature placed in the same group. Which state is appropriate?

options.text: A Map from each signature to an array of words in that group.
A Set containing each signature that has appeared.
A Map from each signature to true.
A Set of all words without retaining their signature groups.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The algorithm must retain multiple members associated with each grouping key.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Presence of a group is not enough when the output must contain the group's members.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Choose the Map value type from what must accumulate under each key.

items.prompt: For every category, an algorithm must retain the highest score observed in that category. Which representation best matches the state?

options.text: A Map from each category to its best score so far.
A Set of categories that have appeared.
A Map from each category to true.
One number containing the best score across all categories.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Each category needs its own changing optimum.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Presence state cannot answer a query about the best associated value for a key.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Model the exact aggregate that must be updated independently for each key.

items.prompt: For each user ID, an algorithm must retain a status, last-seen timestamp, and retry count. Which state is appropriate?

options.text: A Map from user ID to an object containing status, lastSeen, and retryCount.
A Set of user IDs that have appeared.
A Map from user ID to true.
Three global variables shared by every user ID.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Each key owns several associated fields that may differ between keys.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A Map value may be structured metadata when one scalar cannot represent the required per-key state.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Define the complete state record associated with one key.

items.prompt: A function emits only the first occurrence of each value while scanning left to right. It only needs to know whether a value has already been emitted. Which state is sufficient?

options.text: A Set of values already emitted.
A Map from each value to every index where it occurs.
A Map storing the exact total count of every value.
A Map storing both the first and last index of every value.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The decision for each observation depends only on prior membership.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Do not collect counts or positions when the output rule uses only first-seen versus already-seen state.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Store no more information than the emission decision requires.

items.prompt:
A function scans an array and returns only whether two distinct elements sum to target.

It checks whether target - current has already been seen before inserting current.

Which state is sufficient for the seen values?

options.text: A Set, because the decision only requires presence of the complement among earlier indexes.
A Map to an index is required even though no index is returned or otherwise used.
A full frequency Map is required for every pair-existence scan.
A Map from each value to true is necessary because Set membership cannot represent prior occurrence.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The output is boolean, and the scan only asks whether a complement exists in the processed prefix.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The collection should match the information consumed by the algorithm, not information a different output contract might require.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Remove associated values that are never read after insertion.

items.prompt: A function must return the exact number of occurrences of every distinct value. Which representation is required?

options.text: A Map from each distinct value to its exact frequency.
A Set of distinct values.
A Map from each value to true.
One number containing only how many distinct values exist.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The returned result contains a separate numeric answer for every key.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Knowing which keys exist does not reveal how often each key occurs.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Derive the stored value type directly from the value required in the output.

items.prompt:
Compare two graph-search contracts:

A. Determine only whether a node has already been visited.

B. Reconstruct the path by remembering which node discovered each visited node.

Which state selection is correct?

options.text: A can use a Set of visited nodes; B needs a Map from each discovered node to its parent.
Both can use only a Set because membership is enough to reconstruct parent relationships.
Both require a Map from each node to true.
A requires parent metadata, while B needs only presence.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The second contract needs associated provenance information for each key.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The same keys may require different representations when the downstream output changes.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Ask what information must be recovered after membership has been established.

items.prompt:
Two versions of a pair-sum task use the same input:

A. Return only whether any valid pair exists.

B. Return the original index of the earlier element in one valid pair.

Which representation choice is best justified?

options.text: A can use a Set of seen values; B needs a Map from seen value to an original index.
Both should use a Set because the underlying pair condition is identical.
B should use a Map from value to boolean because booleans preserve original positions.
A must use a Map because Map is always preferable when keys are numeric.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The search condition is similar, but the required result contains different information.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Representation choice follows the strongest query or output the state must support, not the broad problem label.
feedbackModel.nextAction: For each key, complete the sentence: "Later I must retrieve..."

items.prompt:
A duplicate-detection scan is about to process values[i].

What should the Set named seen contain at that moment?

options.text: Exactly the distinct values from indexes 0 through i - 1.
The distinct values from indexes 0 through i, including the current value.
The distinct values from the unprocessed suffix beginning at i.
Only values already confirmed to occur more than once.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The membership test asks whether the current value occurred at an earlier index.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Before the lookup, seen represents the processed prefix and excludes the current observation.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Describe seen using an explicit index range before reviewing the update order.

items.prompt: Which sequence correctly processes one value when detecting whether any duplicate exists?

options.text: Check seen.has(value); return true if present; otherwise add value and continue.
Add value first, then check seen.has(value).
Clear seen, check membership, then add value.
Add value, immediately delete it, then check membership.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The lookup must ask whether an earlier occurrence exists before the current occurrence enters the state.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Lookup-before-add preserves the distinction between prior membership and the current observation.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Keep the current value outside seen until its seen-before test is complete.

options.text: Every value is present immediately after being added, so the function returns true on the first iteration.
A Set cannot be used for duplicate detection.
seen.has(value) removes the value before the next iteration.
Each value must be added twice before membership can be checked.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The mutation makes the lookup trivially true for the current value.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The question is whether the value was already present, not whether it is present after insertion.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Move the membership check before the insertion.

items.prompt:
Trace lookup-before-add duplicate detection:

values = [2, 1, 2]

What happens when the final 2 is processed?

options.text: seen contains {2, 1}, so membership succeeds and the algorithm reports a duplicate.
seen contains only {1}, because the earlier 2 was removed automatically.
The final 2 is added first and then treated as its own earlier occurrence.
No duplicate is found because equal values must be adjacent.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The Set retains distinct values from the entire processed prefix, not only the previous position.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Seen-before membership detects duplicates separated by any number of intervening values.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Write the Set contents before each membership check.

items.prompt:

items.prompt: What should duplicate detection return for an empty array?

options.text: false, because no value occurs more than once.
true, because the empty state is encountered repeatedly.

true, because an empty array contains one implicit empty value.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: No observation is processed and no membership check succeeds.

items.prompt:

true, because 9 is present in seen after it is added.
true, because every positive number is considered previously seen.

items.prompt:
A correct Set-based scan processes:

items.prompt:

false, because NaN !== NaN under the === operator.

options.text: A new empty Set is created for every observation, so no earlier value survives into the next iteration.
The state fails because duplicate detection requires an array rather than a Set.
The only problem is that has is called before add.
A Set cannot be used inside a for-of loop.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The state lifetime covers only one iteration instead of the complete processed prefix.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Seen-before state must persist across observations to represent earlier values.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Create the Set once before the scan begins.

options.text: It forgets each value immediately, so later occurrences cannot be recognized as seen before.
It correctly reduces memory while preserving duplicate detection.
It detects non-adjacent duplicates but not adjacent duplicates.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The Set no longer retains the processed prefix's values after each iteration.

items.prompt:

What does new Set(values).size equal?

options.text: 3, because the distinct values are 3, 1, and 2.

items.prompt:
A left-to-right scan emits a value only when it is not already in seen.

For:

items.prompt:
Two solutions check for duplicates:

A is stronger because retaining older values can create false duplicate matches.

options.text: It forgets values from earlier parts of the array, so a later non-adjacent repetition may be missed.

items.prompt: Which invariant precisely describes a correct lookup-before-add duplicate scan after index i has been processed without returning true?

items.prompt:

options.text: Repeated occurrences in a can be pushed repeatedly, so the implementation needs distinct-result state or must iterate distinct values.

items.prompt:

items.prompt: While building a frequency Map from left to right, what should counts.get(value) mean after processing an input prefix?

options.text: The key may still exist with count 0, so membership does not prove that an occurrence remains available.

items.prompt: Which condition precisely defines equality of two multisets?

items.prompt: What frequency state represents an empty collection?

A Map containing every possible key with count 0.

options.text: It mutates the Map into an invalid negative-count state before establishing that an occurrence is available.

items.prompt:
Two approaches test exact multiset equality after first confirming equal total lengths:

items.prompt:

items.prompt: Which invariant precisely describes a Map built from available values and decremented while processing a request prefix?

options.text: For every key x, counts.get(x) equals occurrences of x in the available collection minus occurrences of x consumed from the processed request prefix, and it never becomes negative.
For every key x, counts.get(x) equals all occurrences seen in both collections combined.

options.text: Values from earlier indexes mapped to indexes where those values occurred.
Every value in the array, including the current value and all future values.
Only values already confirmed to belong to a valid pair.

items.prompt:

What should a correct lookup-before-insert algorithm return?

[0, 0], because one occurrence of 3 may be reused.

[1, 1], because the current value is inserted before checking its complement.

items.prompt:

[0, 0], because 4 is its own complement.

items.prompt: A one-pass pair-sum function must return original indexes rather than only true or false. What should each Map entry store?

options.text: A prior value as the key and an original index where it appeared as the associated value.
Only the value, because membership automatically reveals its original index.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The Map value should contain the information required to construct the output.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Derive the stored value type directly from the required return value.

items.prompt:
Two implementations detect a complement among prior values:

A. Uses a Set<number>.
B. Uses a Map<number, number> from value to original index.

The function must return both indexes. Which review is correct?

options.text: Both can detect presence, but only B directly retains the earlier index required for the result.
A Set is sufficient because Set.has returns the index of the matching key.
Only A works because a Map cannot contain duplicate numeric values.
Both representations preserve exactly the same positional information.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Membership and index recovery are different state requirements.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A Set answers whether a partner exists but not where that partner occurred.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Check whether every piece of the returned answer can be reconstructed from the chosen state.

options.text: A valid prior index can be 0, which is falsy, so the pair may be missed.
Map.get cannot return numeric values.
Array indexes begin at one, so zero should never be stored.
The complement must first be converted to a boolean key.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The absence marker undefined and the valid stored value 0 are both falsy.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Use seen.has(complement) to test membership when Map values may legitimately be falsy.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Separate membership testing from retrieval of the associated index.

items.prompt:
The function must return a valid pair whose earlier index is as small as possible.

When the same value appears again before a match is found, which insertion policy better supports that contract?

options.text: Insert the value only if it is not already present, preserving its earliest index.
Always overwrite the stored index with the latest occurrence.
Delete the value whenever a duplicate is observed.
Overwrite the index before lookup so the current position becomes the preferred partner.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Duplicate values offer several possible prior indexes, but the output contract prefers the earliest.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Whether duplicate keys should overwrite depends on which occurrence the result must recover.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Define whether the Map should retain the first, latest, or all indexes for each value.

items.prompt: A function may return any valid pair and needs only one prior index per value. Is overwriting a value's earlier index with a later prior index necessarily incorrect?

options.text: No. It may still produce a valid distinct-index pair, although it changes which pair is returned.
Yes. A Map entry may never be updated after its first insertion.
Yes. Any overwrite automatically pairs the current index with itself.
Yes. Duplicate values require replacing the Map with a Set.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The retained index remains earlier than a future current index and still represents a valid candidate.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Duplicate overwrite policy is part of the output-selection contract, not universally a correctness error.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Separate validity requirements from deterministic or earliest-pair requirements.

items.prompt:
Trace the lookup:

values = [-4, 6, 1, 9]
target = 2

At currentIndex = 1, what happens?

options.text: The current value is 6, its complement is -4, and prior index 0 is found.
The complement is 4, so no pair is found.
The lookup fails because Map keys cannot be negative.
The algorithm must wait until it encounters a positive complement.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The complement calculation is target - current: 2 - 6 = -4.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Complement lookup works with negative keys exactly as it does with positive keys.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Compute the needed prior value algebraically without assuming its sign.

items.prompt:
The input is:

values = [0, 0]
target = 0

How should lookup-before-insert behave?

options.text: The first zero is inserted after its failed lookup; the second zero finds it and returns [0, 1].
The first zero immediately returns [0, 0].
No pair is found because zero cannot be used as a Map key.
The second lookup fails because a stored zero key is treated as absent.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The key is zero, while its associated index is also allowed to be zero.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Map.has correctly distinguishes a stored zero key from absence, and insertion order preserves distinct indexes.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Test both zero keys and zero index values explicitly.

items.prompt:
A Map preserves the first index for each value.

values = [1, 1, 2]
target = 3

What pair is returned when index 2 is processed?

options.text: [0, 2], because complement 1 maps to its preserved first index.
[1, 2], because duplicate values must always overwrite earlier indexes.
[0, 1], because two occurrences of 1 sum to 3.
No pair, because a duplicated complement cannot be stored in a Map.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: At index 2, the needed prior value is 1, and the chosen duplicate policy retains index 0.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The Map can store one selected representative index for repeated values.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Track both the key and the duplicate-retention policy during the trace.

items.prompt: Which invariant most directly explains why lookup-before-insert cannot return the same index twice?

options.text: Before index i is checked, every index stored in seen is strictly smaller than i.
The Map never contains two equal values.
The complement is always numerically different from the current value.
The current index is always greater than zero.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The partner index comes from a completed prefix that excludes the current position.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Distinctness follows from temporal ordering of state, even when the two numeric values are equal.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Express the Map domain in terms of processed index bounds.

items.prompt:
A candidate computes:

const complement = current - target;

Which correction is required for the equation:

prior + current = target?

options.text: Use target - current, because prior must equal target minus the current value.
Use current + target, because both values contribute to the sum.
Use Math.abs(current - target), because complements cannot be negative.
Use target / current, because the two values share the target.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Rearranging prior + current = target gives prior = target - current.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The lookup key must represent the exact earlier value that would complete the target.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Derive the lookup expression algebraically before implementing it.

options.text: [seen.get(complement), currentIndex]
[complement, values[currentIndex]]
[currentIndex, currentIndex]
[seen.get(values[currentIndex]), complement]

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The key identifies the needed prior value, while the associated Map value stores its original index.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Read the output fields from the declared key-value semantics rather than from variable names alone.
feedbackModel.nextAction: State separately what the Map key means and what its associated value means.

items.prompt:
Compare two one-pass implementations:

Solution A:
1. Look up target - current.
2. If absent, insert current.

Solution B:
1. Insert current.
2. Look up target - current.

Which comparison is correct?

options.text: Solution A keeps Map entries limited to prior candidates, preventing self-pairing while still allowing equal values at different indexes.
Solution B is required for duplicates because the current value must be inserted before it can pair with an earlier equal value.
The two orders are always equivalent because Map insertion and lookup are constant-time operations.
Solution A cannot find pairs such as [3, 3] because the second 3 is not inserted before lookup.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: When the second equal value is processed, the first occurrence is already present even though the current occurrence is not.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Lookup-before-insert separates valid duplicate pairing from invalid reuse of the current element.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Test both implementations on a single self-complementing value and on two equal occurrences.

items.prompt: Which property must a grouping key satisfy for the application's equivalence relation?

options.text: Equivalent items must produce the same key, and non-equivalent items must not produce the same key within the supported input domain.
Every input item must produce a different key, including equivalent items.
Items of the same encoded length should produce the same key.
Any key is sufficient because items placed in the same Map bucket are automatically equivalent.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The key defines which items are placed in the same bucket.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A grouping key must represent the equivalence relation itself. Map lookup does not verify that accidentally colliding items are equivalent.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Test both directions: equivalent inputs share a key, and different equivalence classes do not.

items.prompt: Lowercase words should be grouped when they contain exactly the same characters with the same multiplicities, regardless of order. Which key is valid?

options.text: The word's characters sorted into a canonical string.
The number of characters in the word.
The first character of the original word.
The distinct characters with duplicate occurrences removed.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Order is irrelevant, but exact character multiplicity remains relevant.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The canonical key must remove only irrelevant differences. Removing duplicate characters loses required frequency information.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Identify which input differences should disappear and which must remain encoded.

items.prompt:
For lowercase English words, two canonical-key strategies are proposed:

A. Sort every word's characters.
B. Count the 26 letters and serialize the complete count vector.

Which review is correct?

options.text: Both can be valid; B must encode all 26 counts unambiguously so different frequency vectors cannot share a key.
B only needs to record which letters have positive counts.
Only A can be canonical because numeric count vectors cannot represent anagrams.
B can use the sum of all letter counts because words with equal length are equivalent.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: An anagram class is determined by the complete frequency of every supported character.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Different canonical representations may model the same equivalence relation, provided none discard or ambiguously encode required information.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Verify that the serialized vector can be decoded into exactly one count assignment.

items.prompt: Strings should be grouped case-insensitively, but the output must retain each original spelling. Which design matches that contract?

options.text: Use a normalized form such as lowercase as the Map key, but append the original string to the bucket.
Lowercase every string and store only the lowercase result in the output groups.
Use each unchanged original string as the key, because output preservation automatically makes grouping case-insensitive.
Use string length as the key because case changes do not affect length.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The grouping comparison and the returned representation have different requirements.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A canonical key may normalize an item without requiring the original item itself to be replaced in the output.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Store the canonical representation as the key and the required original representation as the bucket member.

items.prompt: Pairs [a, b] and [b, a] should belong to the same group, while other unordered pairs should remain separate. Which key strategy is appropriate?

options.text: Place the two components in a deterministic order, then serialize the ordered pair unambiguously.
Serialize the components in their original order.
Use whichever component appears first as the key.
Use the number of components, which is always two.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Component order is irrelevant to equivalence, but component identities remain relevant.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Canonicalization should remove the irrelevant permutation without collapsing different component combinations.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Normalize symmetric components into one deterministic order before key construction.

items.prompt: Fractions should be grouped by numeric equality, so 1/2, 2/4, and -3/-6 are equivalent. Which key is appropriate?

options.text: Reduce numerator and denominator by their greatest common divisor and normalize the sign to one fixed location.
Use the original numerator and denominator without normalization.
Use numerator + denominator.
Use only the absolute numerator and denominator, ignoring the fraction's sign.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Several syntactic forms represent the same mathematical value.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A canonical representation chooses one normalized form for each equivalence class while preserving distinctions such as positive versus negative values.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Reduce common factors and define one consistent sign convention.

options.text: Different tuples such as ["ab", "c"] and ["a", "bc"] both produce "abc".
String concatenation automatically sorts the characters.
A Map cannot use a concatenated string as a key.
All composite string keys collide regardless of encoding.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The serialized key does not preserve where one component ends and the next begins.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Hash-table collisions are not the only concern. Application-level serialization can make genuinely different inputs produce the exact same key value.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Use an encoding that preserves component boundaries and types.

items.prompt:
Two arbitrary strings must form one composite grouping key.

Solution A:
first + "|" + second

Solution B:
JSON.stringify([first, second])

The input strings may themselves contain "|". Which review is strongest?

options.text: Solution B preserves the two-component structure without relying on a separator that may also occur in the data.
Solution A is always collision-free because adding any separator makes concatenation unambiguous.
Solution B is invalid because array serialization ignores component order.
Both solutions necessarily collapse every pair with the same combined character count.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A delimiter is safe only when its presence in components is impossible or properly escaped.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Composite-key encoding must remain unambiguous for every supported application input, not merely common examples.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Use structured serialization, escaping, or length-prefixed components.

options.text: Different object instances are different Map keys even when their properties contain equal values.
JavaScript Map does not allow objects as keys.
Object property values are deleted when the object is inserted into a Map.
The failure occurs only because each bucket is an array rather than a Set.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The code reconstructs an equal-looking but distinct reference for every observation.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Map object keys use identity, not structural equality. Content-based grouping needs a shared interned object or a canonical primitive representation.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Encode the relevant fields into a stable primitive key or reuse one canonical object identity.

items.prompt:
Which operation correctly adds item to the bucket associated with key?

const groups = new Map<string, Item[]>();

options.text: groups.set(key, [item]);
groups.set(key, [key]);
groups.get(key)?.push(item);

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The operation must preserve prior members and create a bucket when the key is first observed.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Grouping is accumulation. Each equivalent observation extends an existing bucket rather than replacing it.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Implement get-or-create semantics before appending the current item.

options.text: Every equivalent item replaces the previous bucket, so only the final item for each key remains.
Canonical keys cannot be strings or other primitive values.
The original item should be used as the Map key and the canonical form as the bucket value.
There is no bug because a group may contain at most one item.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Map.set replaces the value associated with an existing key.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A grouping bucket must accumulate every member of the equivalence class.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Retrieve the existing bucket, append the item, and only initialize a new bucket when necessary.

items.prompt: Words are grouped as anagrams using only their first character as the key. What is wrong with that representation?

options.text: Non-anagrams such as "cat" and "car" share the key "c" and are incorrectly placed in one group.
Anagrams always begin with different characters, so no valid group can form.
Reading the first character costs more than sorting the complete word.
The key is sufficient because Map automatically separates non-equivalent values inside the same bucket.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The key preserves too little information to distinguish separate equivalence classes.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A compact key is useful only when it remains complete enough to prevent domain-level collisions.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Construct a counterexample pair of non-equivalent items that receives the same proposed key.

options.text: The original index differs for every occurrence, so equivalent items receive different keys and cannot share a bucket.
Indexes are invalid in keys because every numeric index hashes to the same bucket.
The normalized value is unnecessary; the index alone identifies the equivalence class.
The key is too weak because it needs the complete original item and timestamp as well.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The key includes information that is irrelevant to equivalence but unique to each observation.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Canonical keys should retain all equivalence-relevant information and exclude differences that equivalent items are allowed to have.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Remove fields that vary within one intended equivalence class.

items.prompt:
The input contains:

["eat", "eat", "tea"]

The output must preserve every input occurrence while grouping anagrams. What should the shared bucket contain?

options.text: ["eat", "eat", "tea"]
["eat", "tea"] because duplicate group members should be removed.
Only the canonical key, because buckets should not retain original values.
["tea"] because each later equivalent item replaces the earlier one.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The grouping contract preserves occurrences rather than only distinct member values.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A canonical key determines bucket membership; it does not imply deduplication inside the bucket.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Clarify whether the output contains items, distinct items, or counts before choosing the bucket representation.

items.prompt:
Two grouping outputs are considered:

A. Return every original item grouped by canonical key.
B. Return only the number of items in each canonical group.

Which Map value selection is appropriate?

options.text: A should map each key to a bucket of original items; B can map each key to a numeric count.
Both require a Set of canonical keys because the key itself contains every output item.
A needs only a count because original items can always be reconstructed from the canonical key.
B must retain every original item even when only group sizes are returned.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The two outputs require different information to remain associated with each key.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The canonical key determines equivalence, while the Map value should be chosen from the result that must be produced for that class.
feedbackModel.nextAction: For one equivalence class, list exactly what the final output must recover.

items.complexityExplanation: Sorting the m characters of each of n strings costs O(m log m) per string, producing O(nm log m) key-construction time. Retaining all original strings and canonical keys uses O(nm) total character storage in the general case.
items.prompt:
An algorithm groups n strings of length m by sorting the characters of every string to construct its canonical key.

Under the standard expected-cost Map model, what is the dominant time complexity?

options.text: O(nm log m), because key construction sorts m characters for each of n strings.
Expected O(n), because each completed key is inserted into a Map in expected O(1) time.
O(m log m), because all strings share the same grouping Map.
O(n²), because every new group must be compared with every previous group.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The non-constant work occurs before the Map lookup: every item must be converted into its canonical representation.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Expected O(1) Map access does not include sorting, normalization, serialization, or hashing work required to construct a key.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Derive per-item normalization cost and multiply it by the number of items.

items.prompt:
A Map represents active occurrence counts and deletes a key when its count reaches zero.

Which invariant is precise?

options.text: For every key, a present entry stores its exact positive active count, while an absent key represents an active count of zero.
A present entry means only that the key appeared at some time, regardless of its current count.
Every absent key means that its count has not yet been computed.
Zero counts and missing entries may be interpreted differently on each lookup.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The representation keeps only keys with positive active availability.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A keyed-state invariant must define both stored entries and missing keys consistently.
feedbackModel.nextAction: State what presence, absence, and every permitted stored value mean.

items.prompt: A Map stores remaining positive availability and omits exhausted keys. Which sequence correctly consumes one occurrence?

options.text: Look up the count, reject if the key is missing, then decrement it or delete the key when the new count becomes zero.
Write count - 1 first and reject only after the stored value becomes negative.
Delete the key first, then look up how many occurrences had been available.
Check that the key exists but leave its count unchanged after consumption.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Consumption is legal only when availability exists, and successful consumption must change the state by exactly one.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The lifecycle is read, validate, then mutate without creating an invalid intermediate count.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Keep invalid requests from modifying the Map.

options.text: Every repeated occurrence overwrites the previous count with 1 instead of incrementing it.
A newly observed key must begin at count 0.
Map.set cannot update an existing key.
The key must be deleted before every increment.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The stored count should represent all relevant occurrences, not only the latest one.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A missing key begins from zero; an existing key must preserve and extend its prior state.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Update the count as `(counts.get(value) ?? 0) + 1`.

items.prompt:
The Map initially contains:

"a" → 2

The algorithm consumes "a" twice and deletes a key when its count reaches zero. What is the state after each consumption?

options.text: After the first: "a" → 1. After the second: the key "a" is absent.
After the first: "a" → 1. After the second: "a" → 0 remains stored.
The key is deleted after the first consumption because it has been used once.
The count remains 2 after the first consumption and becomes 1 after the second.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Each successful consumption removes one available occurrence, and the representation omits zero-count keys.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Deletion is the final state transition when a positive count is decremented to zero.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Trace the lifecycle as 2 → 1 → absent.

options.text: true and 0, because the key exists with a stored zero value.
false and undefined, because zero removes the key automatically.
false and 0, because Map.has uses truthiness.
true and undefined, because Map.get cannot return zero.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Map membership is independent of whether the associated value is truthy.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A missing key and a key storing zero are distinct Map states unless the application deliberately forbids stored zero counts.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Use Map.has when the distinction between absence and a stored value matters.

options.text: A valid stored result of false enters the branch because false is falsy.
Map.get automatically deletes keys whose value is false.
A Map cannot store boolean values.
A stored true value represents an uncomputed result.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The domain of valid computed results includes false.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Truthiness cannot distinguish cached false from a missing entry.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Use results.has(key) to test whether computation has already occurred.

items.prompt:
A Map is allowed to store undefined as a legitimate computed value.

Which statement is correct?

options.text: Map.get(key) alone cannot distinguish a missing key from a key storing undefined, but Map.has(key) can.
Map refuses to store undefined values.
Map.get returns a separate built-in missing sentinel when the key is absent.
Map.has returns false whenever the stored value is undefined.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Both absence and a stored undefined value produce undefined from Map.get.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: When undefined is part of the value domain, membership must be checked separately or a different sentinel must be used.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Define whether undefined means a computed value or absence, and choose the lookup protocol accordingly.

items.prompt:
A stored value may legitimately be 0 or false. Compare:

A. state.get(key) || fallback
B. state.get(key) ?? fallback

Which review is correct?

options.text: B preserves stored 0 and false, while A replaces them because both are falsy.
Both expressions use fallback only when the key is missing.
A preserves false but replaces only zero.
B fully distinguishes a missing key from a key explicitly storing undefined.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Logical OR reacts to all falsy values, while nullish coalescing reacts only to null or undefined.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Use a defaulting operation whose missing-value semantics match the permitted stored-value domain.
feedbackModel.nextAction: List all legitimate stored values before selecting `||`, `??`, or `Map.has`.

options.text: An unavailable request writes a negative count before it is rejected, violating the state invariant.
The code is wrong because exhausted availability should be represented by -1.
Every request should reduce availability by two.
A missing key must always default to one available occurrence.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The representation promises nonnegative or positive counts, but the code temporarily stores an impossible value.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Validate availability before mutation so failed operations leave keyed state unchanged.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Read the current count, reject missing or zero availability, and only then write the decremented state.

items.prompt:
A positive-count Map starts empty and processes:

1. add "k"
2. add "k"
3. consume "k"
4. consume "k"

Zero-count keys are deleted. Which sequence describes the state of "k"?

options.text: 1 → 2 → 1 → absent
1 → 1 → absent → absent

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Adds increase multiplicity, consumes decrease it, and the final transition removes the exhausted entry.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A count has a complete lifecycle from absence to positive state and back to absence.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Apply exactly one state transition for each add or consume event.

items.prompt:
A Map is intended to contain only keys with positive remaining counts. After decrementing a count from 1 to 0, the implementation stores key → 0.

Why can this break later reasoning?

options.text: Map.has(key) and Map.size no longer describe keys with positive availability, contradicting the intended invariant.
JavaScript Map throws when zero is used as a value.
Setting zero creates two entries for the same key.
The numeric value zero changes the key into boolean false.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The chosen representation equates key presence with positive active state.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Zero-count deletion is not merely cleanup when membership and size are used as semantic shortcuts.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Delete the entry when its count becomes zero or redefine all dependent checks consistently.

items.prompt:
Two designs represent exhausted counts:

A. Retain key → 0 and test count > 0.
B. Delete the key and treat absence as zero.

Which review is correct?

options.text: Both can be correct, but membership, size, and update logic must follow the chosen representation consistently.
Only A is valid because deleting a key always loses required history.
Only B is valid because Maps are never allowed to store zero.
The implementation may switch between both meanings without changing any checks.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The logical count can be represented by an explicit zero or by absence.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Representation choices are flexible, but the invariant cannot change opportunistically between operations.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Document one zero-state policy and derive membership and size checks from it.

items.prompt:
A cache computation may legitimately return false, 0, or undefined. The cache must distinguish each computed result from "not computed yet."

Which design is strongest?

options.text: Use Map.has(key) to determine whether computation occurred, then use Map.get(key) to retrieve the stored result.
Treat any truthy result as computed and every falsy result as uncomputed.
Use Map.get(key) === undefined as the complete test, even though undefined is a valid result.
Convert every computed result to boolean so all value distinctions disappear.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Every ordinary falsy value belongs to the valid result domain.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Computation status is membership metadata, while the stored value is the computation result.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Separate whether a result exists from what that result equals.

items.prompt:
A Map is initialized from available items, stores only positive remaining counts, and is updated after each successfully processed request.

Which invariant should hold after every request prefix?

options.text: For every key, its stored value equals initial supply minus successfully consumed requests; keys with zero remaining supply are absent, and no stored count is zero or negative.
Every stored count remains equal to the original supply regardless of consumption.
Every stored value equals the number of requests observed for that key, including rejected requests.
Every missing key means that its initial supply has not yet been calculated.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The state represents remaining consumable supply after the successfully processed prefix.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A stable invariant links initialization, successful updates, deletion at zero, and the meaning of absence.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Express current state as initial keyed state minus committed operations.

items.prompt: Which statement about JavaScript Map keys is precise?

options.text: Any JavaScript value can be a Map key; primitive keys use SameValueZero equality, while objects and functions are matched by reference identity.
Map keys must be strings, and every other key is converted to a string.
Object and array keys are compared structurally by their properties and elements.
Primitive keys are compared by reference identity, while objects are compared by value.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Map accepts primitive and reference values without coercing all keys into strings.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Primitive equality and object identity use different runtime semantics. Equal-looking objects are not automatically the same key.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Classify the proposed key as a primitive value or a reference value before predicting lookup behavior.

options.text: 3, because 1, "1", and true are distinct Map keys.
1, because every primitive key is converted to the string "1".
2, because 1 and "1" are equal after coercion.
The code throws because a Map cannot mix key types.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Map does not apply loose equality or string coercion when comparing keys.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Numeric, string, and boolean values remain distinct keys even when their textual representations resemble one another.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Compare both the primitive type and value of each key.

options.text: 2, because NaN matches NaN in Map key equality, and 0 and -0 are treated as the same key.
4, because every insertion expression creates a distinct key.
3, because NaN keys match but 0 and -0 remain separate.
The code throws because NaN cannot be used as a Map key.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: JavaScript Map uses SameValueZero rather than === for numeric key comparison.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Under SameValueZero, NaN equals NaN for key matching, and positive and negative zero share one key.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Account explicitly for NaN and signed zero when numeric edge cases affect key identity.

options.text: undefined, because the lookup uses a different object reference even though its property values are equal.
"stored", because Map compares object properties structurally.
It throws because two objects cannot have the same property structure.
"stored", because Map automatically serializes object keys.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Each object literal creates a new reference.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Equal property contents do not imply identical object keys in JavaScript or TypeScript.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Check whether insertion and lookup reuse the exact same object instance.

options.text: "stored", because insertion and lookup use the same object reference.
undefined, because objects can never be retrieved from a Map.
undefined, because Map clones object keys during insertion.
It throws because mutable objects cannot be used as keys.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The exact reference stored as the key is reused for lookup.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Object keys are valid and stable as long as callers retain and reuse the same identity.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Track reference identity separately from the contents stored inside the referenced object.

options.text: At runtime, both tuples are separate array objects, and Map compares array keys by reference identity.
Readonly tuples can be inserted but cannot be retrieved.
The string and number elements cannot appear in one tuple key.
Map ignores all tuple elements and compares only tuple length.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: TypeScript tuple syntax changes static typing but not JavaScript's runtime array identity semantics.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A tuple type does not create structural key equality. Fresh tuple literals remain distinct references.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Use a canonical primitive encoding, a nested Map, or a reused tuple reference.

items.prompt:
State is indexed by an ordered pair consisting of:

- a string category,
- a numeric level.

Which representation avoids string encoding while preserving both component identities?

options.text: Map<string, Map<number, State>>, using the category for the outer lookup and the level for the inner lookup.
Map<string, State> with category + level as the key.
Map<[string, number], State> using a newly created tuple for every lookup.
A Set containing the category and level as separate entries.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Each component already has a valid primitive key type and can be looked up independently.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Nested Maps represent composite keys without flattening component types into an ambiguous string.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Use one Map level per composite-key component when structured lookup is clearer than serialization.

options.text: ["ab", "c"] and ["a", "bc"], because both produce "abc".
["a", "b"] and ["a", "b"], because equivalent inputs must not share a key.
["a", "b"] and ["b", "a"], because concatenation always sorts components.
["", "a"] and ["", "b"], because all empty first components produce the same key.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The encoding removes information about where the first component ends.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Different composite values can become the exact same primitive key before Map hashing is even involved.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Use structured serialization, escaping, length prefixes, or nested Maps.

options.text: Solution B preserves the two string boundaries and escapes their contents, while Solution A is ambiguous unless the delimiter is escaped or forbidden.
Solution A is always collision-safe because a visible separator cannot occur inside a string component.
Solution B is invalid because JSON array serialization sorts the two strings.
Both fail because string Map keys are compared by reference identity.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The delimiter belongs to the supported component alphabet and is not escaped.
