items.prompt:
A reviewer says:

"Three-sum is O(n) after sorting because left and right each move only forward or backward once."

Why is that reasoning incomplete?

options.text: The O(n) movement bound applies to one anchor's scan, and a new scan begins for O(n) different anchors.
The pointers are not monotonic within an anchor scan.
Sorting adds another factor of n, making the algorithm O(n³).
Any algorithm using three indexes is automatically O(n³).

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The pointer lifecycle restarts inside an outer loop.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A traversal can be linear per invocation while the complete algorithm remains quadratic because that traversal is repeated.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Identify the scope over which each movement bound holds.

items.complexityExplanation: The transformation uses a constant number of indexes and overwrites the input array. Excluding the returned logical length and the input array itself, it uses O(1) auxiliary space.
items.prompt: A read/write two-pointer transformation overwrites the input array and returns the new logical length. It allocates no second array. What is its auxiliary-space complexity?

options.text: O(1), because it uses only a constant amount of extra state.
O(n), because the input array contains n elements.
O(n²), because read and write may point to different positions.
O(log n), because the written prefix grows gradually.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The algorithm reuses input storage and retains only a few scalar variables.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Auxiliary space excludes storage already occupied by the input unless the contract says otherwise.
feedbackModel.nextAction: List only newly allocated storage whose size grows with input size.

items.prompt:
A solution sorts the input with a library sort and then uses O(1) pointer variables.

A reviewer states:

"Auxiliary space is definitely O(1)."

What is the most precise response?

options.text: The pointer scan uses O(1) extra space, but the complete bound also depends on the sorting algorithm's auxiliary-space behavior.
Any sorting call always requires O(n) auxiliary space.
The claim is unconditional because sorting never allocates memory.
Only the number of pointer variables matters for the complete algorithm.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The scan and the sort may have different auxiliary-space requirements.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: An in-place pointer phase does not prove that every preceding operation is also in-place.
feedbackModel.nextAction: State whether the sorting implementation is in-place or include its documented auxiliary-space cost.

items.complexityExplanation: The scan performs O(n) pointer work, while materializing r result records requires Θ(r) additional time and output storage.
items.prompt:
A monotonic pointer scan processes n elements and emits r constant-size result records.

What is the appropriate complexity when the output list is included?

options.text: O(n + r) time and O(r) output space, excluding any other auxiliary state.
O(n) time and O(1) space regardless of how many records are returned.
O(nr) time because every output record must be multiplied by the scan length.
O(r) time because pointer movement is free.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Every materialized result contributes unavoidable construction and storage cost.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A linear scan does not hide an output that may itself grow with the input.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Introduce an output-size variable whenever the algorithm may emit multiple results.

options.text: Each slice copies the range, so total time and output space must include the sum of all copied lengths.
Nothing; Array.prototype.slice is O(1) regardless of range length.
Copying changes the pointer scan itself to O(log n).
Only the number of returned ranges matters; their lengths do not affect copying cost.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The algorithm creates new arrays whose sizes depend on the selected ranges.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Tracking range boundaries can be O(1) per result, but copying complete ranges is proportional to their lengths.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Sum the lengths of all copied outputs rather than counting only pointer iterations.

items.expectedSpaceComplexity: O(s) excluding output, where s is the auxiliary space used by sorting
items.complexityExplanation: The duplicate-skipping loops advance the same monotonic pointers used by the scan. Their total movement per anchor remains O(n), so the scan phase remains O(n²). The complete auxiliary-space bound also includes the sorting algorithm's O(s) space.
items.prompt:
In sorted three-sum, after finding a result, the code skips repeated left and right values with additional while loops.

How do these loops affect the standard O(n²) time bound?

options.text: The bound remains O(n²), because duplicate skipping only advances the same monotonic pointers within each anchor scan.
The bound becomes O(n³) because every additional while loop adds another multiplicative factor of n.
The bound becomes O(n) because duplicates reduce the number of unique values.
The bound becomes O(n log n) because equal runs are skipped.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Skip loops consume positions that the pointers would otherwise traverse later.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Syntactically separate loops do not multiply complexity when their combined movement shares one bounded monotonic traversal.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Charge each pointer advance once within the enclosing anchor scan.

items.prompt:
A reviewer says:

"Skipping duplicates is O(1) because all skipped values are equal."

What is the precise correction?

options.text: A single duplicate run can contain O(n) values, although total monotonic skipping may still fit within the scan's O(n) movement bound.
The claim is correct because comparing equal values has no cost.
Every duplicate-skipping loop is O(n²).
Skipping duplicates affects space complexity but never time complexity.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Equality of values does not limit how many array positions the pointer may cross.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A loop can be linear in isolation yet add no new asymptotic factor when its movement is part of an already bounded traversal.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Separate the cost of one skip loop from the aggregate cost across the complete scan.

items.prompt:
Compare these strategies on n values:

A. Pair search with two nested loops.
B. Sort, then perform one two-pointer pair scan.
C. Sort, then for every anchor perform a two-pointer suffix scan for three-sum.

Which comparison is correct?

options.text: A is O(n²), B is O(n log n), and C is O(n²).
A is O(n²), while B and C are both O(n) because they use two pointers.
A is O(n³), while B and C are O(n log n).
All three are O(n²) because each considers combinations of values.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The pair scan occurs once in B but is repeated for every anchor in C.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The same two-pointer subroutine can contribute different total costs depending on its enclosing control structure.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Count how many times each scan phase is invoked.

items.prompt: Which explanation provides the strongest justification that a pointer-based scan is O(n)?

options.text: Each pointer moves only in one direction, never resets, and can cross at most n positions, so the combined number of advances is O(n).
The code contains one while loop, so it must be O(n).
The variables are named left and right, which guarantees linear time.
The algorithm uses O(1) auxiliary space, so its running time is O(n).

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The explanation gives a global upper bound on all pointer movement.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Big-O follows from bounded work, not from variable names, loop count, or space usage.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Prove how often each pointer can cross an input position over the complete execution.

items.prompt: Which problem property most strongly suggests a sliding-window strategy?

options.text: The candidate is one contiguous interval, and its state can be updated when an element enters or leaves.
Only two independently selected values determine the candidate.
Many unrelated range queries must be answered after preprocessing.
The result may freely skip positions inside its first and last selected indexes.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A window represents every element in one active contiguous interval whose boundaries move through the input.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Two boundaries alone do not define a sliding window; the complete interval and its maintained state must form the current candidate.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Identify whether every position between the boundaries belongs to the current candidate.

items.prompt: A task asks for the maximum sum among every contiguous block of exactly k elements. Which signal is present?

options.text: Each candidate is a complete length-k interval, and consecutive candidates differ by one outgoing and one incoming element.
Only the first and last values of the array should be compared as a pair.
The task consists of unrelated range queries supplied independently.
Any k values may be selected while skipping positions.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The candidate length is fixed, and adjacent candidates overlap in k - 1 positions.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Fixed-size windows should usually reuse state rather than recompute each complete block.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Name the value leaving and the value entering when the interval shifts once.

items.prompt:
Compare two tasks:

A. Find the longest contiguous segment satisfying a condition while moving through the array.
B. Answer thousands of independent sum queries for arbitrary ranges.

Which classification is correct?

options.text: A suggests a sliding window; B suggests prefix sums.
A suggests prefix sums; B suggests a sliding window.
Both are sliding-window problems because both involve ranges.
Both are pair-oriented two-pointer problems because each range has two boundaries.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A evolves one active candidate interval; B evaluates unrelated requested intervals from precomputed cumulative state.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Sliding windows traverse overlapping candidates, while prefix sums provide direct aggregate access for arbitrary ranges.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Ask whether the boundaries evolve as one scan or arrive as independent queries.

items.prompt:
Compare two candidate definitions:

A. Every value between left and right contributes to the maintained condition.
B. Only values[left] and values[right] form the current candidate.

Which mapping is correct?

options.text: A describes a sliding window; B describes pair-oriented two pointers.
Both describe sliding windows because both use left and right.
Both describe endpoint-pair searches because each candidate has two boundaries.
A describes prefix sums; B describes binary search.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A evaluates the complete contiguous interior, while B evaluates two selected positions.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Classify the semantic candidate rather than the names or number of pointer variables.
feedbackModel.nextAction: List which input elements participate in evaluating one current candidate.

items.prompt: A property must be checked for every contiguous subarray, but a failed interval gives no information about whether moving left or right preserves possible answers. What is the best assessment?

options.text: A standard variable-size sliding window is not justified; enumeration, prefix-based state, or another problem-specific method may be required.
Move left after every failure because every interval problem supports shrinking.
Move both boundaries after each check so every interval is eventually covered.
The task is automatically a sliding-window problem because the candidates are contiguous.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: No comparison supplies a safe rule for permanently discarding intervals associated with one boundary.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Contiguity is necessary for a window candidate but does not by itself justify variable-size boundary movement.
feedbackModel.nextAction: State what becomes impossible after each proposed boundary movement.

items.prompt:
A task asks for the longest subsequence satisfying a condition. Selected values may skip arbitrary positions.

A reviewer proposes a sliding window because the result has a first and last selected index.

What is wrong?

options.text: A sliding window includes every position between its boundaries, while a subsequence may omit interior positions.
A subsequence does not preserve the original order of selected values.
The only issue is that sliding windows require sorted arrays.
The proposal is correct because any selection has a first and last index.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Interior membership is optional for the requested selection.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A pair of outer indexes does not turn a non-contiguous selection into a window.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Check whether every index between the candidate boundaries must be selected.

items.prompt:
A candidate wants to find a contiguous subarray whose sum equals target.

The array may contain positive and negative values. They propose:

- expand right when the sum is too small,
- shrink left when the sum is too large.

What is the central flaw?

options.text: With mixed signs, expanding may decrease the sum and shrinking may increase it, so the comparison does not justify either movement.
An exact-sum problem can never involve contiguous subarrays.
The input must be filtered so only positive values remain.
The strategy becomes valid by moving both boundaries after every comparison.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The maintained sum is not monotonic under expansion or contraction.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: An ordinary target-sum window relies on value-domain constraints; mixed-sign exact sums commonly require prefix-sum state instead.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Test whether adding or removing one permitted value always moves the sum in a predictable direction.

items.prompt:
A task asks for the shortest contiguous subarray with sum at least target.

The input may contain negative values. A solution shrinks left whenever the current sum reaches target.

Why is the ordinary window rule unsafe?

options.text: Removing a left value may increase or decrease the sum depending on its sign, so the usual shrink invariant does not hold.
Shortest-range problems can never use sliding windows.
The method is valid only when target is negative.
The only correction is to move right backward after every valid interval.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The effect of removing the outgoing value is not directionally predictable.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Shortest-window contraction is valid only when shrinking has a known effect on the maintained constraint.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Prove that removing the leftmost permitted value changes the state monotonically.

items.prompt: Which constraint makes a variable-size sum window especially plausible for finding the longest contiguous subarray with sum at most target?

options.text: All values are nonnegative, so expanding cannot decrease the sum and shrinking cannot increase it.
Values may have arbitrary signs, so every movement changes the sum unpredictably.
The array is unsorted, which alone guarantees safe window movement.
The target is even, so boundary movement is monotonic.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The value domain makes the sum respond predictably to adding and removing elements.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Sliding-window validity often depends on input constraints that create monotonic boundary effects.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Describe how the maintained sum changes under each possible boundary movement.

items.prompt:
A solution computes the sum of every length-k contiguous block by looping through all k elements again for each starting index.

What sliding-window opportunity was missed?

options.text: Reuse the previous sum by subtracting the outgoing value and adding the incoming value.
Sort every length-k block before computing its sum.
Compare only the first and last values of each block.
Skip interior values because only the boundaries affect a window sum.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Consecutive fixed-size windows overlap except for one departing and one arriving value.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A rolling window carries forward the aggregate instead of rebuilding it from the overlapping contents.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Write the next state as previous state minus outgoing plus incoming.

items.prompt:
A reviewer says:

"The code uses variables named left and right, so it is a sliding-window algorithm."

What is the best correction?

options.text: It is a sliding window only if left and right delimit one active contiguous candidate whose state is maintained as the boundaries move.
It becomes a sliding window only after renaming the variables start and end.
The statement is correct because every two-pointer algorithm is a sliding window.
Sliding windows require right to move from the end toward the beginning.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Variable names reveal no invariant about the interval between them.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A window is defined by candidate membership and maintained state, not surface syntax.
feedbackModel.nextAction: State what the interval contains and whether its complete contents form the candidate.

items.prompt: Which property makes a contiguous-range condition suitable for efficient sliding-window maintenance?

options.text: The relevant state can be updated when the right element enters and when the left element leaves.
Every boundary movement requires rebuilding all information from the complete interval.
Only the two boundary values are meaningful, and the interior never participates.
The candidate may omit arbitrary interior values while retaining the same boundaries.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Window movement changes membership locally at its boundaries.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The pattern is strongest when candidate state can be synchronized with those local membership changes.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Define explicit add-incoming and remove-outgoing operations for the maintained state.

items.prompt:
An array contains mixed positive and negative values.

A. Find the maximum sum among blocks of exactly k consecutive elements.
B. Find a variable-length block whose sum reaches a target by expanding when low and shrinking when high.

Which assessment is correct?

options.text: A still supports a fixed-size rolling window; B is not justified without a monotonic movement argument.
Neither can use a window because any negative value invalidates all sliding-window techniques.
Both are valid sliding windows because both candidates are contiguous.
B is valid because it compares the sum with a target, while A requires full rescanning.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A has a predetermined one-step shift, while B needs the sum comparison to justify an irreversible boundary choice.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Mixed signs break many variable-size sum rules but do not prevent rolling state across fixed-size windows.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Separate scheduled fixed-size movement from comparison-driven variable-size movement.

items.prompt:
Match each task to the strongest classification:

A. Maintain a frequency map for one contiguous active substring.
B. Answer independent immutable range-sum queries.
C. Compare two selected sorted endpoint values.
D. Choose elements while freely skipping indexes.
E. Compute every complete length-k block by rolling one incoming and one outgoing contribution.
F. Use sum-based expansion and contraction on mixed-sign input without a monotonic proof.

Which mapping is correct?

options.text: A: variable sliding window; B: prefix sums; C: pair-oriented two pointers; D: non-window selection; E: fixed-size sliding window; F: invalid window rule.
All six are sliding-window tasks because each may refer to indexes or ranges.
A: prefix sums; B: sliding window; C: sliding window; D: fixed window; E: pair search; F: valid variable window.
The classifications are interchangeable whenever an implementation can be made linear.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Each task differs in candidate membership, maintained state, query workload, or legality of boundary movement.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Sliding-window recognition requires both a contiguous active candidate and a correct boundary-update model.
feedbackModel.nextAction: For each task, identify candidate membership, state ownership, and the proof supporting its next boundary movement.

items.prompt:
A window uses inclusive boundaries:

[left, right]

If left = 2 and right = 5, which indexes belong to the window?

options.text: Only 2 and 5.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Both endpoints are included under the inclusive convention.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: An inclusive window contains every index from left through right.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Enumerate the concrete indexes represented by the interval before deriving formulas.

items.prompt:
A window contains indexes [left, right] inclusively.

Which formula gives its length?

options.text: right - left + 1
right - left
right + left
right - left - 1

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The interval includes both its first and final index.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The difference measures gaps between indexes; the additional one counts the starting position.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Test the formula on a one-element window where left === right.

items.prompt:
A window uses the half-open convention:

[left, right)

If left = 2 and right = 5, which indexes belong to the window?

options.text: Only 3 and 4.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: left is included, while right is the first position outside the window.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A half-open interval stops immediately before its right boundary.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Translate [left, right) into indexes from left through right - 1.

items.prompt:
A window contains indexes [left, right).

Which formula gives its length?

options.text: right - left
right - left + 1
right - left - 1
right + 1

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The exclusive right boundary is also the position immediately after the final contained element.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Half-open intervals encode their length directly as end minus start.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Check that [k, k) correctly produces length zero.

items.prompt:
A window contains only the element at index 4.

Which representations are correct?

options.text: Inclusive: [4, 4]. Half-open: [4, 5).
Inclusive: [4, 5]. Half-open: [4, 4).
Both conventions use [4, 4].
Both conventions use [4, 5].

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: An inclusive singleton has equal endpoints, while a half-open singleton has boundaries one position apart.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The same logical window has different endpoint values under different boundary conventions.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Apply each convention's length formula and confirm that the result is one.

items.prompt: Which statement correctly compares a canonical empty window under inclusive and half-open conventions?

options.text: An inclusive empty window may have left > right, while a half-open empty window is represented by left === right.
left === right represents an empty window under both conventions.
Both conventions require left > right for an empty window.
Neither convention can represent an empty window.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Equal inclusive endpoints contain one element, while equal half-open boundaries contain none.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Emptiness must be derived from the interval convention rather than from pointer equality alone.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Compute the length implied by each convention when left === right.

items.prompt:
An algorithm declares that its current window is inclusive:

[left, right]

but computes:

const length = right - left;

What is wrong?

options.text: The formula omits one contained position, so a singleton window incorrectly has length zero.
The formula overcounts the window by one.
Inclusive windows cannot use an arithmetic length formula.
The only valid correction is to reinterpret right as exclusive without changing any other logic.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Both boundary positions belong to the represented window.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: An inclusive interval requires right - left + 1.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Test the formula when both boundaries point to the same element.

items.prompt:
An algorithm declares that its current window is half-open:

[left, right)

but computes:

const length = right - left + 1;

What is wrong?

options.text: The formula counts the excluded right boundary as though it were contained in the window.
The formula fails to count the included left boundary.
The correct formula is right - left - 1.
The formula is correct because all windows include both boundaries.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: right identifies the first position outside the logical window.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The half-open length is exactly right - left.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Check that an empty interval [k, k) produces length zero.

items.prompt:
An inclusive window currently represents [left, right].

The algorithm expands by incrementing right. Which update sequence keeps a running sum synchronized?

options.text: Increment right, then add values[right] to the sum.
Add values[right], then increment right without adding the new element.
Increment right, then subtract values[right].
Increment right without changing the sum.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: After the increment, the new right index becomes part of the inclusive window.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The incoming contribution must match the element newly included by the boundary movement.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Identify the membership difference between the old and new intervals.

items.prompt:
A half-open window represents [left, right), so values[right] is currently outside the window.

Which expansion sequence is coherent?

options.text: Add values[right] to the maintained state, then increment right.
Increment right, then add values[right], skipping the element that actually entered.
Remove values[right], then increment right.
Increment right without adding any incoming contribution.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The old exclusive boundary identifies the next element that will enter.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: For [left, right), add the element at right before moving the exclusive boundary past it.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Compare the old interval [left, right) with the expanded interval [left, right + 1).

items.prompt: Under either inclusive or half-open conventions, which sequence correctly shrinks a non-empty window from the left?

options.text: Remove values[left] from the maintained state, then increment left.
Increment left, then remove values[left].
Remove the rightmost element, then increment left.
Increment left without updating the maintained state.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The outgoing element is located at the old left boundary.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: State mutation must describe the membership change caused by the boundary update.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Remove the element before changing the index that identifies it.

items.prompt:
The boundaries describe the inclusive window [2, 4], but the maintained sum still includes values[1].

What is the correct review?

options.text: The sum and boundaries describe different element sets, so validity and answer decisions based on that sum are unreliable.
The state may include any processed prefix as long as left and right are correct.
The boundaries automatically remove values[1] from the numeric sum.
The mismatch can affect only the length formula, never validity.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The aggregate contains an element outside the represented interval.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Window-local state must describe exactly the elements currently between the boundaries.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Audit every boundary movement for a matching incoming or outgoing state update.

items.prompt:
The boundaries describe [left, right] inclusively, but the frequency map has not yet counted values[right].

What invariant is broken?

options.text: The map describes [left, right) while the boundaries describe [left, right], so duplicate or distinct-count checks use stale state.
No invariant is broken because right is always excluded.
A frequency map needs to track only values[left].
Computing right - left + 1 automatically inserts values[right] into the map.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The endpoint convention includes right, but the state update does not.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Boundary semantics and state membership must use the same convention at every observation point.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Add the incoming endpoint before evaluating window validity.

items.prompt:
A reviewer says:

"left and right are independent candidates, so only values[left] and values[right] belong to the current sliding window."

What is the best correction?

options.text: A sliding window is one contiguous region, so every index between its boundaries belongs according to the chosen interval convention.
The statement is correct because window state uses only endpoint values.
Interior elements belong only when the input is sorted.
Sliding-window boundaries do not define any collection of elements.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The candidate evaluated by a sliding window is the complete contiguous interval.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Window boundaries jointly define membership; they are not independent selected positions.
feedbackModel.nextAction: List every index whose contribution must be represented by the current state.

options.text: The update logic represents [left, right), but the length formula treats right as included and overcounts by one.
The only issue is that right must remain fixed.
The incoming contribution should always be values[left].
The formula is correct because exclusive boundaries require +1.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The membership updates and length calculation assume different meanings for right.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Choose one interval convention and derive all accesses, updates, lengths, and termination rules from it.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Document the interval notation and verify every formula against it.

items.prompt: Which invariant most completely supports a sliding-window implementation?

options.text: The chosen boundary convention defines exactly which contiguous indexes belong to the window; the length formula follows that convention; and every state entry or aggregate equals the contribution of exactly those contained elements.
left and right may describe unrelated positions as long as both move forward.
The maintained state may include any previously processed element even after it leaves the window.
right - left + 1 is valid regardless of whether right is inclusive or exclusive.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Correctness requires one coherent definition of membership shared by boundaries, state, length, and updates.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A window is not correct when its pointers, aggregate, and formulas each describe different regions.
feedbackModel.nextAction: After every expansion or contraction, verify the represented indexes, computed length, and maintained state together.

items.prompt:
A sliding-window algorithm must evaluate contiguous windows of exactly k elements.

When is the current inclusive window [left, right] complete?

options.text: When right - left + 1 === k.
Whenever right - left + 1 <= k.
Whenever right === k.
Only while left === 0.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The fixed-size contract requires exactly k included positions.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A growing prefix smaller than k is initialization state, not a valid complete window.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Derive the current width from the declared boundary convention before recording a result.

items.prompt: Which sequence correctly initializes a maximum-sum scan over windows of width k?

options.text: Accumulate the first k elements, then use that complete sum to initialize the best result.
Treat every prefix sum before k elements are present as a candidate window.
Begin comparison only after adding element k, so the first width-k window is never evaluated.
Initialize the best sum to 0 and avoid computing the first complete window.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The first valid candidate consists of indexes 0 through k - 1.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Best-state initialization should come from a real complete candidate, especially when values may be negative.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Establish one valid width-k window before comparing later candidates.

items.prompt:
The current width-k window covers indexes [left, right].

To slide it one position right, which sum update is correct?

options.text: Subtract values[left], increment both boundaries, and add the new values[right].
Increment right and add values[right] without removing values[left].
Increment left first and subtract the value at the new left boundary.
Replace the sum with the sum of all values before left.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Exactly one old element leaves and exactly one new element enters during a one-position slide.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Rolling state must remove the outgoing contribution and add the incoming contribution exactly once.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Name the element leaving the old interval and the element entering the new interval.

items.prompt:
A one-pass implementation adds values[right] on every iteration.

Once right >= k, which element must be removed so the maintained sum represents the latest width-k window?

options.text: values[right - k], because that is the element immediately before the new width-k window.
values[right - k + 1], because it is the first element that should remain in the window.
values[right], because every incoming element must be removed immediately.
values[0], regardless of how far the window has moved.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: After adding index right, the desired window begins at right - k + 1, so index right - k has just expired.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Evict the element outside the new window, not the new leftmost element that must remain.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Write the exact inclusive indexes of the new width-k window.

items.prompt:
A width-3 window currently covers indexes [2, 4].

To slide to [3, 5], the code subtracts values[3] and adds values[5].

What is wrong?

options.text: values[3] remains inside the new window; the outgoing contribution is values[2].
values[5] is not part of the new window.
Both values[2] and values[3] should be subtracted.
Sliding a fixed-size window never removes an old value.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The overlap between the old and new windows contains indexes 3 and 4.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Only the old left boundary exits during a one-position fixed-window slide.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Compare the old and new index sets and remove only their difference.

items.prompt:
A loop adds values[right] to windowSum and immediately compares windowSum with bestSum.

Only afterward, when the width exceeds k, it removes values[left] and increments left.

What can happen?

options.text: The comparison may evaluate a k + 1 element sum as though it were a valid width-k candidate.
The first complete width-k window can never be evaluated.
The maintained sum always represents k - 1 elements.
The result remains correct; only auxiliary space changes.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The state is evaluated while it still includes both the expired and incoming elements.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The current valid interval is itself a candidate for the maximum.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: For longest at-most problems, shrink repairs invalidity rather than minimizes a valid window.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Stop contraction at the first valid left boundary for the current right position.

items.prompt: Which statement most completely explains validity and answer timing in a variable-size sliding window?

options.text: State must exactly represent [left, right]; incoming and outgoing updates must precede validity decisions that depend on them; longest valid windows are recorded after invalidity is repaired, while shortest valid windows are recorded during every valid contraction before validity is lost.
Every expanded interval should update both the longest and shortest answers before any state checks.
At most one left movement is needed after each right movement, regardless of the constraint.
If left and right remain inside the input, the current interval is automatically a valid answer.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Correctness requires synchronized state, a live validity predicate, complete restoration or contraction, and objective-specific answer placement.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Sliding-window answer updates are consequences of the invariant and optimization contract, not a fixed line that can be reused in every template.
feedbackModel.nextAction: For each algorithm, mark when state becomes current, when validity is guaranteed, and which valid candidates the objective requires recording.

items.prompt: A sliding-window problem compares the current window with a target frequency requirement. What should the two frequency maps represent?

options.text: The target map stores fixed required counts, while the window map stores counts only for the current active window.
Both maps store frequencies from the complete input.
Both maps store the target counts and never change.
The target map stores counts, while the window map stores only character indexes.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: One structure defines the immutable contract; the other mirrors the changing contents of the active window.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Target requirements and current-window observations have different lifecycles and should not be conflated.
feedbackModel.nextAction: State which map changes when an element enters or leaves the window.

items.prompt: A window must contain two occurrences of `a` and one occurrence of `b`. Why is a Set insufficient for checking the requirement?

options.text: A Set records only whether a value is present, so it cannot distinguish one `a` from two `a` values.
A Set cannot store string values.
A Set is insufficient only because its values are not sorted.
A Set always requires asymptotically more space than a frequency map.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Membership state answers whether a key exists; frequency state answers how many occurrences exist.

items.prompt:
A candidate says these strings match because they contain the same Set of characters:

What is wrong?

Nothing; equal Sets prove equal character multiplicities.

items.prompt: When values[right] enters the active window, how should its window frequency be updated?

options.text: Set its count to the previous window count plus one.
Set its count to one even if the same value is already inside the window.
Decrease its count because right moved forward.
Increase the required count in the target map instead.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Boundary movement must be reflected by an equivalent change in the state representing the window.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Pair every right-boundary expansion with one increment for the entering key.

items.prompt:
A window maintains both a frequency map and a distinctCount.

When should distinctCount increase after an incoming value is added?

options.text: Only when that value's window count changes from 0 to 1.
Every time any occurrence enters the window.
Only when the count changes from 1 to 2.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A key becomes newly distinct only when its first active occurrence enters.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Distinct count measures positive-frequency keys, not total observations.

items.prompt:

Leave distinctCount unchanged because the key once appeared in the window.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Distinct state must describe the current interval, not historical membership.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Update distinctCount only on transitions between absent and present states.

items.prompt:
A window frequency map is used like this:

items.prompt:

items.prompt: A sliding-window implementation increments frequencies for incoming values but never decrements frequencies for outgoing values. What does the map eventually represent?

options.text: It represents frequencies from the processed prefix or accumulated history, not frequencies from the current window.
It still represents the current window because left moves forward.

items.prompt:

items.prompt: Which invariant most completely supports a sliding window using frequency maps, distinct counts, and required/formed state?

options.text: The window map contains exactly the positive frequencies inside the current boundaries; zero-count keys are absent when map size represents distinctness; distinctCount equals the number of positive-frequency window keys; required counts target keys; and formed counts exactly the target keys whose current frequencies meet their requirements.

items.prompt:

items.prompt:

items.prompt: A shortest-window problem has a condition that remains satisfied while redundant values are removed from the left. Which inner-loop structure is appropriate?

options.text: The loop may remove one or more valid candidate windows before their lengths are recorded, and the final state may already be invalid.
The minimum should always be computed from the first invalid window.
Shortest-window algorithms must keep left fixed.
The only issue is that Math.max should replace Math.min.

items.prompt: A problem asks only whether any non-empty valid window exists. What may the algorithm do after proving that the current non-empty window is valid?

items.prompt: A window must contain at most k distinct values. When should left advance?

options.text: While the current distinct count is greater than k, removing outgoing values until the window is valid again.
Whenever the distinct count equals k, because equality is invalid.

items.prompt:

options.text: Every non-empty suffix ending at right and starting between left and right also contains at most k distinct values.

items.prompt:

options.text: It counts only one window ending at right, although every start from left through right forms another valid suffix.

Nothing; at most one valid window can end at each right position.

items.prompt: Which identity correctly counts subarrays containing exactly k distinct values?

options.text: countAtMost(k) - countAtMost(k - 1)
countAtMost(k) + countAtMost(k - 1)
longestAtMost(k) - longestAtMost(k - 1)
countAtMost(k), because every at-most-k window has exactly k distinct values.

items.prompt:

  longestAtMostK - longestAtMostKMinusOne;

items.prompt:
A window is shrunk until it contains at most k distinct values.

to count windows with exactly k distinct values.

options.text: Some shorter suffixes beginning after left may contain fewer than k distinct values even though the full window contains exactly k.

right - left + 1 always equals one after normalization.
Exactly-k counting works only on sorted arrays.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A width-based count is valid only when every represented start position satisfies the same requested contract.

items.prompt: A problem asks whether any non-empty window contains exactly k distinct values. Which condition proves success?

distinctCount <= k, because every at-most-k window is exactly k.
The window length equals k, regardless of duplicate values.

items.prompt:

For every normalized window it executes:

items.prompt: Which statement most completely explains correctness across longest, shortest, existence, count, at-most, and exactly-k window problems?

options.text: The boundaries and state must describe the current window exactly; shrinking must restore or exploit the required validity relation; and each valid state must be recorded using the contract-specific rule—maximum, minimum, early success, or a proved number of represented windows.
Once pointer movement is correct, every problem may use the same answer update.
The current window length is always the required result.
Exactly-k and at-most-k contracts are interchangeable whenever the same frequency map is used.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Sliding-window families often share mechanics while differing critically in validity, contraction, and answer aggregation.
feedbackModel.nextAction: For each problem, define the candidate, validity relation, contraction rule, and exact meaning of one answer update.

items.prompt: What should a standard monotonic deque store while computing fixed-window maxima or minima?

options.text: Indexes of elements that are still eligible to become the extreme value of a current or future window.
Every value currently inside the window, in its original order.
Only the current maximum or minimum value, with no positional information.

items.prompt: Which value ordering should indexes in a deque maintain for fixed-window maximum queries?

options.text: Their referenced values should be nonincreasing from front to back, so the front references the maximum.
Their referenced values should be nondecreasing from front to back, so the back references the maximum.

items.prompt: How does the monotonic ordering change when computing fixed-window minima instead of maxima?

The deque remains nonincreasing, and the front still gives the minimum.
Indexes must be stored in decreasing arrival order.
Every index should be negated before insertion.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Maximum and minimum deques use symmetric dominance rules with opposite value ordering.

items.prompt:
For a complete window ending at right with size k, the window starts at:

options.text: Indexes strictly smaller than windowStart.
Indexes equal to windowStart, because the left endpoint is excluded.
Indexes larger than right.

items.prompt:

When an index leaves the window, the implementation removes an entry from the back.

options.text: Stale entries are the oldest indexes and therefore appear at the front, not the back.
Nothing; the newest candidate is always the first to expire.
Every eviction must remove one entry from both ends.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The front handles expiration; the back handles value dominance.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Associate each deque end with one responsibility: age at the front and dominance at the back.

items.prompt:

options.text: Remove indexes from the back while values[deque.back] <= values[right].
Remove indexes from the front while values[deque.front] > values[right].

options.text: Several trailing candidates may be smaller than the incoming value, so all of them must be removed to restore monotonic order.
Dominance cleanup should never remove existing candidates.
The repair is to pop exactly one entry from the front.
The incoming index must be discarded whenever any smaller value exists.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Monotonicity is a global deque property and may require repeated back removal.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Replace the single conditional with a bounds-safe while loop.

items.prompt:
Compare two deque representations:

A. Store candidate indexes.
B. Store only candidate values.

Which advantage does A provide directly?

options.text: It exposes both candidate age for stale eviction and the original index of the current extreme, while the value remains available through the input array.
Indexes make value comparisons unnecessary.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Indexes preserve identity and age without sacrificing access to values.
feedbackModel.nextAction: List every operation that requires an element's value, index, or age.

items.prompt:
A value-only maximum deque removes equal duplicates and stores each candidate value at most once.

Why can stale eviction become incorrect?

options.text: Equal values from different positions leave the window at different times, but the representation no longer records which occurrence remains.
There is no issue because equal numeric values always leave the window simultaneously.
Equal values are ineligible to be window maxima.
The physical window size can no longer be calculated.

feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Collapsing equal values without indexes or multiplicity information loses candidate identity.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Store indexes, or explicitly preserve enough occurrence information to distinguish expiration times.

items.prompt:
A fixed-window maximum implementation emits values[deque.front] after processing every right index, starting at right = 0.

What is wrong?

options.text: Outputs produced before right reaches k - 1 describe prefixes smaller than the required window size.
The deque front cannot represent a maximum before the final input index.

items.prompt:
An algorithm finds the optimal window using O(1) auxiliary state.

Implementation A returns [bestLeft, bestRight].
Implementation B returns values.slice(bestLeft, bestRight + 1), whose length is L.

Which space statement is precise?

options.text: A has O(1) result space, while B requires O(L) output space even though the search itself still uses O(1) auxiliary space.
Both require O(1) total space because the search uses only scalar variables.
Both require O(n) auxiliary space because the input contains n values.
B uses O(L) auxiliary space but no output space.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Materializing the selected values allocates storage proportional to the returned window length.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Auxiliary working memory and memory required by the requested output should be reported separately.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Classify every allocation as input, auxiliary state, temporary storage, or returned output.

items.prompt:
A TypeScript function sums windows where:

n <= 100_000
|values[i]| <= 1_000_000_000

The absolute sum can therefore reach about 10^14.

Which accumulator choice is appropriate?

options.text: A JavaScript Number is exact for these integer bounds, but a signed 32-bit accumulator would be unsafe.
A signed 32-bit integer is sufficient because each individual value fits in 32 bits.
A boolean is sufficient because only window validity matters.
A JavaScript Number is exact for integer sums of any magnitude.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The accumulator must hold the aggregate bound, not merely one input element.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Numeric-width analysis multiplies the largest contribution by the maximum number of contributions that may coexist.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Compute the largest possible absolute aggregate and compare it with the accumulator's exact range.

items.prompt:
A TypeScript algorithm uses integer inputs whose possible window sum can exceed Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER.

It compares the rolling sum with an exact integer target to decide when to shrink.

What is the risk?

options.text: The Number value may no longer represent every integer exactly, so equality and threshold decisions can be wrong.
JavaScript automatically throws an overflow exception before any inaccurate comparison occurs.
Loss of integer precision can affect formatting but never algorithmic comparisons.
Using two moving boundaries guarantees that the numeric sum stays safe.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The validity invariant depends on exact comparison of an aggregate outside Number's safe-integer range.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A mathematically correct window rule can fail when its numeric representation cannot preserve the required values.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Use BigInt or another exact representation when all operands are integers and the derived bound exceeds the safe-integer range.

items.prompt:
A JavaScript implementation simulates left-boundary movement with:

values.shift();

The contract requires preserving the input and returning original indexes.

What are the main problems?

options.text: shift mutates the caller's array, changes the meaning of original indexes, and typically moves remaining elements in O(n) time.
There is no problem because shift only changes a logical pointer in O(1).
Mutating the array makes original-index output more reliable.
shift affects only auxiliary space and cannot alter correctness or time complexity.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Physical removal is observably different from advancing an index into an unchanged array.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Use a left boundary for logical eviction unless mutation is explicitly allowed and its cost is acceptable.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Keep the input unchanged and represent the active start with an index.

items.prompt: Which review statement most completely evaluates a sliding-window solution beyond its core boundary logic?

options.text: Include all helper-operation costs, bound total stored state, separate auxiliary and output space, prove the accumulator can represent every possible aggregate exactly, and verify that preprocessing or eviction does not violate the input-mutation contract.
The solution is automatically O(n), O(1), numerically safe, and non-mutating whenever it uses left and right pointers.
Passing several examples removes the need to inspect overflow, hidden copies, or mutation.
Once the current boundaries are correct, runtime, storage, numeric representation, and side effects cannot affect correctness.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Production correctness includes resource assumptions, numeric representation, result allocation, and observable side effects.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A valid window invariant is necessary but does not by itself establish the full implementation contract.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Audit time, auxiliary storage, output storage, numeric bounds, and mutation independently after proving the core algorithm.

options.text: Reject binary search on answer because feasibility is not monotonic.

items.prompt: Choose the correction.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: For a candidate largest subarray sum X, the check greedily counts how many parts are needed if no part may exceed X. What makes this predicate monotonic?

items.prompt: Choose the monotonicity explanation.

items.instruction: For a candidate largest subarray sum X, the check greedily counts how many parts are needed if no part may exceed X. What makes this predicate monotonic?
items.answerFeedback: Larger X makes the constraint looser, so feasibility cannot flip from true back to false.

options.text: As X increases, the limit becomes looser, so a feasible X stays feasible for larger X.
The check is monotonic because greedy algorithms are always monotonic.
The check is monotonic only if the input array is sorted.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A task asks for the maximum minimum distance between placed items. A check says whether distance mid is achievable. If achievable, what should happen?

options.text: Keep mid as feasible and try larger distances.
Move to smaller distances because feasible means the answer is too high.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Plan A tries every possible answer from low to high and runs a check each time. Plan B binary-searches the answer range and runs the same check. Which comparison matters?

items.prompt: Why is the standard left/right two-pointer pair-sum procedure not generally correct on an unsorted array?
items.title: Hash map versus sorting: complement 008
items.answerFeedback: After sorting, increasing the left pointer cannot decrease its value and decreasing the right pointer cannot increase its value. That ordering makes directional elimination valid. An unsorted array provides no such guarantee.

options.text: Without sorted values, the current sum does not tell us which pointer movement can safely discard candidates.
Two-pointer algorithms are valid only when a hash table has already been built.
Two pointers cannot process arrays containing duplicate values.
Two pointers can never return array indexes.

[2, 3], because 6 + 3 also equals 9.
[0, 3], because the first and last values are checked before adjacent values.

items.prompt: A reviewer claims: "Frequency signatures and sorted canonical keys always have identical runtime and space because both eventually become Map keys." Which response is accurate?
items.title: Hash map versus sorting: grouping 012
items.answerFeedback: A sorted key commonly costs O(m log m) time and O(m) key or copy space. A frequency signature may cost O(m + σ) time and O(σ) key space, but those bounds depend on whether the domain is fixed, large, or sparse.

options.text: Their costs depend on input length, domain size, key encoding, copying, and retained key size; sorting and counting do not have universally identical behavior.
The claim is correct because all Map keys have constant size internally.
Frequency signatures always use O(1) time and space, regardless of the value domain.
Sorted keys always use O(1) space because sorting can always mutate the original collection.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Canonical contents are stored in newly allocated object keys.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Canonicalization of contents does not change the equality semantics of the key type used by the Map.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Confirm whether the map compares keys structurally or by object identity.

distractorExplanations.sort_is_random: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Numeric sorting produces a random order for equal values.
distractorExplanations.map_ignores_arrays: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Map cannot use arrays as keys under any circumstances.
distractorExplanations.duplicates_change_reference: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Only collections containing duplicates receive different references.

items.title: Hash map versus sorting: grouping 013
items.answerFeedback: The arrays have canonical contents but remain separate object references. A primitive value-based encoding, such as an unambiguous string, is needed unless the environment provides structural key equality.

options.text: Map compares array objects by reference identity, so two separately created arrays are different keys even when their contents are equal.
Numeric sorting produces a random order for equal values.
Map cannot use arrays as keys under any circumstances.
Only collections containing duplicates receive different references.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Canonicalization discards original order, but downstream behavior still requires it.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The key and the stored group serve different purposes: identity of the equivalence class versus preservation of its members.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Store canonical data in the key and presentation or source data in the group value.

distractorExplanations.canonical_key_only: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Only the sorted key, because it can always reconstruct every original ordering.
distractorExplanations.group_count_only: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Only the group size, because original values are irrelevant after hashing.
distractorExplanations.last_item_only: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Only the most recently encountered collection for that key.

items.prompt: A sorted canonical key is used only to identify an equivalence class, but later code must display every collection in its original order. What should the Map store as its value?
items.title: Hash map versus sorting: grouping 014
items.answerFeedback: The canonical key determines group membership, while the Map value retains the original objects or collections. This preserves information intentionally removed from the key.

options.text: A bucket containing the original collections associated with that canonical key.
Only the sorted key, because it can always reconstruct every original ordering.
Only the group size, because original values are irrelevant after hashing.
Only the most recently encountered collection for that key.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: All inputs share the same distinct-value Set, but one has different occurrence counts.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Sorted keys and correct frequency signatures both preserve multiplicity; plain membership signatures do not.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Use duplicate-heavy cases to verify that the key represents a multiset rather than a Set.

distractorExplanations.all_together: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: All three belong together because they contain the same distinct values.
distractorExplanations.all_separate: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: All three are separate because their original orders differ.
distractorExplanations.a_c_together: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: A and C belong together because they have the same length and endpoints.

items.prompt:

items.title: Hash map versus sorting: grouping 015
items.answerFeedback: A and B both contain two 1s, one 2, and one 3. C contains one 1 and two 2s, so its multiplicity signature differs.

options.text: A and B belong together; C belongs in a different group.
All three belong together because they contain the same distinct values.
All three are separate because their original orders differ.
A and C belong together because they have the same length and endpoints.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Multiple correct representations exist, but their costs and information requirements differ.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Canonicalization strategy is chosen from the equivalence relation and data model, not from a universal rule that hashing or sorting is always superior.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Verify correctness and encoding safety first, then compare construction time, retained key size, and implementation constraints.

distractorExplanations.frequency_always_best: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Always use a frequency signature because hashing is necessarily O(1) for every possible key domain.
distractorExplanations.sorting_always_best: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Always sort because a sorted representation preserves original order and requires no auxiliary memory.
distractorExplanations.map_makes_choice_irrelevant: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: The representation does not matter because the outer Map automatically canonicalizes all keys.

items.prompt: Which statement gives the most accurate basis for choosing between a frequency signature and a sorted canonical key?
items.title: Hash map versus sorting: grouping 016
items.answerFeedback: Both representations can be correct, but under different assumptions. Frequency signatures are attractive for bounded or efficiently countable domains; sorted keys are more general but usually require comparison sorting, copying, and sequence encoding.

options.text: Choose based on the equivalence contract, value domain, multiplicity requirements, comparator cost, key size, mutation constraints, and whether the encoding is deterministic and unambiguous.
Always use a frequency signature because hashing is necessarily O(1) for every possible key domain.
Always sort because a sorted representation preserves original order and requires no auxiliary memory.
The representation does not matter because the outer Map automatically canonicalizes all keys.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Correctness depends on which values are adjacent after ordering by value.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Sorting is justified by the structure produced by order, not merely because the values are sortable.
feedbackModel.nextAction: State which adjacency, run, or rank relationship becomes visible after sorting.

distractorExplanations.exact_key_lookup: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: The algorithm knows an exact key and needs only to determine whether that key has appeared.
distractorExplanations.seen_before: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: The algorithm must answer whether the current value was encountered earlier in the original scan.

items.prompt: Which requirement is the strongest signal that sorting may provide useful structure rather than merely an alternative implementation?

items.answerFeedback: Sorting creates global order. That order makes equal values adjacent and exposes relationships between neighboring values or ranks. Hash-based state supports keyed access but does not directly create those relationships.

options.text: The algorithm must reason about equal values, neighboring ranks, or gaps between consecutive values in global value order.
The algorithm knows an exact key and needs only to determine whether that key has appeared.
The algorithm must answer whether the current value was encountered earlier in the original scan.
The algorithm needs only the count associated with one known value.

items.answerFeedback: Sorting simultaneously creates ascending value order and groups duplicates. A run scan can then emit one representative per distinct value.

options.text: Sort the values, then emit the first value of each maximal equal-value run.

items.prompt:

items.answerFeedback: A seen Set can be updated incrementally and can identify a repeated value at its arrival time. Sorting is naturally batch-oriented because it requires a collection of available values and usually delays the result.

items.prompt: Which decision rule best captures the operational contrast between hash-based state and sorting?

items.answerFeedback: The choice depends on data availability, response timing, query types, representation reuse, and update frequency. Hashing is naturally incremental; sorting is often attractive for stable batches and reusable ordered access.

options.text: Use online hash state when answers and updates must be incremental; consider sorted preprocessing when a stable batch can serve many order-dependent queries, while accounting for rebuild costs after updates.
