items.prompt: A solution scans n elements once. For each element, it performs one expected O(1) hash lookup and at most one expected O(1) insertion. In the worst case, the map stores one entry for every element. What is the appropriate complexity description under standard hash-table assumptions?
items.title: Hash map versus sorting: complexity 001
items.complexityExplanation: There are n expected constant-time hash operations, so the total expected time is O(n). The keyed state may contain n distinct entries, giving O(n) auxiliary space.
items.answerFeedback: There are n expected constant-time hash operations, so the total expected time is O(n). The keyed state may contain n distinct entries, giving O(n) auxiliary space.

options.text: Expected O(n) time and O(n) auxiliary space.
Guaranteed O(n) time and O(1) auxiliary space.
Expected O(n log n) time and O(n) auxiliary space.
Expected O(1) time and O(n) auxiliary space.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The second loop begins only after the first loop ends rather than running once for every first-loop iteration.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Multiply costs for nested or repeated-per-item work. Add costs for sequential phases.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Write the phases as a sum before simplifying the asymptotic expression.

distractorExplanations.hash_pass_constant: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Building a hash map is O(1), so only the second pass matters.
distractorExplanations.two_passes_logarithmic: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Any algorithm with exactly two passes is O(log n).
distractorExplanations.frequency_map_sort: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: A frequency map automatically sorts its keys, reducing the total to O(log n).

items.prompt: A reviewer says that an algorithm is O(n²) because it first makes one O(n) pass to build a frequency map and then makes a separate O(n) pass to inspect the input. What is wrong with that reasoning?
items.title: Hash map versus sorting: complexity 002
items.answerFeedback: Sequential phases are added, not multiplied. A constant number of O(n) passes remains O(n), assuming expected constant-time hash operations and constant-cost keys.

options.text: The passes are sequential, so their costs add to O(n + n) = O(n). They are not nested.
Building a hash map is O(1), so only the second pass matters.
Any algorithm with exactly two passes is O(log n).
A frequency map automatically sorts its keys, reducing the total to O(log n).

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The two-pointer phase depends on an earlier comparison sort.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Analyze every required phase. A linear final scan does not make the entire algorithm linear when preprocessing is more expensive.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Add sorting and scanning costs before selecting the dominant asymptotic term.

distractorExplanations.n: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: O(n), because each pointer moves at most n times.
distractorExplanations.n_squared: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: O(n²), because sorting and scanning are two separate phases.
distractorExplanations.logn: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: O(log n), because the values become ordered.

items.prompt: A solution sorts n values using a comparison sort and then performs one O(n) two-pointer scan. What is the total time complexity when comparisons are O(1)?
items.title: Hash map versus sorting: complexity 003
items.complexityExplanation: The total is O(n log n + n), which simplifies to O(n log n). The linear scan does not remove the cost of establishing sorted order.
items.answerFeedback: The total is O(n log n + n), which simplifies to O(n log n). The linear scan does not remove the cost of establishing sorted order.

options.text: O(n log n), because O(n log n) sorting dominates the following O(n) scan.
O(n), because each pointer moves at most n times.
O(n²), because sorting and scanning are two separate phases.
O(log n), because the values become ordered.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The claim uses absolute language about hash operations without stating assumptions.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Expected lookup and amortized insertion describe different assumptions. Without stronger guarantees, neither proves a worst-case linear pass.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Use the exact output and implementation contract before committing to this strategy.

distractorExplanations.lookup_always_logn: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Every hash lookup is guaranteed O(log n), so the algorithm is always O(n log n).
distractorExplanations.map_lookup_linear_normally: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Hash lookup is normally O(n), so the entire algorithm is normally O(n²).
distractorExplanations.worst_case_irrelevant: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Expected and worst-case complexity mean the same thing for hash tables.

items.prompt: A review says Map lookup is always O(1), so a whole pass is guaranteed worst-case O(n). Which correction is precise?
items.title: Hash map versus sorting: complexity 004
items.answerFeedback: Expected lookup and amortized insertion describe different assumptions. Without stronger guarantees, neither proves a worst-case linear pass.

options.text: Lookup is commonly expected or average O(1); insertion can be expected and amortized O(1) because resize work is spread across insertions. Neither guarantees every operation is worst-case O(1).
Every hash lookup is guaranteed O(log n), so the algorithm is always O(n log n).
Hash lookup is normally O(n), so the entire algorithm is normally O(n²).
Expected and worst-case complexity mean the same thing for hash tables.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The algorithm retains keyed state whose size grows with the number of distinct input values.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Measure peak live auxiliary memory, not merely the size of the returned value.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Count the maximum number of entries simultaneously stored in every auxiliary structure.

distractorExplanations.result_determines_space: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: The auxiliary space is O(1) because only the return value remains after the function finishes.
distractorExplanations.set_is_input_space: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Set storage is not counted because it contains values copied from the input.
distractorExplanations.set_is_logarithmic: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: The auxiliary space is O(log n) because hash tables organize values into buckets.

items.prompt: A function returns only a boolean, but during its scan it may store every distinct input value in a Set. A reviewer calls its auxiliary space O(1) because the returned result has constant size. What is the correct diagnosis?
items.title: Hash map versus sorting: complexity 005
items.answerFeedback: Space complexity counts working data retained while the algorithm runs. A constant-sized result does not cancel an O(n)-sized Set or Map.

options.text: The auxiliary space is O(n) in the worst case because the Set may retain n distinct keys.
The auxiliary space is O(1) because only the return value remains after the function finishes.
Set storage is not counted because it contains values copied from the input.
The auxiliary space is O(log n) because hash tables organize values into buckets.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Input immutability is achieved by materializing another array proportional to the input size.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: An in-place sort of the copy is not an O(1)-space solution relative to the original input because the copy itself is auxiliary storage.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Separate the memory used to copy or decorate the input from the sorting algorithm's internal workspace.

distractorExplanations.copy_constant: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: The copy requires O(1) auxiliary space because it contains primitive values.
distractorExplanations.sort_erases_copy: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: The sort immediately reuses nums, so the copied array does not count.
distractorExplanations.scan_log_space: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: The pointer scan requires O(log n) auxiliary space.

items.prompt:
A solution must not mutate nums, so it begins with:

const sorted = [...nums].sort((a, b) => a - b);

It then scans sorted with two pointers.

Which auxiliary-space statement is necessarily true before considering the sort implementation's own workspace?
items.title: Hash map versus sorting: complexity 006
items.complexityExplanation: Creating a separate n-element array costs O(n) auxiliary space regardless of how much additional workspace the sorting implementation uses.
items.answerFeedback: Creating a separate n-element array costs O(n) auxiliary space regardless of how much additional workspace the sorting implementation uses.

options.text: The copied array already requires O(n) auxiliary space.
The copy requires O(1) auxiliary space because it contains primitive values.
The sort immediately reuses nums, so the copied array does not count.
The pointer scan requires O(log n) auxiliary space.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The complexity claim depends on unspecified implementation details.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Do not infer internal memory behavior solely from an API that mutates the supplied array.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Name the sorting algorithm or runtime guarantee before asserting a precise auxiliary-space bound.

distractorExplanations.sorting_always_linear_space: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Every possible sorting algorithm necessarily allocates a second O(n) array.
distractorExplanations.sorting_never_uses_stack: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Sorting space depends only on the comparator, never on the sorting algorithm.
distractorExplanations.input_counts_as_auxiliary: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: The input array itself always counts as O(n) auxiliary space.

items.prompt: A reviewer writes: "Sorting uses O(1) auxiliary space." No sorting algorithm or runtime implementation is identified. Why is this claim incomplete?
items.title: Hash map versus sorting: complexity 007
items.answerFeedback: An in-place interface does not prove O(1) auxiliary memory. Implementations may use O(log n) stack space, O(n) workspace buffers, or other workspace. The assumption must be stated and supported.

options.text: Auxiliary space depends on the sorting implementation; some algorithms sort in place but use recursion or workspace buffers, and built-in sort behavior must not be assumed without evidence.
Every possible sorting algorithm necessarily allocates a second O(n) array.
Sorting space depends only on the comparator, never on the sorting algorithm.
The input array itself always counts as O(n) auxiliary space.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The operation described as one lookup includes preprocessing proportional to the key size.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: O(1) hash-table access assumes a ready, constant-cost key. Include construction, normalization, serialization, equality, and hashing costs when they scale with the input.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Identify all independent size parameters and account for the work needed before the table operation begins.

distractorExplanations.n: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Expected O(n), because map operations are expected O(1).
distractorExplanations.k: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: O(k), because every generated key has the same number of fields.
distractorExplanations.nlogn: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: O(n log n), because string keys cause the map to sort its entries.

items.prompt: An algorithm processes n records. For every record, it serializes k fields into a new string key in O(k) time and then performs an expected O(1) map operation on that key. Assuming k is not a constant, what time bound should be reported for the pass?
items.title: Hash map versus sorting: complexity 008
items.complexityExplanation: Expected constant-time table access does not make key creation free. Constructing n keys at O(k) each contributes O(nk) work. Hashing long keys may add similar representation-dependent cost.
items.answerFeedback: Expected constant-time table access does not make key creation free. Constructing n keys at O(k) each contributes O(nk) work. Hashing long keys may add similar representation-dependent cost.

options.text: Expected O(nk), because key construction is performed for every record.
Expected O(n), because map operations are expected O(1).
O(k), because every generated key has the same number of fields.
O(n log n), because string keys cause the map to sort its entries.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The comparator performs non-constant work each time the sorting algorithm invokes it.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The standard O(n log n) comparison-sort bound assumes O(1)-cost comparisons.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Multiply the comparison count by the cost of one comparator call, or analyze a key-precomputation alternative.

distractorExplanations.nlogn: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: O(n log n), because comparator work is never included in sorting complexity.
distractorExplanations.nk: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: O(nk), because every element appears in only one comparison.
distractorExplanations.klogn: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: O(k log n), because n elements are processed in parallel.

items.prompt: A comparison sort performs O(n log n) comparisons. Each comparator call derives and compares data in O(k) time. What is the resulting sorting time if the derived data is not precomputed?
items.title: Hash map versus sorting: complexity 009
items.complexityExplanation: The number of comparisons is O(n log n), and each comparison costs O(k), producing O(k n log n). Precomputing comparison keys may change the tradeoff to O(nk + n log n) time with additional storage.
items.answerFeedback: The number of comparisons is O(n log n), and each comparison costs O(k), producing O(k n log n). Precomputing comparison keys may change the tradeoff to O(nk + n log n) time with additional storage.

options.text: O(k n log n).
O(n log n), because comparator work is never included in sorting complexity.
O(nk), because every element appears in only one comparison.
O(k log n), because n elements are processed in parallel.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The ordered representation survives across queries, but the pair search is repeated for each target.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Reusable preprocessing should be charged once, while per-query work must still be multiplied by the number of queries.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Separate one-time preprocessing from repeated query work before simplifying the total cost.

distractorExplanations.sort_every_query: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: O(qn log n), because the sorting cost must be charged again for every query even when the sorted copy is reused.
distractorExplanations.one_sort_only: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: O(n log n), because querying a sorted array is automatically O(1).
distractorExplanations.qlogn: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: O(q log n), because two pointers halve the search range on every step.

items.prompt: The same n-element array is queried for q different target sums. A sorted copy is built once and reused. Each query performs a fresh O(n) two-pointer scan. What is the total time complexity?

items.complexityExplanation: The O(n log n) preprocessing cost is paid once. Each target still requires an O(n) two-pointer traversal, giving O(n log n + qn) overall.
items.answerFeedback: The O(n log n) preprocessing cost is paid once. Each target still requires an O(n) two-pointer traversal, giving O(n log n + qn) overall.

options.text: O(n log n + qn).
O(qn log n), because the sorting cost must be charged again for every query even when the sorted copy is reused.
O(n log n), because querying a sorted array is automatically O(1).
O(q log n), because two pointers halve the search range on every step.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: One strategy has a better asymptotic bound but fails a required correctness condition.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: First establish correctness, representation sufficiency, and contract compliance. Optimize only among valid alternatives.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Reject or repair incorrect candidates before comparing their performance characteristics.

distractorExplanations.a_better_asymptotic: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Solution A should be accepted because expected O(n) is asymptotically faster than O(n log n).
distractorExplanations.both_equivalent: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Both are equally correct because membership proves that some indexes must exist.
distractorExplanations.b_invalid_due_sort: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: Solution B is invalid because any use of sorting loses original indexes.

items.prompt:
The task requires returning original indexes.

Solution A uses a Map in expected O(n) time but stores only boolean membership, so it cannot reconstruct the indexes.

Solution B sorts { value, originalIndex } records and returns a correct index pair in O(n log n) time.

Which review conclusion is valid?
items.title: Hash map versus sorting: complexity 011
items.answerFeedback: Complexity comparisons apply only among solutions that meet the contract. A faster algorithm that cannot construct the required result is not a valid implementation.

options.text: Solution B is the only currently valid solution. Solution A's better expected complexity is irrelevant until its state is changed to satisfy the output contract.
Solution A should be accepted because expected O(n) is asymptotically faster than O(n log n).
Both are equally correct because membership proves that some indexes must exist.
Solution B is invalid because any use of sorting loses original indexes.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The proposed decision uses one asymptotic number while ignoring the assumptions and constraints behind both alternatives.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Big-O is a decision input, not an automatic ranking system detached from correctness, resources, and workload shape.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Compare valid strategies across time model, peak memory, guarantees, preprocessing lifecycle, and output semantics.

distractorExplanations.hash_always_best: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: The choice is always correct because a lower asymptotic expression overrides every other requirement.
distractorExplanations.sorting_always_best: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: The choice is always wrong because sorting has deterministic ordering and therefore always runs faster.
distractorExplanations.big_o_irrelevant: This alternative misses a stated part of the contract: The two strategies should be chosen randomly because asymptotic complexity has no practical meaning.

items.prompt: A developer chooses hashing solely because "O(n) is smaller than O(n log n)." Which review response is most complete?
items.title: Hash map versus sorting: complexity 012
items.answerFeedback: Expected O(n) hashing can be the best option, but the asymptotic time expression is not the entire decision. Memory limits, guarantees, key behavior, repeated use, data representation, mutation rules, and correctness can justify another strategy.

options.text: Expected time matters, but the decision must also consider correctness, O(n) keyed memory, worst-case requirements, key cost, mutation constraints, preprocessing reuse, and actual implementation guarantees.
The choice is always correct because a lower asymptotic expression overrides every other requirement.
The choice is always wrong because sorting has deterministic ordering and therefore always runs faster.
The two strategies should be chosen randomly because asymptotic complexity has no practical meaning.

items.prompt:
A loop contains variables named left and right.

What additional information is most important for deciding whether it is a sliding-window algorithm?

options.text: Whether left and right bound one current contiguous range whose aggregate or validity state is maintained as the range changes.
Nothing else; variables named left and right always indicate sliding window.
Whether both variables store numeric indexes rather than references.
Whether both variables are updated inside the same loop body.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Pattern classification depends on what the pointers represent and what state the algorithm maintains.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Names such as left and right are implementation details. Sliding window requires one meaningful contiguous range, not merely two indexes.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Describe the semantic role of each pointer before assigning a pattern name.

items.prompt: A sorted array must be checked for two distinct values whose sum equals a target. Each comparison can eliminate one endpoint value from all remaining candidate pairs. Which strategy signal is present?

options.text: Opposite-direction two pointers evaluating one endpoint pair at a time.
A sliding window maintaining the sum of every value between the endpoints.
Read/write pointers compacting accepted values in place.
A fixed-size window whose width is determined by the target.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The current candidate consists of two values, and sorted order supports eliminating candidate pairs.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The interior values are possible future candidates, not members of one maintained aggregate window.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Ask whether the answer uses two selected positions or every element in the interval.

items.prompt: A task asks for the largest total among all contiguous ranges containing exactly k elements. Which strategy best matches the problem structure?

options.text: A fixed-size sliding window maintaining the aggregate of the current contiguous range.
Opposite-end pointers comparing only the first and last value of each range.
Read/write pointers that overwrite rejected elements.
Two independent candidate indexes selected according to endpoint values.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Each candidate answer is one complete contiguous range, and adjacent candidates overlap heavily.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Because every element in the range contributes to the result, the algorithm should maintain window state rather than evaluate only two endpoints.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Identify whether adjacent candidate ranges can reuse most of the same aggregate state.

items.prompt:
An in-place algorithm scans an array with read. When values[read] should be retained, it writes that value at values[write] and advances write.

How should this pattern be classified?

options.text: A read/write two-pointer technique: one pointer scans input and the other marks the next output position.
A sliding window because the indexes define a range between write and read.
Opposite-end pair elimination because two positions are being compared.
A fixed window whose size equals read - write + 1.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The pointers have different operational roles: scanning and writing.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The indexes do not bound one candidate subarray whose aggregate is maintained. The distance between them is incidental.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Name each pointer's job rather than interpreting the interval between them as a window.

items.prompt: A string must be checked by comparing its first character with its last, its second with its second-last, and so on. Which strategy signal is strongest?

options.text: Opposite-direction two pointers comparing required mirrored pairs.
A variable-size sliding window maintaining all characters between the boundaries.
Read/write pointers building a compacted version of the string.
A fixed-size window because two characters are examined at each step.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The property is defined by relationships between mirrored endpoint positions.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The interior is not summarized as one changing aggregate. Each step verifies one pair of positions.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Determine whether the property concerns endpoint relationships or the contents of a whole contiguous range.

items.prompt: A task asks for the longest contiguous substring containing at most two distinct character types. The current substring must be tracked as its boundaries move. Which strategy fits?

options.text: A variable-size sliding window maintaining state for the current contiguous substring.
Opposite-end pointers comparing only the two boundary characters.
Read/write pointers that overwrite characters outside the allowed set.
Two independent indexes selecting the two character types.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The candidate answer is a contiguous substring whose internal composition determines whether it is valid.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The algorithm needs state describing the entire current range, not merely its endpoint values.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Look for a contiguous-range objective combined with state that changes when elements enter or leave.

items.prompt:
A candidate searches an unsorted array for two values summing to a target. They place one pointer at each end and move one inward according to whether the endpoint sum is too low or too high.

What is the central problem?

options.text: Without an ordering relationship, moving an index inward does not reliably make its value larger or smaller, so the endpoint elimination rule is unsupported.
Any unsorted array requires treating the entire interval as a sliding window.
Two pointers can only be used when the requested answer is a contiguous subarray.
The algorithm is correct because the two endpoint values form the aggregate state of the active window.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The proposed movement assumes a predictable value change when an index moves.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Opposite-end convergence is useful only when a comparison proves that discarded candidates cannot be valid.
feedbackModel.nextAction: State the ordering or monotonic relationship that justifies each endpoint elimination.

items.prompt:
A reviewer describes a sorted pair-sum scan as follows:

"The active sliding window is everything between left and right, and the algorithm checks whether that window reaches the target."

What is the best correction?

options.text: The current candidate is values[left] and values[right]. The interior contains remaining possible partners but is not one summed or validated window.
The description is correct because every pair of indexes defines a sliding window.
The endpoint values are excluded; only the values strictly between them form the candidate.
It is a sliding window even though no property of the complete interval is maintained.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The comparison reads two endpoint values rather than an aggregate of the full interval.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A remaining search region is not automatically an active window. Sliding-window state must describe the represented contiguous range.
feedbackModel.nextAction: List which elements directly contribute to the current comparison.

items.prompt:
An algorithm maintains:

- left and right boundaries,
- a sum representing every element from left through right,
- and a best result based on that contiguous range.

Which classification is most accurate?

options.text: Sliding window, because the boundaries represent one current contiguous range and the algorithm maintains state for that range.
Independent endpoint candidates, because only left and right determine the pattern.
Read/write pointers, because right reads new values while left identifies output positions.
It cannot be classified without knowing the variable names used for the sum.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The stored sum corresponds to all elements inside one contiguous range.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Maintained range state is the defining sliding-window signal; the pointer names are secondary.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Check whether the auxiliary state can be described as the state of exactly the current interval.

items.prompt:
Compare two objectives:

A. Find two values that satisfy a relationship.
B. Find one contiguous range whose combined contents satisfy a relationship.

Which classification is generally the better starting point?

options.text: A suggests candidate-position two pointers or another pair-search method; B suggests a sliding window when the range state can be maintained incrementally.
Both suggest sliding window because every pair of indexes encloses a contiguous range.
Both suggest opposite-end pointers because every range has two endpoints.
The correct classification depends mainly on whether the implementation uses left and right as variable names.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The distinction is between selecting positions and evaluating all contents of one contiguous range.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Endpoints alone do not make a range the semantic candidate. The problem's answer contract determines the useful abstraction.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Rewrite the candidate answer as either selected indexes or an inclusive contiguous interval.

items.prompt: A linked-list algorithm advances one pointer by one node and another by two nodes to detect whether they meet. How should it be classified?

options.text: A fast/slow two-pointer technique; the pointers have different movement rates and do not bound a contiguous window.
A variable sliding window because the distance between the pointers changes.
A fixed sliding window because both pointers move through the same structure.
An opposite-end pair search because the pointers are candidate values.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The pointers represent traversal roles and movement rates, not boundaries of one maintained range.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A changing distance between pointers does not by itself create a sliding window.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Check whether the interval between pointers has any semantic state used by the algorithm.

items.prompt: A task asks for the longest contiguous sequence whose total cost stays within a budget. The total depends on every item currently included. Which high-level signal should guide the strategy choice?

options.text: Maintain one contiguous range and its current total as the boundaries move: a sliding-window signal.
Compare only the two endpoint costs as independent candidates.
Use mirrored comparisons because the answer has two boundaries.
Use one pointer to read costs and another to overwrite rejected costs.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The answer is a contiguous range, and its validity depends on a combined property of all included elements.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: When the interior contributes to validity, endpoint-only comparisons cannot represent the current candidate.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Identify the aggregate that must correspond to the complete candidate range.

items.prompt: Which observation most strongly supports an opposite-end two-pointer strategy for a pair problem?

options.text: The data has an ordering relationship that lets one comparison prove that one endpoint cannot participate in any valid remaining pair.
The implementation can store two variables called left and right.
The answer contains two values, even though no comparison can eliminate other pairs.
Moving either pointer makes the interval smaller, regardless of which candidates are discarded.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A useful pointer movement must eliminate a class of candidate pairs without losing a possible answer.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Convergence alone is not the strategy signal. The key is a valid elimination rule derived from problem structure.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Explain what becomes impossible after each possible comparison outcome.

items.prompt:
A learner states:

"Sliding window and two pointers are identical. Any algorithm that moves two indexes through an input belongs to both categories."

Which correction is most precise?

options.text: Sliding window commonly uses two boundaries, but specifically represents one current contiguous range with maintained state. Other two-pointer techniques may represent a pair, read/write roles, mirrored positions, or different traversal speeds.
The statement is correct because implementation syntax is the only meaningful basis for classifying patterns.
Sliding window never uses two pointers and should be treated as completely unrelated.
They differ only because sliding-window variables must be named left and right.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Several algorithms use two moving positions but assign fundamentally different meanings to them.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Sliding window is defined by the represented contiguous range and its maintained state, not by the raw number of indexes.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Classify an algorithm from its state invariant and pointer roles rather than its surface syntax.

items.prompt: Which statement best describes the relationship between two pointers and sliding window?

options.text: Sliding window is a specialized two-boundary technique in which the boundaries represent one current contiguous range with maintained state.
They are disjoint categories because a sliding window does not use pointers.
They are identical categories because every algorithm with two indexes maintains a window.
They differ only in naming: left and right indicate sliding window, while other names indicate two pointers.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Sliding window has the additional semantic requirement that two boundaries describe one maintained contiguous range.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Two pointers is a broader family. Sliding window is one possible interpretation of two moving boundaries, not a separate implementation shape.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Classify the represented state before deciding whether the narrower sliding-window label applies.

items.prompt:
A learner says:

"Any algorithm with two moving indexes is a sliding-window algorithm."

What is the most precise correction?

options.text: Two indexes may instead represent independent candidates, read/write positions, different traversal speeds, or source/destination positions; only some pairs bound one maintained window.
The statement is wrong only for opposite-direction pointers; every same-direction pair is a window.
Sliding window requires at least three indexes, so no two-index algorithm can qualify.
The statement becomes correct whenever the indexes are named left and right.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The same number of indexes can support several different invariants and algorithmic roles.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Pattern identity comes from what the indexes mean, not from counting how many variables move.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Explain the responsibility of each pointer and the meaning of any region between them.

options.text: left is the next write position, and right is the read position scanning observations.
left and right are boundaries of one valid contiguous window.
left and right are independent candidate elements being tested as a pair.
left reads the source value, and right identifies where the value should be written.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: right inspects each input element, while left advances only when an output element is produced.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Names such as left and right do not force boundary semantics. Operations reveal that these are read/write roles.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Describe which pointer consumes input and which pointer grows the output.

items.prompt: Two pointers move from left to right through the same array. What is required before classifying them as sliding-window boundaries?

options.text: The algorithm must treat the contiguous region between them as one current candidate and maintain state describing that region.
Nothing else; same-direction movement automatically defines a sliding window.
Their distance only needs to change during execution.
The pointer named left must move fewer times than the pointer named right.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Sliding-window boundaries jointly represent a range whose complete contents matter.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Movement direction and pointer distance are insufficient without a range-level invariant.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Identify whether any maintained state corresponds exactly to the elements inside the pointer interval.

items.prompt:
Compare two algorithms:

A. It evaluates only values[left] and values[right] as the current candidate pair.

B. It maintains a sum representing every element from left through right.

Which interpretation is correct?

options.text: A uses independent candidate positions; B uses boundaries of one current sliding window.
Both are sliding windows because both have an interval between left and right.
Both evaluate only two independent candidates because only two pointers are stored.
A is a sliding window, while B is a read/write compaction technique.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The decisive difference is whether the interior contributes to the represented candidate state.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: An interval can be merely a search region in one algorithm and the actual candidate range in another.
feedbackModel.nextAction: List every input position that contributes to the current candidate evaluation.

items.prompt:
An array scan uses two indexes:

- fast advances after every observation,
- slow advances only when the observation satisfies a condition.

Which semantic description is strongest?

options.text: fast scans the input, while slow tracks a more selective form of progress such as retained output or the next processed position.
slow and fast necessarily bound a variable-size sliding window.
slow and fast are independent pair candidates because they may point to different values.
Their distance is the required fixed window size.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The pointers differ by advancement policy and operational responsibility.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Names such as slow and fast describe traversal behavior, but do not imply that the region between them is an active candidate.
feedbackModel.nextAction: State what event causes each pointer to advance and what progress that movement represents.

items.prompt:
A transformation copies selected elements from source[sourceIndex] into destination[destinationIndex].

How should the two indexes be interpreted?

options.text: They identify a source observation and a destination output slot; they do not jointly bound one candidate window.
They are boundaries of a window spanning both arrays.
They are independent values being tested for a pair relationship.
Every position numerically between the two indexes is a discarded region.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Each index belongs to a different side of a data transfer.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Pointer roles may describe data flow rather than boundaries within one shared candidate range.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Name which data structure each pointer indexes and whether any shared interval exists at all.

items.prompt: Which invariant most clearly identifies left and right as sliding-window boundaries?

options.text: The maintained state describes exactly the elements at indexes left through right, and the current candidate is that contiguous range.
left is never greater than right.
Both indexes move only forward.
The distance between the indexes equals the number of rejected observations.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A window invariant binds both the represented range and the state maintained for that range.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Ordering and monotonic movement are common mechanics, but they do not define window semantics.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Name both the current range and the state guaranteed to correspond to it.

items.prompt:
In a read/write transformation, write is the number of accepted elements produced so far.

Which invariant best describes [0, write)?

options.text: It is the completed logical output prefix containing accepted observations in their required order.
It is the current candidate window whose validity may still change.
It contains only rejected values that can no longer be used.
It contains values that the read pointer has not yet observed.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: write represents produced output length rather than a movable range boundary.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A finalized processed prefix is structurally different from an active window that may continue changing.
feedbackModel.nextAction: State whether the region is finalized output, a current candidate, or unprocessed input.

items.prompt:
During in-place filtering, read is ahead of write.

A reviewer calls [write, read) the current active window. What is missing from that claim?

options.text: The reviewer has not shown that the region is one candidate or that any maintained state describes all of it; it may simply be rejected or overwritable space.
The only problem is that a window must use a closed interval rather than a half-open interval.
The names are reversed because the pointer farther right must be called left.
The region becomes a window only when its length is at most two.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A numerical interval exists, but no range-level candidate meaning has been established.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Not every area between two indexes is algorithmically meaningful as a window.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Describe what the region contains and whether the algorithm evaluates or maintains it as one unit.

items.prompt:
An algorithm uses variables named start and cursor. It maintains currentSum as the sum of every element from start through cursor.

How should start and cursor be classified?

options.text: They are sliding-window boundaries because their shared range is the current candidate represented by currentSum.
They cannot be window boundaries because they are not named left and right.
start is a destination position and cursor is a source position.
They are independent pair candidates because two separate indexes are involved.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The maintained sum corresponds to the complete contiguous region between the two indexes.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Semantic invariants remain the same even when pointer variables use different names.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Replace variable names mentally with descriptions of the state they delimit.

items.prompt:
Three algorithms each use two indexes:

A. The indexes identify the two values currently compared as a candidate pair.

B. The indexes bound a contiguous range represented by a maintained count.

C. One index scans input while the other marks the next output position.

Which mapping is correct?

options.text: A uses independent candidates, B uses sliding-window boundaries, and C uses read/write roles.
All three are sliding windows because every pair of indexes encloses a range.
All three use independent candidates because the indexes may contain different values.
A uses a window, B uses independent candidates, and C uses opposite-end pair elimination.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Each algorithm assigns a different invariant and responsibility to its indexes.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Identical surface structures can encode different algorithmic abstractions.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Classify each pointer pair by current candidate meaning, maintained state, and movement responsibility.

items.prompt: Which explanation gives the most useful semantic account of a two-pointer algorithm?

options.text: right scans new observations, left marks the beginning of the current candidate range, and the maintained state corresponds exactly to [left, right].
The algorithm has two variables, and both of them move.
left usually remains numerically smaller than right.
The variables are named left and right, so the algorithm is a sliding window.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A useful explanation identifies responsibilities, represented regions, and correspondence between state and indexes.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Mechanical observations do not explain why the pointers exist or what correctness invariant they maintain.
feedbackModel.nextAction: State what each pointer controls and what every relevant array region currently means.

items.prompt:
A code review says:

"This is not a two-pointer algorithm because it is a sliding window."

What is the best response?

options.text: The labels can overlap: if two moving boundaries represent one maintained contiguous range, the algorithm is both a two-pointer technique and, more specifically, a sliding window.
The review is correct because an algorithm must belong to exactly one pattern category.
Sliding window is unrelated to pointer movement and therefore cannot be a two-pointer technique.
It is two pointers only when the variables are explicitly named pointerOne and pointerTwo.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: One label describes the broad mechanism, while the other describes a more specific represented state.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Algorithmic pattern categories may be hierarchical rather than mutually exclusive.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Use the broadest valid category and then add the more specific semantic classification.

items.prompt: In an opposite-direction two-pointer pair search, what does the current state [left, right] usually represent?

options.text: One candidate pair formed by values[left] and values[right]; the interior is not automatically maintained as one aggregate state.
One active sliding window whose sum or frequency state must include every value from left through right.
Two unrelated scans that can move without affecting which candidate pairs remain possible.
A processed prefix ending at left and a processed suffix beginning at right.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The algorithm evaluates a relationship between the two endpoint values rather than an aggregate over the entire interval.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The interval between the pointers describes the remaining search region, but the current candidate is the endpoint pair.
feedbackModel.nextAction: State explicitly whether the decision uses only values[left] and values[right] or all elements between them.

items.prompt:
A sorted ascending array is searched for two distinct values whose sum equals target.

The current pair satisfies:

values[left] + values[right] < target

Which movement is justified?

options.text: Increment left, because pairing the current left value with any index smaller than right would produce an equal or smaller sum.
Decrement right, because a smaller right value will increase the sum toward target.
Move both pointers inward because every comparison should discard both endpoint values.
Move either pointer arbitrarily because the remaining interval becomes smaller in both cases.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The current left value already fails when paired with the largest remaining right value.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Sorted order proves that keeping left and choosing a smaller right cannot repair a sum that is already too small.
feedbackModel.nextAction: For the endpoint being discarded, compare its current partner with every alternative partner still available.

items.prompt:
A sorted ascending array is searched for two distinct values whose sum equals target.

The current pair satisfies:

values[left] + values[right] > target

Which movement is justified?

options.text: Decrement right, because pairing the current right value with any index larger than left would produce an equal or larger sum.
Increment left, because a larger left value will reduce the sum toward target.
Move both pointers because both endpoint values participated in a failed comparison.
Reset left to zero and decrement right because elimination cannot be performed incrementally.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The current right value already produces too large a sum with the smallest remaining left value.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Sorted order proves that keeping right and choosing a larger left cannot repair a sum that is already too large.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Identify which endpoint cannot participate in any valid remaining pair under the current comparison.

items.prompt:
Trace pair-sum search on:

values = [1, 3, 4, 6, 8, 11]
target = 10

The search starts at 1 and 11. Which pointer movements occur before the pair 4 and 6 is found?

options.text: right inward, left inward, right inward, left inward.
left inward, right inward, left inward, right inward.
Both pointers inward twice.
right inward twice, then left inward twice.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Each movement follows whether the current sum is above or below 10.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The movement sequence is comparison-dependent: 12 is too large, 9 too small, 11 too large, and 9 too small.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Write the current endpoint values and sum before choosing each movement.

items.prompt:
Two implementations search for a pair summing to target.

Solution A applies opposite-end elimination to an ascending sorted array.

Solution B applies the same movement rules directly to an arbitrary unsorted array.

Which review is correct?

options.text: Solution A has a valid elimination argument. Solution B is not generally correct because index movement no longer guarantees that values become larger or smaller.
Both are correct because moving pointers inward eventually compares enough pairs.
Solution B is preferable because sorting destroys the relationship between the values.
Both are equally correct; sorting affects only runtime and never the movement proof.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The elimination rule assumes that moving an index has a predictable effect on its value.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Sorted order is a correctness precondition for this pair-sum movement rule, not merely a performance optimization.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Before using value-based pointer movement, verify the ordering relationship between neighboring indexes.

items.prompt:
A candidate moves both pointers inward after every failed comparison.

Consider:

values = [1, 2, 3, 5, 9]
target = 8

Why is that rule unsafe?

options.text: After 1 + 9 is too large, moving both pointers discards 5 even though the valid pair 3 + 5 remains.
An algorithm is never allowed to update two pointer variables in one iteration.
The algorithm must first compute the sum of every value between the pointers.
The value 9 must remain because the larger endpoint is always more likely to form the target.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The comparison proves that one endpoint is impossible, but it does not necessarily eliminate the other endpoint.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A failed pair comparison usually justifies discarding one side. Moving both can remove an endpoint that still has a valid partner.
feedbackModel.nextAction: For each pointer movement, state which endpoint is being eliminated and prove why none of its remaining pairs can work.

options.text: Both movements push the sum in the wrong direction: decreasing right cannot increase a low sum, and increasing left cannot decrease a high sum.
Nothing is wrong because either inward movement reduces the remaining search range.
Each branch should move both pointers instead of one.
Only the comparison operators are wrong; the pointer movements themselves are always interchangeable.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: In ascending order, left movement raises the lower endpoint and right movement lowers the upper endpoint.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Pointer direction must change the pair sum toward the target while preserving the elimination proof.
feedbackModel.nextAction: State how each possible pointer movement changes the endpoint value before selecting a branch.

items.prompt: Which invariant best supports a correct opposite-end search for a target sum in a sorted array?

options.text: If a valid pair has not already been found or eliminated, at least one valid candidate pair still has both indexes within the inclusive range from left to right.
Every value between left and right currently belongs to one valid sliding window.
The current endpoint pair is always the closest possible pair to the target.
After every failed comparison, both current endpoint values are invalid for all remaining pairs.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Each movement should remove only candidates that the latest comparison has proven impossible.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The interval represents the unexplored candidate-index region, not one aggregate window or a guarantee about the current pair.
feedbackModel.nextAction: After each branch, identify the exact row or column of candidate pairs that has been eliminated.

items.prompt:
A reviewer defends a pointer movement by saying:

"It must be correct because every iteration makes the interval smaller."

What is the best response?

options.text: A shrinking interval helps prove termination, but correctness also requires proving that the discarded endpoint cannot participate in any valid remaining pair.
The reviewer is correct because every terminating two-pointer algorithm is also correct.
The interval should sometimes grow; otherwise valid pairs cannot be discovered.
No correctness proof is needed once the scan is known to be linear.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Progress toward termination and preservation of valid answers are separate proof obligations.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Making the search space smaller is useful only when the removed portion has been proven irrelevant.
feedbackModel.nextAction: For each branch, prove both progress and answer preservation separately.

items.prompt:
A sorted array is searched for the pair whose sum is closest to target.

The current endpoint sum is below target. Why is advancing left the justified movement?

options.text: For the current left value, replacing right with any smaller value would make the sum even lower and therefore cannot improve this below-target candidate.
left should advance after every closest-pair comparison regardless of the current sum.
Both endpoints can be discarded because the current pair is not an exact match.
Advancing left increases the sum of all values in the interval, which is the maintained window state.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The current right endpoint is already the largest partner available to the current left endpoint.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: When even that largest partner leaves the sum too low, smaller partners cannot produce a closer value on the target side.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Compare the current endpoint with all alternative partners it could still use before discarding it.

items.prompt:
Find the pair sum closest to 12:

values = [1, 4, 6, 10]

The algorithm starts with 1 and 10. Which pair remains the best after the complete opposite-end scan?

options.text: 1 and 10, with sum 11 and distance 1 from the target.
4 and 10, with sum 14 and distance 2 from the target.
4 and 6, with sum 10 and distance 2 from the target.
6 and 10, because the two largest values must produce the closest sum.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The first candidate has distance 1; later candidates reached by valid movements have distance 2.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The current candidate must be compared with the best-so-far before its endpoint is eliminated.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Track the current sum, absolute distance, and best distance as separate values.

items.prompt:
A palindrome check compares characters at left and right.

When the two characters are equal, what is the correct next step?

options.text: Increment left and decrement right because that mirrored pair has been verified.
Increment only left because right must remain available for another comparison.
Move the pointer whose character is alphabetically smaller.
Add both characters to an aggregate representing the active interval.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The task pairs each position with exactly one mirrored position.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Unlike pair-sum elimination, a successful symmetry comparison fully resolves both endpoints, so both may move.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Distinguish moving both after a verified mirrored match from moving both after an unresolved pair-sum comparison.

items.prompt:
During a direct palindrome check, characters[left] !== characters[right].

What should the algorithm conclude?

options.text: Return false immediately because this required mirrored pair does not match.
Move the pointer with the alphabetically smaller character and continue searching.
Move both pointers inward because the interior may still be symmetric.
Shrink whichever side currently contains more characters.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A palindrome requires every mirrored pair to match; there is no alternative partner for either endpoint.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: This is symmetry verification, not pair search. A mismatch disproves the complete property rather than triggering candidate elimination.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Ask whether endpoints have alternative partners or one required mirrored partner.

items.prompt: What should happen when an odd-length palindrome check reaches a state where left === right?

options.text: The center character needs no separate partner, so all required mirrored comparisons have succeeded.
The center must be compared with itself repeatedly until both pointers leave the string.
The string must be rejected because one character remains unpaired.
The pointers must return to both ends to verify the center belongs to the interval.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Every non-center position has already been matched with its mirror.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A single middle character is symmetric around itself and does not require another comparison.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Use left < right as the set of states that still contain an unverified mirrored pair.

items.prompt:
Compare these tasks:

Task A: In a sorted array, determine whether two distinct values sum to target.

Task B: Find a contiguous range of fixed length k with the largest sum.

Which distinction is correct?

options.text: Task A evaluates independent endpoint candidates and eliminates pairs using sorted order. Task B maintains one contiguous window whose aggregate includes every element inside it.
Both maintain the entire interval between left and right as one active sum.
Both use only values[left] and values[right]; interior values never affect either answer.
The movement rules are interchangeable because both algorithms use two indexes.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: One answer concerns two selected values, while the other concerns every value in a contiguous range.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The number and names of pointers do not define the pattern. The meaning of the represented state does.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Describe exactly which input elements contribute to the current candidate answer.

items.prompt:
The task asks whether two distinct array elements sum to 8.

Which statement about these inputs is correct?

A: [4]
B: [4, 4]

options.text: A is false because one index cannot be used twice; B is true because the two equal values occur at distinct indexes.
Both are true because 4 + 4 equals 8, regardless of how many elements exist.
Both are false because opposite-end search requires the endpoint values to be different.
A is true because the same endpoint may be reused, while B is false because duplicates are not allowed.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Distinct elements means distinct indexes, not necessarily distinct numeric values.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A pair search requires left < right. Equal values are valid when they come from two separate positions.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Separate the constraint on indexes from the possible equality of their stored values.

items.prompt:
An in-place filtering algorithm uses:

- read to inspect each original element,
- write to mark where the next accepted element should be stored.

What do the two pointers represent?

options.text: Two different roles: read scans observations, while write tracks the next position in the compacted output prefix.
The boundaries of one candidate window whose contents must satisfy a validity condition.
Two independent candidate values that should be compared with each other.
The first and last positions of the unprocessed suffix.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: One pointer observes input while the other identifies the next output slot.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The pointers do not bound one candidate range. Their distance reflects how many values were rejected, not a window contract.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Name the operational responsibility of each pointer before interpreting the range between them.

options.text: Because write counts accepted values and must continue pointing to the next free position in the compacted prefix.
Because write must remain exactly one position behind read.
Because rejected values stay inside an active window until an outgoing element is removed.
It should also advance for rejected values so that both pointers remain synchronized.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: write represents output length, not traversal progress.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A rejected value consumes no output position. Advancing write would leave a gap or preserve rejected data in the logical result.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Tie write movement to creation of one new valid output element.

items.prompt:
During stable compaction, read = 9 and write = 6.

What does the distance between them most directly indicate?

options.text: Three observed positions have not produced retained output positions, assuming both started at zero.
The current candidate window has length four and must be evaluated as one range.
Three elements must now be removed from a maintained rolling aggregate.
The algorithm is invalid because read and write must always remain adjacent.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: read counts observations while write counts accepted output positions.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Their separation records filtering progress. It is not automatically the size of a semantically meaningful range.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Ask whether the algorithm maintains any property of every element between write and read.

items.prompt: Which sequence correctly processes one observation in a stable read/write compaction algorithm?

options.text: Inspect values[read]; if accepted, copy it to values[write] and advance write; then continue by advancing read.
Advance write first; inspect values[read]; then decide whether the newly reserved output slot should remain empty.
Remove the outgoing contribution at write; inspect values[read]; then shrink while invalid.
Copy every observed value to values[write], advance both pointers, and later remove rejected values.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: An output slot should be consumed only after the observation has been accepted.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The decision precedes write advancement. There is no window-validity phase or outgoing contribution.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Structure each iteration as observe, decide, optionally emit.

items.prompt:
A stable in-place filter keeps only positive values:

values = [3, -1, 2, 0, 5]

What should the retained prefix contain after processing all elements?

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Accepted values are emitted in the same order in which read observes them.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Stable compaction removes rejected values without rearranging the retained ones.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Append each accepted observation to a conceptual output list before mapping that list back onto the prefix.

items.prompt:
The contract says: "Remove rejected values in place while preserving the relative order of retained values."

Which implementation strategy satisfies that contract?

options.text: Scan left to right and write each accepted value into the next output position.
Whenever a value is rejected, replace it with the last unprocessed value.
Collect accepted values at the front and sort them afterward.
Prefer writing larger accepted values earlier to reduce later overwrites.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The retained sequence must preserve observation order.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Filling holes from the end may compact the array, but it can reorder retained values and violate stability.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Check whether accepted elements appear in output in the same relative order as in input.

items.prompt:
A sorted array must be compacted so that each distinct value appears once.

Which read/write interpretation is most useful?

options.text: read observes the next sorted value, while write identifies the end or next slot of the unique retained prefix.
read and write bound a variable window that remains valid while every value inside it is duplicated.
read and write are independent candidates whose sum determines whether one is a duplicate.
write marks the beginning of a suffix containing only rejected duplicates.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Sorted order makes duplicates adjacent, and the retained prefix summarizes the distinct values already accepted.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The algorithm compares an observation with retained output state; it does not maintain one candidate window between the pointers.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Describe write in terms of the logical unique output rather than pointer distance.

items.prompt:
A sorted duplicate-collapse scan observes values[read] equal to the most recently retained value.

Which movement is correct?

options.text: Advance read only, because the observation is consumed but does not create a new retained output value.
Advance both pointers so that their distance continues representing the duplicate window size.
Advance write only because the retained prefix must reserve a position for every observation.
Run a shrink loop that removes outgoing values until the current range becomes distinct.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The duplicate has been inspected but should not extend the logical result.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: read advances for consumed input; write advances only when output grows.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Separate consumption of an observation from emission of a retained value.

items.prompt:
A sorted array is compacted to one occurrence per distinct value:

values = [1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 4]

What should the logical retained prefix contain?

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Only the first observation from each adjacent duplicate group extends the unique output prefix.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The resulting prefix contains retained values, not one representative for every pointer movement.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Track the last retained value and emit only when the observation differs from it.

items.prompt:
A learner proposes this change to an in-place filter:

"Whenever read advances, subtract or remove the element at write because it is leaving the range between the pointers."

Why is that reasoning incorrect?

options.text: The algorithm does not maintain an aggregate for the range between write and read; write marks an output position, not an outgoing window boundary.
The reasoning is almost correct, but read rather than write is always the outgoing boundary.
An outgoing contribution should be removed only when the observed value is rejected.
Removal is unnecessary only because read and write must remain a fixed distance apart.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: No maintained state corresponds to all elements inside the pointer interval.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Window updates are meaningful only when the pointers are boundaries of one represented range. Compaction pointers have different semantic roles.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Identify the exact state that an alleged outgoing update would modify before adding such logic.

items.prompt:
During stable filtering, all indexes below write contain accepted values in their original relative order.

What does the half-open range [0, write) represent?

options.text: The completed logical output produced from the observations processed so far.
The current candidate window awaiting a validity check.
The portion of the input known to contain only rejected values.
The observations that read has not yet inspected.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: write is the logical output length after processing the current input prefix.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The prefix before write is finalized retained output, not a movable candidate range.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Partition the array conceptually into retained output, observed-but-not-retained space, and unprocessed input.

items.prompt:
The task is:

"Remove all values that fail a predicate in place, preserve the order of retained values, and return the new logical length."

Which design better matches the contract?

options.text: Use read to inspect every value and write to append each accepted value to the retained prefix.
Treat the region between left and right as a variable window and shrink it repeatedly whenever it contains a rejected value.
Compare values at both ends and discard whichever endpoint fails the predicate.
Maintain a fixed-size range whose length equals the number of accepted values seen so far.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The output is a stable sequence of independently accepted observations, not one valid contiguous subarray of the original input.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A shrink loop solves a range-validity problem. Compaction instead builds a logical output prefix.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Classify whether the task asks for a subarray boundary or a rewritten retained sequence.

items.prompt: A stable in-place filter rejects every element in a non-empty array. Where should write finish if it starts at zero?

options.text: At zero, because no observation created an element in the retained output prefix.
At the array length, because write must advance once for every processed observation.
At the final array index, because one slot must remain reserved for rejected values.
One position behind read, because the pointer distance represents a size-one window.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: write counts retained output elements.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Processing an observation advances read, but rejecting it does not increase logical output length.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Derive the final write value from the number of accepted elements.

items.prompt: A stable in-place filter accepts every element. How should read and write behave?

options.text: Both advance once per observation, so write finishes at the original length and the logical output is unchanged.
write remains at zero because no rejected value creates a gap.
write should begin advancing only after read finds the first rejected value.
The algorithm should shrink the full-array window until only one accepted value remains.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Every observation extends the retained output by one value.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Even when the write is effectively a self-assignment, it still represents growth of the logical retained prefix.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Check that write equals the number of accepted observations in every edge case.

items.prompt: In a fixed-size sliding window with size k, what do left and right represent?

options.text: They are the boundaries of one contiguous range, and every recorded window must satisfy right - left + 1 === k.
They are two independent candidate indexes whose values are compared with each other.
They mark an approximate range whose size may vary whenever the current aggregate changes.
left marks all processed elements, while right marks all elements that remain unprocessed.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The problem asks about every contiguous range containing exactly k elements.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The two indexes are not independent candidates. Together they describe one window with a fixed length.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Write the invariant right - left + 1 === k for every window that contributes a result.

items.prompt:
A loop advances right from left to right and maintains a rolling sum for windows of exactly k elements.

Which sequence correctly processes each complete window?

options.text: Add values[right]; once the window is complete, record its sum; remove values[left]; then increment left.
Record the sum; add values[right]; remove values[left]; then increment left.
Add values[right]; immediately remove values[left]; record the sum; then increment left.
Add values[right]; record the sum; shrink only when the sum exceeds a threshold.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The current complete window must be measured before its outgoing element is removed.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: The incoming contribution completes the new window. Record that window first, then prepare the state for the next one.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Separate processing the current window from preparing the next window.

items.prompt:
A rolling sum processes:

values = [5, 2, 4, 1]
k = 3

What sums should be recorded, in order?

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: There are two complete contiguous windows: [5, 2, 4] and [2, 4, 1].
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Moving to the second window adds 1 but also removes the outgoing 5.
feedbackModel.nextAction: For every shift, explicitly name the incoming and outgoing elements.

options.text: The code advances left without subtracting values[left], so sum continues to contain elements outside the next window.
right must move backward whenever left moves forward.
The code records windows before they contain k elements.
left must be reset to zero after every recorded window.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The boundary moves, but the maintained aggregate is not updated to match the new boundary.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Window state and window indexes must describe the same range. Moving left requires removing the contribution that left leaves behind.

items.prompt:
A task asks for the maximum sum among all contiguous ranges of exactly k elements.

Which movement strategy matches that contract?

options.text: Whenever a complete size-k window is recorded, remove its leftmost contribution and move left forward exactly once.
Shrink repeatedly while the current sum exceeds the best sum seen so far.
Shrink only when the outgoing value is negative.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: Every candidate range must contain exactly k elements, regardless of its values.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: A fixed-size contract determines when left moves. The aggregate does not decide whether the window should shrink.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Derive pointer movement from the required size, not from a validity condition.

options.text: It updates best before checking that the current range contains k elements, so incomplete initial ranges can be accepted.
It must remove values[left] before adding values[right].
A fixed-size window requires a nested loop to verify every element in the current range.
best must start at zero for every fixed-size window problem.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The result is updated for ranges of sizes 1, 2, and so on before the first size-k range exists.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Only a complete window satisfying the exact size contract may contribute to the answer.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Place result recording inside the branch that proves the window size is exactly k.

items.complexityExplanation: The first window is built once, and every subsequent window is derived with one incoming addition and one outgoing subtraction.
items.prompt:
Two implementations compute the sum of every contiguous range of k elements.

Solution A starts a new inner loop for every starting index and sums all k elements again.

Solution B computes the first sum once, then adds the incoming value and removes the outgoing value after each shift.

Which comparison is correct?

options.text: Both can produce the same results, but Solution B is O(n), while Solution A is O(nk) in the general case.
Both are O(n) because both inspect the same set of windows.
Solution A is more correct because a valid window must always be reconstructed from scratch.
Solution B requires O(k) auxiliary space because it must copy every active window.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The outgoing contribution can be reversed, so most work from the previous window can be reused.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: Conceptually containing k values does not require processing all k values again after every one-position shift.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Identify which contribution enters and which leaves when adjacent windows overlap.

items.prompt: For an input containing n elements and k = 1, how should a correct fixed-size window behave?

options.text: Every individual element is a complete window, so exactly n windows are recorded.
No windows are recorded because a sliding window requires at least two elements.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A contiguous range of exactly one element is valid at every array position.

items.prompt: An array has n elements and the required fixed window size is also n. How many complete windows exist?

options.text: Exactly one: the entire array.
n, because right visits every index.
n - 1, because the first index only initializes the window.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: A complete window does not need to have a later neighboring window.
feedbackModel.mentalModelCorrection: When k equals n, the initial completed range is also the only valid range.
feedbackModel.nextAction: Use the complete-window count n - k + 1 for valid values of k.

items.prompt:
A size-3 window maintains the number of odd values rather than their sum.

The first window [1, 2, 5] contains 2 odd values. What count should the next window [2, 5, 4] contain, and how is it obtained?

options.text: 1; remove the contribution of outgoing 1 and add the contribution of incoming 4.
2; the window size remains three, so the count cannot change.
3; add incoming 4 without changing the previous count.
0; all state must be reset whenever the window moves.

feedbackModel.decisionSignal: The maintained state is a sum of per-element contributions: one for odd and zero for even.
